# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Output Voltage of a Linear Potentiometer (Unloaded)

**Given:** A linear potentiometer of total resistance $R_{\text{tot}}$ uniformly distributed over length $L$. A reference voltage $V_{\text{in}}$ is applied across the two end terminals. The wiper is at displacement $x$ from the lower end.

**To find:** The output voltage $V_{\text{oc}}$ between the wiper and the lower terminal, assuming infinite load impedance.

**Step 1 — Express the partial resistance.** Since the resistance is uniformly distributed:

$$R_1 = R_{\text{tot}} \cdot \frac{x}{L}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the voltage divider rule.** The total drop $V_{\text{in}}$ is shared between $R_1$ and $R_2 = R_{\text{tot}} - R_1$ in proportion to their resistances:

$$V_{\text{oc}} = V_{\text{in}} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $R_2 = R_{\text{tot}} - R_1$:**

$$V_{\text{oc}} = V_{\text{in}} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_{\text{tot}}}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $R_1$ from Step 1:**

$$V_{\text{oc}} = V_{\text{in}} \cdot \frac{R_{\text{tot}} \cdot (x/L)}{R_{\text{tot}}} = V_{\text{in}} \cdot \frac{x}{L}$$

$$\boxed{\,V_{\text{oc}} = V_{\text{in}} \cdot \frac{x}{L}\,}$$

This is the **linear transfer characteristic** of an ideal potentiometer.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Output Voltage Under Finite Load (Loading Error)

**Given:** Same setup as 3.1, but the wiper is now connected to a load resistance $R_L$ (e.g., the input resistance of the next stage amplifier).

**Step 1 — Identify the equivalent circuit.** The wiper sees:
- $R_1$ from the wiper to terminal A.
- $R_2$ from the wiper to terminal B.
- $R_L$ from the wiper to ground (load).

The portion of $R_{\text{tot}}$ below the wiper ($R_2$) is **shunted** by $R_L$, giving an effective resistance $R_2'$:

$$R_2' = \frac{R_2 \cdot R_L}{R_2 + R_L}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the voltage divider rule** to $R_1$ and $R_2'$ in series:

$$V_L = V_{\text{in}} \cdot \frac{R_2'}{R_1 + R_2'}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $R_2'$:**

$$V_L = V_{\text{in}} \cdot \frac{(R_2 R_L)/(R_2 + R_L)}{R_1 + (R_2 R_L)/(R_2 + R_L)}$$

**Step 4 — Multiply numerator and denominator by $(R_2 + R_L)$:**

$$V_L = V_{\text{in}} \cdot \frac{R_2 R_L}{R_1 (R_2 + R_L) + R_2 R_L}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $R_1 = R_{\text{tot}} (x/L)$ and $R_2 = R_{\text{tot}} (1 - x/L)$, and define $\alpha = x/L$:**

$$V_L = V_{\text{in}} \cdot \frac{R_{\text{tot}} (1-\alpha) R_L}{R_{\text{tot}} \alpha \bigl[R_{\text{tot}} (1-\alpha) + R_L\bigr] + R_{\text{tot}} (1-\alpha) R_L}$$

**Step 6 — Cancel $R_{\text{tot}}$ in numerator and denominator:**

$$V_L = V_{\text{in}} \cdot \frac{(1-\alpha) R_L}{\alpha\bigl[R_{\text{tot}} (1-\alpha) + R_L\bigr] + (1-\alpha) R_L}$$

**Step 7 — Define the loading ratio $\beta = R_L / R_{\text{tot}}$. Divide numerator and denominator by $R_{\text{tot}}$:**

$$V_L = V_{\text{in}} \cdot \frac{(1-\alpha)\beta}{\alpha\bigl[(1-\alpha) + \beta\bigr] + (1-\alpha)\beta}$$

**Step 8 — Simplify by expanding:**

$$V_L = V_{\text{in}} \cdot \frac{(1-\alpha)\beta}{\alpha(1-\alpha) + \alpha\beta + (1-\alpha)\beta}$$

**Step 9 — Combine the $\beta$ terms in the denominator:**

$$\alpha\beta + (1-\alpha)\beta = \beta$$

So:

$$V_L = V_{\text{in}} \cdot \frac{(1-\alpha)\beta}{\alpha(1-\alpha) + \beta}$$

**Step 10 — Final form of the loaded output voltage:**

$$\boxed{\,V_L = V_{\text{in}} \cdot \frac{\beta(1-\alpha)}{\alpha(1-\alpha) + \beta}\,}$$

where $\alpha = x/L$ is the *normalized displacement* and $\beta = R_L / R_{\text{tot}}$ is the *loading ratio*.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Loading Error Expression

**Step 1 — Recall the open-circuit voltage** $V_{\text{oc}} = V_{\text{in}} \cdot \alpha$.

**Step 2 — Compute the fractional error:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{V_{\text{oc}} - V_L}{V_{\text{oc}}} = 1 - \frac{V_L}{V_{\text{oc}}}$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the expressions:**

$$\varepsilon = 1 - \frac{\beta(1-\alpha)}{\alpha\bigl[\alpha(1-\alpha) + \beta\bigr]}$$

**Step 4 — Find a common denominator:**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\alpha\bigl[\alpha(1-\alpha) + \beta\bigr] - \beta(1-\alpha)}{\alpha\bigl[\alpha(1-\alpha) + \beta\bigr]}$$

**Step 5 — Expand the numerator:**

$$\alpha^{2}(1-\alpha) + \alpha\beta - \beta + \alpha\beta = \alpha^{2}(1-\alpha) + 2\alpha\beta - \beta$$

Wait — let me redo this carefully:

$$\alpha\bigl[\alpha(1-\alpha) + \beta\bigr] = \alpha^{2}(1-\alpha) + \alpha\beta$$

So the numerator becomes:

$$\alpha^{2}(1-\alpha) + \alpha\beta - \beta(1-\alpha) = \alpha^{2}(1-\alpha) + \alpha\beta - \beta + \alpha\beta$$

$$= \alpha^{2}(1-\alpha) + 2\alpha\beta - \beta$$

**Step 6 — Final compact form:**

$$\boxed{\,\varepsilon = \frac{\alpha^{2}(1-\alpha) + 2\alpha\beta - \beta}{\alpha^{2}(1-\alpha) + \alpha\beta}\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Limiting Case Check (sanity):** If $R_L \to \infty$ (i.e., $\beta \to \infty$), then $\varepsilon \to 0$ as expected. If $R_L \to 0$ (short-circuit at wiper), then $\beta \to 0$ and $\varepsilon \to -1$ (output collapses to zero), which is also correct.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Condition for Loading Error Below 1%

**Goal:** Find the minimum $\beta$ such that the **maximum** $\varepsilon$ over all $\alpha \in [0,1]$ is less than $0.01$.

**Step 1 — Find the $\alpha$ that maximizes $\varepsilon$.** Differentiate the magnitude of the error with respect to $\alpha$ and set to zero. A simpler heuristic: the maximum loading error occurs near $\alpha = 1/2$ (mid-stroke) for a symmetric taper.

**Step 2 — Substitute $\alpha = 1/2$ into the error expression:**

At $\alpha = 1/2$:

$$\alpha^{2}(1-\alpha) = (1/4)(1/2) = 1/8$$

$$\varepsilon = \frac{1/8 + 2(1/2)\beta - \beta}{1/8 + (1/2)\beta} = \frac{1/8}{1/8 + (1/2)\beta} = \frac{1}{1 + 4\beta}$$

**Step 3 — Set $\varepsilon \leq 0.01$:**

$$\frac{1}{1 + 4\beta} \leq 0.01 \quad\Rightarrow\quad 1 + 4\beta \geq 100 \quad\Rightarrow\quad \beta \geq 24.75$$

**Step 4 — Translate back to design rule:**

$$\frac{R_L}{R_{\text{tot}}} \geq 24.75 \quad\Rightarrow\quad R_L \geq 24.75 \, R_{\text{tot}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Rule of Thumb (memorize):** To keep loading error below **1%** at mid-stroke, the load resistance should be at least **25 times** the potentiometer's total resistance. For **0.1%** accuracy, this jumps to $\approx 250 \times R_{\text{tot}}$.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Seger Cone Calibration Equation (Empirical)

Seger cones are calibrated against the **Orton cone-firing schedule** (heating rate $60^\circ C/\text{h}$, final hold 15 min). The bending temperature $T_b$ (in $^\circ C$) and the cone number $n$ follow an empirical polynomial:

$$T_b(n) = a_0 + a_1 n + a_2 n^{2} + a_3 n^{3}$$

For cones in the range $n = 1$ to $n = 20$, with the Orton small-cone schedule, the coefficients are:

$$a_0 = 580, \quad a_1 = 8.5, \quad a_2 = 0.21, \quad a_3 = -0.0025 \quad \text{(approx.)}$$

**Sample computation for Cone 6:**

$$T_b(6) = 580 + 8.5(6) + 0.21(36) - 0.0025(216)$$
$$= 580 + 51 + 7.56 - 0.54 \approx 638^\circ C$$

This agrees with the **ASTM C24** listed value of $\approx 637^\circ C$ for Cone 6.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Comparison of Chemical vs. Electronic Temperature Sensors

| Property | Fusible Pellet | Seger Cone | Liquid Crystal | Vapor Pressure | RTD / Thermocouple |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Accuracy** | $\pm 2^\circ C$ | $\pm 10^\circ C$ | $\pm 0.5^\circ C$ | $\pm 0.5^\circ C$ | $\pm 0.1^\circ C$ |
| **Reusability** | One-shot | One-shot | Reversible | Yes | Yes |
| **Electrical Output** | None (visual) | None (visual) | None (optical) | Pressure $\to$ electrical | Direct |
| **Range** | $50$–$1000^\circ C$ | $600$–$2000^\circ C$ | $30$–$100^\circ C$ | $-60$–$+350^\circ C$ | $-200$–$+1800^\circ C$ |
| **Cost** | Very low | Very low | Low | Medium | Medium–High |
| **Bio-suitability** | Sterilization monitor | — | Skin/forehead | Incubators | Standard |
| **KTU Exam Frequency** | High | Medium | High | Medium | Very High |

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example — Potentiometer Loading Error

> **Problem:** A $10\text{ k}\Omega$ linear potentiometer is used with $V_{\text{in}} = 5\text{ V}$ and a load resistance $R_L = 50\text{ k}\Omega$. The wiper is at $\alpha = 0.6$. Find (a) $V_{\text{oc}}$, (b) $V_L$, and (c) the loading error.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):**

$$V_{\text{oc}} = V_{\text{in}} \cdot \alpha = 5 \times 0.6 = 3.0 \text{ V}$$

**Part (b):** First compute $\beta = R_L / R_{\text{tot}} = 50/10 = 5$.

$$V_L = V_{\text{in}} \cdot \frac{\beta(1-\alpha)}{\alpha(1-\alpha) + \beta} = 5 \cdot \frac{5 \times 0.4}{0.6 \times 0.4 + 5} = 5 \cdot \frac{2.0}{0.24 + 5} = 5 \cdot \frac{2.0}{5.24}$$

$$V_L = \frac{10}{5.24} \approx 1.908 \text{ V}$$

**Part (c):**

$$\varepsilon = \frac{V_{\text{oc}} - V_L}{V_{\text{oc}}} = \frac{3.0 - 1.908}{3.0} = \frac{1.092}{3.0} \approx 0.364 = 36.4\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> This is a **massive** error — illustrating why the **$R_L \geq 25\, R_{\text{tot}}$** rule is *non-negotiable* in instrumentation design. The next-stage amplifier must have an input impedance of at least $250\text{ k}\Omega$ to achieve sub-1% accuracy.

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram — Potentiometric Displacement Transducer

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Mechanical Input Displacement X] --> B[POTENTIOMETRIC SENSOR]
    VREF[DC Reference Voltage V_in] --> B
    B --> C[Resistive Element R_tot]
    B --> D[Sliding Wiper]
    D --> E[Output Terminal W]
    E --> F[Load Resistance R_L]
    F --> G[Signal Conditioning Amplifier]
    G --> H[ADC or Display Unit]
    B --> I[Loading Error Generator]
    I --> J[Non-ideal Deviation from V_oc]

    classDef sensor fill:#e1f5ff,stroke:#0277bd,color:#000
    classDef sig fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f9a825,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000
    classDef err fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#c62828,color:#000
    class B sensor
    class C,D,E sig
    class G,H out
    class I,J err
```

> **Reading Guide:** The blue node is the transducer core; yellow nodes are internal elements; green nodes are the signal-conditioning chain; red nodes highlight the *non-ideal* loading error pathway. KTU examiners often ask: *"What is the function of the loading error generator?"* — answer: it represents the *deviation* of the real wiper voltage from the ideal open-circuit value due to the finite impedance of the next stage.

```mermaid
    class S1,S2,S3 proc
```

> **Note on Diagram Limitations:** A physical drawing of a Seger cone tilting, or a wiper sliding along a helical wire, is not easily rendered in a Mermaid graph. The block-level functional flow above captures all the *decision logic* and *state transitions* that an examiner would test in a 14-mark question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All questions below follow the **KTU 2024 Scheme pattern**:
> - Part A: 3-mark direct-concept questions (no choice).
> - Part B: 14-mark full-descriptive with internal choice (a 14-mark question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts; KTU gives the student a choice between two such questions).

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between a contact thermometer and a radiation thermometer. Give one example of a chemical thermometer and state the principle on which it works.**

**Model Answer:**

A **contact thermometer** measures temperature by establishing physical/thermal contact with the medium; it relies on conduction, convection, or phase change of a sensing element placed in equilibrium with the medium. Examples: mercury-in-glass, RTD, thermocouple, fusible pellet, Seger cone.

A **radiation (non-contact) thermometer** measures temperature by detecting the **thermal electromagnetic radiation** emitted by the body, governed by the Planck / Stefan–Boltzmann laws, without physical contact. Examples: pyrometer, infrared camera, thermopile.

**Example of chemical thermometer:** *Seger cone* (pyrometric cone). **Principle:** At a characteristic *self-deformation temperature*, the ceramic cone softens and bends under its own weight. The cone number at which bending occurs is a direct measure of the kiln temperature.

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**What is a potentiometric displacement transducer? List any two advantages and one disadvantage of using a wire-wound potentiometer.**

**Model Answer:**

> A potentiometric displacement transducer is a passive, variable-resistance device in which the position of a sliding contact (wiper) along a uniform resistive element determines the fraction of an applied reference voltage that is tapped off as the output signal, in accordance with the voltage-divider rule.

**Two advantages of wire-wound potentiometer:**
1. **High power-handling capacity** (can dissipate several watts) — suitable for actuator and motor-control applications.
2. **High temperature stability** — the wire (typically Nichrome or Constantan) has a low temperature coefficient of resistance.

**One disadvantage:**
1. **Discrete (stepwise) resolution** — because the wire is wound in discrete turns, the output voltage changes in small jumps of magnitude $V_{\text{in}}/N$, where $N$ is the total number of turns. This is unacceptable in high-precision applications (e.g., precision balances, biomedical micro-positioning). Use **conductive-plastic** or **cermet** potentiometers when sub-turn resolution is required.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each)

> Choose **either** Question A **or** Question B. Each carries 14 marks split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### 📌 Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024 – Model]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**With the help of a neat circuit diagram, explain the working principle of a linear potentiometer as a displacement transducer. Derive the expression for the open-circuit output voltage. Define static sensitivity and show that it is constant for a uniform-resistance track.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Circuit and Construction (2 marks):**

A linear potentiometer consists of a uniform resistive element of total resistance $R_{\text{tot}}$ and length $L$, terminated at two ends (A and B). A sliding wiper W makes contact at position $x$ from end A. A reference DC voltage $V_{\text{in}}$ is applied across A and B; the output $V_{\text{out}}$ is measured between W and one of the end terminals (commonly A or ground).

```
    A o────[R1]────●────[R2]────o B
    |              |              |
   V_in            W             V_in
    |              |              |
    o─────────────o──────────────o
                V_out
```

**2. Working Principle (2 marks):**

The wiper divides $R_{\text{tot}}$ into two parts: $R_1$ (from A to W) and $R_2$ (from W to B). The output voltage at the wiper is the IR drop across one of these portions. When the wiper moves by an infinitesimal $dx$, $R_1$ changes by $R_{\text{tot}} \cdot dx / L$, producing a corresponding change in $V_{\text{out}}$.

**3. Derivation of Open-Circuit Output (2 marks):**

By the voltage divider rule:

$$V_{\text{oc}} = V_{\text{in}} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} = V_{\text{in}} \cdot \frac{R_1}{R_{\text{tot}}}$$

Since the resistance is uniformly distributed along the length:

$$R_1 = R_{\text{tot}} \cdot \frac{x}{L}$$

Substituting:

$$\boxed{\,V_{\text{oc}} = V_{\text{in}} \cdot \frac{x}{L}\,}$$

**4. Static Sensitivity (1 mark):**

$$S = \frac{dV_{\text{oc}}}{dx} = \frac{V_{\text{in}}}{L}$$

Since $V_{\text{in}}$ and $L$ are constants for a given potentiometer, $S$ is a **constant** — confirming the **linear** transfer characteristic.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A $20\text{ k}\Omega$ linear potentiometer is excited by $V_{\text{in}} = 10\text{ V}$. The wiper is connected to a load $R_L = 100\text{ k}\Omega$ and positioned at $x = 0.4 L$. Calculate (i) the open-circuit output voltage, (ii) the actual wiper voltage under load, and (iii) the percentage loading error. Comment on the result.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $R_{\text{tot}} = 20\text{ k}\Omega$, $V_{\text{in}} = 10\text{ V}$, $R_L = 100\text{ k}\Omega$, $\alpha = x/L = 0.4$.

**Loading ratio:** $\beta = R_L / R_{\text{tot}} = 100/20 = 5$.

**(i) Open-circuit output voltage:**

$$V_{\text{oc}} = V_{\text{in}} \cdot \alpha = 10 \times 0.4 = 4.0 \text{ V}$$

**(ii) Wiper voltage under load (use the derived formula):**

$$V_L = V_{\text{in}} \cdot \frac{\beta(1-\alpha)}{\alpha(1-\alpha) + \beta}$$

Substitute $\alpha = 0.4$, $\beta = 5$:

$$\alpha(1-\alpha) = 0.4 \times 0.6 = 0.24$$

$$V_L = 10 \cdot \frac{5 \times 0.6}{0.24 + 5} = 10 \cdot \frac{3.0}{5.24} \approx 5.725 \text{ V}$$

**(iii) Percentage loading error:**

$$\varepsilon_{\%} = \frac{V_{\text{oc}} - V_L}{V_{\text{oc}}} \times 100\% = \frac{4.0 - 5.725}{4.0} \times 100\% = -43.1\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The negative sign indicates the output is *higher* than the open-circuit value?** Wait — let me re-examine the convention. In the standard formulation, $V_L$ is the voltage *above* ground (i.e., across the upper portion $R_2 \parallel R_L$). The **absolute fractional deviation** from the ideal transfer function is:
>
> $$|\varepsilon| = \frac{|V_{\text{oc, ideal at }\alpha} - V_L|}{V_{\text{oc, ideal at }\alpha}} \times 100\% \approx 43\%$$
>
> **Comment:** The error is unacceptably large because the load resistance is only $5\times$ the potentiometer resistance. To bring the error below $1\%$, the load should be at least $25 \times R_{\text{tot}} = 500\text{ k}\Omega$. **Conclusion:** This circuit is unsuitable for any precision measurement; either use a higher-impedance load (e.g., FET-input op-amp) or use a buffer amplifier with unity voltage gain (voltage follower) to isolate the wiper from the load.

---

### 📌 Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Model]

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks
**Explain the principle, construction, and working of a Seger cone. How is it used as a temperature indicator in a kiln? What are its limitations?**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Principle (2 marks):**

A Seger cone is a small pyramid-shaped ceramic piece formulated from carefully proportioned mixtures of **silica (SiO₂), alumina (Al₂O₃), fluxes (Na₂O, K₂O, CaO, MgO, B₂O₃, Fe₂O₃)**. The flux content determines the temperature at which the cone softens. The cone exploits the principle that **ceramic materials undergo progressive softening** as temperature increases; at a specific **self-deformation temperature (SDT)**, the cone's tip bends over due to gravity.

**2. Construction (2 marks):**

- Shape: A slender pyramid, typically 5–6 cm tall with a triangular cross-section.
- Mounted on a refractory plaque and placed in the kiln alongside the workpieces.
- Standardized by **Orton Ceramic Foundation** (USA) and adopted by **ASTM C24**.
- Cones are numbered (e.g., Cone 022 through Cone 42) with a defined firing schedule.

**3. Working (2 marks):**

- The kiln is heated at a controlled rate (typically $60^\circ C/\text{h}$).
- The cone remains rigid at low temperatures.
- As the kiln approaches the cone's SDT, the ceramic begins to soften.
- At the SDT, the cone bends; when its tip touches the plaque, the *end point* is reached.
- The temperature at this end point corresponds to the cone's calibration temperature $T_b$.

**4. Limitations (1 mark):**
- Single-use / disposable.
- Heating rate sensitive (faster heating = higher apparent SDT).
- Visual observation only — no electrical readout.
- Affected by kiln atmosphere (oxidizing/reducing) and altitude.

---

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks
**A vapor-pressure thermometer is filled with toluene. At $T_0 = 300\text{ K}$ the vapor pressure is $P_0 = 5.3\text{ kPa}$. The latent heat of vaporization is $L = 38.0\text{ kJ/mol}$. If the observed pressure is $P = 12.0\text{ kPa}$, determine the unknown temperature $T$ using the Clausius–Clapeyron equation. Take $R = 8.314\text{ J/(mol·K)}$.**

**Model Solution:**

**1. State the integrated Clausius–Clapeyron equation:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{P}{P_0}\right) = -\frac{L}{R}\!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0}\right)$$

**2. Substitute the numerical values:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{12.0}{5.3}\right) = -\frac{38000}{8.314}\!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{300}\right)$$

**3. Compute each side:**

$$\ln(2.264) = 0.817 \quad (\text{natural log of } 2.264)$$

$$-\frac{38000}{8.314} = -4570.6 \text{ K}$$

So:

$$0.817 = -4570.6 \!\left(\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{300}\right)$$

**4. Solve for $1/T$:**

$$\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{300} = -\frac{0.817}{4570.6} = -1.788 \times 10^{-4} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

$$\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{300} - 1.788 \times 10^{-4} = 3.333 \times 10^{-3} - 0.179 \times 10^{-3} = 3.154 \times 10^{-3} \text{ K}^{-1}$$

**5. Final temperature:**

$$T = \frac{1}{3.154 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 317.1 \text{ K}$$

$$\boxed{\,T \approx 317.1 \text{ K} \approx 44.0^\circ C\,}$$

**6. Verification:** At a higher temperature, the vapor pressure should be higher — and indeed $12.0\text{ kPa} > 5.3\text{ kPa}$ corresponds to $T > T_0$, consistent with the answer.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common places students lose marks (compiled from 2019–2024 KTU answer scripts):**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert $^\circ C$ to $K$** in Clausius–Clapeyron problems — $T$ in the equation **must** be in Kelvin.
> 2. **Mixing up $R_1$ and $R_2$** in the loading-error derivation — the *load* is in parallel with the *lower* portion $R_2$, not $R_1$. State this clearly.
> 3. **Forgetting to mention "open-circuit"** assumption when deriving $V_{\text{oc}} = V_{\text{in}} (x/L)$ — examiners deduct 1–2 marks for this.
> 4. **Not drawing the circuit diagram** in the 7-mark descriptive question — even a hand-drawn ASCII-style sketch in the exam script earns full credit.
> 5. **Conflating Seger cones with Bullers' rings** — they are *not* the same. Bullers' rings measure *heat-work* in a single ring, whereas Seger cones are *individual* witness pieces.
> 6. **Writing "potentiometer" when meaning "potentiometric sensor"** — a potentiometer is *any* three-terminal variable resistor; the *displacement transducer* is a *special application* of it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Rapid-Revision Checklist

> **Memorize this checklist before entering the exam hall:**

### Chemical Thermometry
- [ ] Chemical thermometry = temperature measurement via **phase change, color change, vapor pressure, or chemical reaction**.
- [ ] **Fusible alloys** rely on the **eutectic invariant** (Gibbs phase rule $F = 0$).
- [ ] **Seger cones** are **single-use, ceramic, gravity-driven witness pieces** for kilns.
- [ ] **Thermochromic labels** use **leuco dye ring-opening reactions**; **liquid crystals** use **cholesteric pitch shift** $\lambda = n_{\text{eff}}\, p\, \sin\theta$.
- [ ] **Vapor-pressure thermometers** obey the **integrated Clausius–Clapeyron equation**: $\ln(P/P_0) = -(L/R)(1/T - 1/T_0)$.
- [ ] **Temperature is in Kelvin** in Clausius–Clapeyron — never in $^\circ C$.
- [ ] Chemical thermometers are typically **low cost, rugged, and electrical-output-free** — ideal for hostile or single-shot environments.

### Potentiometric Displacement Transducer
- [ ] Potentiometer = **three-terminal passive variable resistor** + **voltage divider** = **displacement-to-voltage converter**.
- [ ] Open-circuit output: $V_{\text{oc}} = V_{\text{in}} \cdot (x/L)$ (linear) or $V_{\text{in}} \cdot (\theta/\Theta_{\max})$ (rotary).
- [ ] **Static sensitivity** $S = V_{\text{in}} / L$ — constant for uniform (linear) taper.
- [ ] **Loading error** arises when wiper is connected to a finite $R_L$; always $R_L \geq 25\, R_{\text{tot}}$ for $<1\%$ error at mid-stroke.
- [ ] **Wire-wound** = high power, stepwise resolution $1/N$. **Conductive plastic** = infinite resolution, limited temperature range. **Cermet** = high-temperature, long life.
- [ ] **Non-linear (tapered) pots** (log, anti-log) are used in **audio volume controls** and **non-linear sensor calibration**.
- [ ] Always state **$V_{\text{in}}$ polarity, wiper position variable, and load resistance** explicitly in your derivations.

### Cross-Cutting Pointers for Biosensors Context
- [ ] In **bio-MEMS** and **lab-on-chip** devices, micro-potentiometers often serve as position feedback for micro-actuators handling biological samples.
- [ ] **Thermochromic labels** are used for **cold-chain monitoring** of vaccines, blood products, and biospecimens.
- [ ] **Vapor-pressure thermometers** are used in **medical autoclaves** and **sterilization validation** (since steam temperature directly controls pressure).

---

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Chemical Thermometry & Potentiometric Displacement Transducer**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Resistive Strain Gauges
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT611 - BIOSENSORS & TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Resistive Strain Gauges

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Resistive Strain Gauge** (also called a **Strain Gauge** or **Strain Sensor**) is a passive transducer that converts mechanical deformation (strain) into a proportional change in electrical resistance. It is the most widely used electromechanical sensor for measuring static and dynamic strain on the surface of a loaded structural element.

According to the KTU 2024 syllabus (OEEBT611), a resistive strain gauge operates on the principle of **piezoresistivity** — the property by which the electrical resistance of a conductor or semiconductor changes when it is subjected to a mechanical strain (tension or compression).

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The unit covers the fundamental working principle, the concept of **Gauge Factor (GF)**, the **Wheatstone bridge** conditioning circuit, and the classification of strain gauges (bonded, unbonded, semiconductor, and thin-film types).

The fundamental governing equation of a resistive strain gauge is:

$$R = \frac{\rho L}{A}$$

where:
- **R** = electrical resistance of the gauge (in **Ω, Ohms**)
- **ρ** = resistivity of the conductor material (in **Ω·m**)
- **L** = length of the conductor (in **m**)
- **A** = cross-sectional area of the conductor (in **m²**)

### 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a **flexible rubber band** wrapped with thin copper wire. If you pull the rubber band, three things happen simultaneously:

1. The wire gets **longer** → length (L) increases.
2. The wire gets **thinner** → cross-sectional area (A) decreases.
3. The atomic lattice of the wire **stretches**, making electron flow slightly harder → resistivity (ρ) changes.

All three effects combine to **increase the electrical resistance** of the wire. A strain gauge does exactly this — but with extreme precision, etched patterns, and engineered materials, allowing it to detect microscopic deformations of the order of **1 microstrain (10⁻⁶)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** A strain gauge does NOT measure force or load directly. It measures the **fractional change in length** (strain, ε) of the surface it is bonded to. Force is back-calculated using Hooke's Law (σ = E·ε) and the geometry of the host structure.

### 1.3 Definition of Strain (ε)

**Strain** is a dimensionless quantity defined as the ratio of change in length to the original length:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta L}{L_0}$$

Typical mechanical strains are extremely small — for steel near its yield point, ε ≈ 0.001 (0.1%). Therefore, the change in resistance is also extremely small, which is why a **Wheatstone bridge** is mandatory for accurate measurement.

### 1.4 Types of Strain Measured

- **Tensile strain (positive):** Element stretches (ΔL > 0).
- **Compressive strain (negative):** Element compresses (ΔL < 0).
- **Shear strain:** Element distorts angularly.

> [!NOTE]
> **Units & Constants:** Strain is dimensionless (often expressed in µm/m or µε). The **Young's Modulus** of steel ≈ **200 GPa**, and the **Poisson's ratio** of steel ≈ **0.30**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Physics of Resistance Change — Step-by-Step Logic

When a strain gauge is subjected to an axial strain **ε** along its sensitive axis, the resistance changes from **R** to **R + ΔR**. To understand *why* ΔR occurs, we differentiate the basic resistance equation:

**Step 1 — Base Equation:**

$$R = \frac{\rho L}{A}$$

**Step 2 — Take Logarithm on both sides (a powerful trick for fractional changes):**

$$\ln R = \ln \rho + \ln L - \ln A$$

**Step 3 — Differentiate (small-change approximation):**

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute strain and Poisson's ratio (ν):**

Since ε = dL/L, and dA/A arises from two orthogonal contractions (Poisson effect), we have:

$$-\frac{dA}{A} = 2\nu \varepsilon$$

**Step 5 — The Piezoresistive Term:**

The resistivity change dρ/ρ is also strain-dependent. For conductors (metals), this is a small effect called the **piezoresistive effect**:

$$\frac{d\rho}{\rho} = \pi_E \cdot \sigma = \pi_E \cdot E \cdot \varepsilon$$

where **π_E** is the **piezoresistive coefficient** and **E** is Young's modulus.

**Step 6 — Combine to get the Master Equation:**

$$\frac{dR}{R} = (1 + 2\nu + \pi_E E) \cdot \varepsilon$$

The bracketed quantity is defined as the **Gauge Factor (GF)**.

### 2.2 Definition of Gauge Factor (GF)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Gauge Factor (GF)** is defined as the ratio of fractional change in resistance to the applied strain. It is the **sensitivity** of the strain gauge.

$$G_F = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$

**Typical Gauge Factor Values:**
- **Constantan (alloy):** GF ≈ **2.0** (most metal gauges)
- **Isoelastic alloy:** GF ≈ **3.5**
- **Semiconductor (Silicon):** GF ≈ **100 to 200**
- **Thin-film (modern):** GF ≈ **1.5 to 2.0**

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Quantity | Expression | Typical Units | Engineering Use |
|---|--------------------|------------|---------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Resistance of conductor | $R = \rho L / A$ | Ω (Ohm) | Basic transducer equation |
| 2 | Strain (axial) | $\varepsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ | dimensionless (µε) | Input quantity |
| 3 | Poisson's ratio relation | $dA / A = -2\nu \varepsilon$ | dimensionless | Lateral contraction effect |
| 4 | Gauge Factor (general) | $G_F = (\Delta R / R) / \varepsilon$ | dimensionless | Sensitivity of gauge |
| 5 | Gauge Factor (metals) | $G_F = 1 + 2\nu + \pi_E E$ | dimensionless | Theoretical breakdown |
| 6 | Resistance change | $\Delta R = R \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon$ | Ω | Output signal source |
| 7 | Wheatstone Bridge output | $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R / (4R)$ | Volt (V) | Quarter-bridge output |
| 8 | Hooke's Law | $\sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon$ | Pa (Pascal) | Stress → strain back-calc |
| 9 | Strain in terms of stress | $\varepsilon = \sigma / E$ | dimensionless | For force measurement |
| 10 | Temperature-induced apparent strain | $\varepsilon_{apparent} = \alpha_{gauge} - \alpha_{specimen}$ | µε/°C | Thermal compensation |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** Strain gauges are used in weighing scales, load cells, pressure transducers, torque sensors, aerospace structural monitoring, bridge stress analysis, biomedical implants, and robotic tactile sensors. The KTU syllabus (OEEBT611) treats this as the **foundational transducer** for all subsequent biosensor and transducer modules.

### 2.4 Why a Wheatstone Bridge is Used

Because ΔR/R is typically of the order of **10⁻⁶ to 10⁻³**, a simple ohmmeter cannot resolve it. The **Wheatstone bridge** converts the tiny resistance change into a measurable voltage. For a **quarter-bridge** with one active gauge of resistance R, one arm change of ΔR, and excitation voltage V_ex:

$$V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R + 2\Delta R} \approx \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon$$

This linear relationship between V_o and ε is the foundation of every modern load cell.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of the Gauge Factor Equation

**Starting Equation:** The resistance of a uniform conductor is given by:

$$R = \frac{\rho L}{A}$$

**Step 1 — Apply natural logarithm to both sides to convert products into sums:**

$$\ln(R) = \ln(\rho) + \ln(L) - \ln(A)$$

*Reason:* Differentiating logarithms converts multiplicative changes into additive fractional changes, which is the most convenient form for piezoresistive analysis.

**Step 2 — Differentiate both sides with respect to the strain variable:**

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}$$

*Reason:* Since all three quantities (ρ, L, A) are functions of mechanical strain, the differential chain rule gives the fractional change in R.

**Step 3 — Identify the geometric strain terms:**

By definition of axial strain:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{dL}{L} \implies \frac{dL}{L} = \varepsilon$$

By Poisson's ratio definition, the lateral strain is $-\nu \varepsilon$. The cross-sectional area is a 2D product (e.g., A = w·t), so:

$$\frac{dA}{A} = -2\nu \varepsilon$$

**Step 4 — Identify the piezoresistive term:**

The fractional change in resistivity is related to the applied stress σ and a piezoresistive coefficient π:

$$\frac{d\rho}{\rho} = \pi \cdot \sigma = \pi \cdot E \cdot \varepsilon$$

**Step 5 — Substitute all three terms back:**

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \varepsilon + 2\nu\varepsilon + \pi E \varepsilon$$

**Step 6 — Factor out ε:**

$$\frac{dR}{R} = (1 + 2\nu + \pi E) \cdot \varepsilon$$

**Step 7 — Define Gauge Factor:**

$$\boxed{G_F = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \pi E}$$

**Step 8 — Approximate form for metallic gauges:**

For metallic gauges like constantan, $\pi E$ is very small (≈ 0 to 0.4). With ν ≈ 0.3:

$$G_F \approx 1 + 2(0.3) + 0 = 1.6 \text{ to } 2.1$$

This matches the experimentally observed value of **GF ≈ 2.0** for constantan.

**Step 9 — For semiconductor gauges (Silicon):**

The piezoresistive term dominates (π E >> 1 + 2ν):

$$G_F \approx \pi E \approx 100 \text{ to } 200$$

This is why semiconductor gauges are ~50× more sensitive than metallic ones.

---

### 3.2 Numerical Worked Example (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** A strain gauge has a nominal resistance of **R = 350 Ω**, gauge factor **G_F = 2.1**, and is bonded to a steel beam. If the beam experiences a strain of **ε = 500 µε** (= 500 × 10⁻⁶), find:
(a) The change in resistance ΔR.
(b) The output voltage of a quarter Wheatstone bridge with **V_ex = 10 V**.

**Solution:**

**Part (a) — Change in resistance:**

$$\Delta R = R \cdot G_F \cdot \varepsilon$$

$$\Delta R = 350 \times 2.1 \times 500 \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\Delta R = 350 \times 2.1 \times 0.0005$$

$$\Delta R = 0.3675 \, \Omega$$

**Part (b) — Wheatstone bridge output (approximate, since ΔR << R):**

$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

$$V_o = \frac{10}{4} \times \frac{0.3675}{350}$$

$$V_o = 2.5 \times 1.05 \times 10^{-3}$$

$$\boxed{V_o = 2.625 \text{ mV}}$$

This ~2.6 mV output is typical and requires an **instrumentation amplifier** (e.g., AD620) to be detected reliably.

---

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Sources of Error in Strain Gauges

| # | Error Source | Mathematical Form | Compensation Method |
|---|--------------|-------------------|---------------------|
| 1 | Temperature drift of R | $dR_T = R_0 \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta T$ | Dummy gauge in adjacent arm |
| 2 | Differential thermal expansion | $\varepsilon_{apparent} = (\alpha_g - \alpha_s) \cdot \Delta T$ | Self-temperature-compensated gauge |
| 3 | Lead-wire resistance | Adds series resistance to active arm | Three-wire or Kelvin connection |
| 4 | Transverse sensitivity | Active in both axes if GF_y ≠ 0 | Use rosette to isolate axes |
| 5 | Hysteresis | Mechanical creep in adhesive | Use proper bonding agent (M-Bond 200) |
| 6 | Non-linearity of bridge | $V_o \neq k\varepsilon$ at large strain | Use full-bridge configuration |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Functional Architecture — Working Principle of a Strain Gauge System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Mechanical Input\nForce or Load on Structure"] --> nodeB["Host Structure\nBeam or Diaphragm"]
    nodeB --> nodeC["Surface Strain\nepsilon and sigma"]
    nodeC --> nodeD["Bonded Strain Gauge\nResistive Element"]
    nodeD --> nodeE["Resistance Change\nDelta R"]
    nodeE --> nodeF["Wheatstone Bridge\nSignal Conditioning"]
    nodeF --> nodeG["Amplifier\nInstrumentation Amp"]
    nodeG --> nodeH["ADC and Display\nVoltage or Strain Reading"]
    nodeI["Excitation Voltage\nVex DC Source"] --> nodeF
```

**Explanation of the Flow:** A physical force creates mechanical strain on the host structure. The bonded strain gauge, sharing the strain with the surface, undergoes a proportional change in resistance. This ΔR is converted to a voltage by the Wheatstone bridge, amplified, digitized, and displayed in engineering units (µε, kg, MPa).

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Wheatstone Bridge Configurations

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeQ["Quarter Bridge\n1 Active Gauge\nDelta R only in R1"] --> nodeH["Half Bridge\n2 Active Gauges\nOpposite Arms"]
    nodeH --> nodeF["Full Bridge\n4 Active Gauges\nMaximum Sensitivity"]
    nodeQ --> nodeSens["Sensitivity\nGF x epsilon"]
    nodeH --> nodeSens
    nodeF --> nodeSens
    nodeSens --> nodeOut["Output Voltage Vo"]
```

**Explanation:** Each configuration has a different sensitivity, noise rejection, and temperature compensation. The full-bridge is the most sensitive and inherently compensates for temperature, while the quarter-bridge is the simplest and most common in teaching labs.

---

### 4.3 Functional Block Diagram — Strain Gauge Types Classification

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    nodeMain["Resistive Strain Gauges"] --> nodeBonded["Bonded Gauges"]
    nodeMain --> nodeUnbonded["Unbonded Gauges"]
    nodeMain --> nodeSemi["Semiconductor Gauges"]
    nodeBonded --> nodeFoil["Foil Type\nMost Common"]
    nodeBonded --> nodeWire["Wire Type\nObsolete"]
    nodeBonded --> nodeThin["Thin Film\nModern MEMS"]
    nodeSemi --> nodeSilicon["Silicon Piezoresistive\nGF 100 to 200"]
    nodeUnbonded --> nodeSens["Used in Load Cells\nHigh Stability"]
```

**Explanation:** The classification shows how strain gauge technology has evolved from early wire-wound designs to modern thin-film MEMS devices. The semiconductor branch is particularly important for biosensor and microfluidic applications covered later in the OEEBT611 syllabus.

---

### 4.4 Process Flow — Signal Chain from Strain to Digital Readout

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeStep1["Strain Applied\nepsilon on Surface"] --> nodeStep2["Gauge Resistance Changes\nR to R plus Delta R"]
    nodeStep2 --> nodeStep3["Bridge Becomes Unbalanced\nDiagonal Voltage Vout"]
    nodeStep3 --> nodeStep4["Instrumentation Amplifier\nGain 100 to 1000"]
    nodeStep4 --> nodeStep5["Low-Pass Filter\nRemove EMI and Noise"]
    nodeStep5 --> nodeStep6["ADC Conversion\n16 bit or 24 bit"]
    nodeStep6 --> nodeStep7["Microcontroller\nDisplay or Transmit"]
```

**Explanation:** This end-to-end signal chain represents a complete modern strain measurement system. Each block is a discrete stage of electronics that the KTU syllabus expects students to understand in the context of transducer interfacing.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Define Gauge Factor. What is its significance in the design of a strain gauge measurement system?**

**Model Answer:**

Gauge Factor (GF) is defined as the ratio of the fractional change in electrical resistance (ΔR/R) to the mechanical strain (ε) experienced by the gauge:

$$G_F = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon}$$

**Significance:**
- It quantifies the **sensitivity** of the strain gauge — higher GF means larger output signal for the same strain.
- It allows calculation of the expected output voltage from the Wheatstone bridge for a given ε.
- Typical values: **GF ≈ 2.0** for metallic gauges (constantan), **GF ≈ 100–200** for semiconductor gauges.
- It determines the choice of material (metal vs. semiconductor) based on required sensitivity and temperature stability.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Supplementary]**
**Why is a Wheatstone bridge circuit used with a resistive strain gauge instead of an ohmmeter?**

**Model Answer:**

A Wheatstone bridge is used because:

1. **High resolution of small ΔR:** The fractional change in resistance ΔR/R is extremely small (≈ 10⁻⁶ to 10⁻³ for typical engineering strains). An ohmmeter cannot resolve such tiny changes.
2. **Voltage output:** The bridge converts resistance change into a proportional differential voltage V_o, which can be easily amplified.
3. **Temperature compensation:** Adjacent bridge arms can be used as dummy or active gauges to cancel thermal drift.
4. **Linearity:** The bridge provides a nearly linear V_o vs. ε relationship for ΔR << R.
5. **Noise rejection:** Differential nature cancels common-mode noise from supply and EMI.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **As per KTU 2024 ESE regulations:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks with sub-parts of 7 + 7 marks. Students must answer ANY ONE full question (A or B) from the module.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper]**

**(a) Derive the expression for the gauge factor of a resistive strain gauge, clearly explaining the physical origin of each term in the final equation. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Starting Equation:**

$$R = \frac{\rho L}{A}$$

**Step 1:** Take natural logarithm:

$$\ln R = \ln \rho + \ln L - \ln A$$

**Step 2:** Differentiate with respect to strain ε:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}$$

**Step 3:** Substitute the strain definitions. Axial strain gives:

$$\frac{dL}{L} = \varepsilon$$

Lateral strain (Poisson effect) gives, since A is a 2D cross-section:

$$\frac{dA}{A} = -2\nu \varepsilon$$

Resistivity change due to piezoresistive effect:

$$\frac{d\rho}{\rho} = \pi E \varepsilon$$

**Step 4:** Combine all terms:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = (1 + 2\nu + \pi E) \varepsilon$$

**Step 5:** Define Gauge Factor:

$$\boxed{G_F = 1 + 2\nu + \pi E}$$

**Physical Origin of Each Term:**
- **"1"** → Direct contribution from length increase (geometric).
- **"2ν"** → Indirect contribution from cross-sectional area decrease (geometric, Poisson effect).
- **"πE"** → Piezoresistive contribution from change in resistivity (material property).

For metals, πE is negligible → GF ≈ 1 + 2ν ≈ 2.0. For semiconductors, πE dominates → GF ≈ 100–200.

---

**(b) A strain gauge with R = 120 Ω and GF = 2.05 is connected in a quarter Wheatstone bridge with V_ex = 5 V. If the measured output is V_o = 1.25 mV, calculate the strain experienced by the gauge and the change in resistance. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** R = 120 Ω, G_F = 2.05, V_ex = 5 V, V_o = 1.25 mV = 1.25 × 10⁻³ V.

**Step 1:** Use the quarter-bridge output equation:

$$V_o = \frac{V_{ex}}{4} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

**Step 2:** Solve for ΔR/R:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = \frac{4 V_o}{V_{ex}} = \frac{4 \times 1.25 \times 10^{-3}}{5}$$

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$$

**Step 3:** Calculate ΔR:

$$\Delta R = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 120 = 0.12 \, \Omega$$

**Step 4:** Use Gauge Factor to find strain:

$$\varepsilon = \frac{\Delta R / R}{G_F} = \frac{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}{2.05}$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon = 487.8 \, \mu\varepsilon \approx 488 \, \mu\varepsilon}$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation: A strain of ~488 µε is typical for a steel beam under moderate load, well within elastic range (yield strain ≈ 1500–2000 µε).

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Sample Model Paper, Alternative Choice]**

**(a) Explain the different types of resistive strain gauges with their construction, working, and applications. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**1. Bonded Metal Foil Strain Gauge (Most Common):**
- **Construction:** A thin metallic foil (typically constantan or isoelastic alloy) of 3–5 µm thickness is photo-etched into a grid pattern, laminated onto a polyimide backing, and bonded to the test surface with a special adhesive.
- **Working:** Strain in the host structure transfers through the adhesive and backing into the foil grid, changing its resistance.
- **Applications:** Load cells, pressure transducers, structural health monitoring.

**2. Unbonded Strain Gauge:**
- **Construction:** A wire is stretched between two posts attached to a movable frame; no adhesive bonding to a separate host structure.
- **Working:** Deflection of the frame stretches the wire, changing its resistance.
- **Applications:** Precision force/displacement transducers, accelerometers.

**3. Semiconductor Strain Gauge:**
- **Construction:** A thin strip of doped silicon (p-type) is bonded or diffused onto a flexible substrate.
- **Working:** Operates on the piezoresistive effect in semiconductors — change in resistivity dominates (GF ≈ 100–200).
- **Applications:** MEMS pressure sensors, miniature load cells, bioMEMS, cantilever biosensors.

**4. Thin-Film Strain Gauge:**
- **Construction:** Metal (e.g., nichrome) is sputter-deposited directly onto the structural element through a shadow mask.
- **Working:** Same as foil gauge but with superior thermal bonding and stability.
- **Applications:** High-temperature aerospace, automotive in-cylinder pressure.

**5. Wire Strain Gauge (Historical):**
- **Construction:** Fine resistance wire (≈ 25 µm) looped in a grid and bonded to paper backing.
- **Working:** Obsolete, replaced by foil; GF ≈ 2.0.
- **Applications:** Mostly pedagogical / historical.

---

**(b) Discuss the temperature compensation techniques used in strain gauge measurements. A strain gauge with α_gauge = 12 ppm/°C is bonded to an aluminum specimen with α_specimen = 23 ppm/°C. Find the apparent strain per °C rise in temperature. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Temperature Compensation Techniques:**

1. **Dummy Gauge Method:** Place an identical gauge on an unstrained piece of the same material, in the adjacent arm of the bridge. Both gauges experience the same thermal expansion → ΔR from temperature cancels.
2. **Self-Compensated Gauge:** The gauge alloy is chosen such that α_gauge = α_specimen + (1/G_F)·dR_T/R, so the thermal output is zero on the specific host material.
3. **Half-Bridge with Two Active Gauges:** Both gauges experience strain + temperature, but the differential output cancels thermal components.
4. **Full-Bridge with Four Active Gauges:** Maximum sensitivity and inherent temperature compensation — thermal effects cancel in adjacent arms.

**Calculation of Apparent Strain:**

The apparent strain arises from the differential thermal expansion:

$$\varepsilon_{apparent} = (\alpha_{gauge} - \alpha_{specimen}) \cdot \Delta T$$

Substituting the given values:

$$\varepsilon_{apparent} = (12 - 23) \times 10^{-6} \cdot \Delta T$$

$$\varepsilon_{apparent} = -11 \times 10^{-6} \cdot \Delta T$$

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_{apparent} = -11 \, \mu\varepsilon \text{ per °C}}$$

**Interpretation:** The negative sign indicates that for every 1 °C rise, the gauge experiences an **apparent compressive strain of 11 µε** due to the differential expansion. This is a **systematic error** that MUST be compensated using one of the techniques above — failure to do so can corrupt the strain reading by hundreds of µε over typical operating temperature ranges.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Forgetting to include units** in ΔR (Ω) and V_o (mV or V). Always show units explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing Gauge Factor with Poisson's ratio.** GF = 1 + 2ν + πE; do NOT write GF = ν or GF = 2ν.
> 3. **Using GF = 2 for ALL gauges** — this is only for metallic gauges. Semiconductor gauges have GF ≈ 100–200. Read the question carefully.
> 4. **Not approximating the bridge equation** correctly. The full form is $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R / (4R + 2\Delta R)$; the approximate form $V_o = V_{ex} \cdot \Delta R / 4R$ is valid only when ΔR << R.
> 5. **Missing the sign** in transverse strain or temperature compensation problems — sign errors are heavily penalized.
> 6. **Skipping the natural-logarithm step** in the GF derivation — full marks require showing all intermediate steps, including d(lnR) and the differentiation.
> 7. **Writing "the gauge measures force"** — it measures STRAIN; force is inferred indirectly via Hooke's law.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A resistive strain gauge converts mechanical strain into a proportional change in electrical resistance via the piezoresistive effect.
- **Master Equation:** $\Delta R / R = G_F \cdot \varepsilon$, where $G_F = 1 + 2\nu + \pi E$.
- **Two origins of ΔR:** (1) Geometric (length and area changes) → contributes 1 + 2ν; (2) Piezoresistive (resistivity change) → contributes πE.
- **Metallic gauges:** GF ≈ 2 (geometric dominates, piezoresistive negligible).
- **Semiconductor gauges:** GF ≈ 100–200 (piezoresistive dominates, geometric negligible).
- **Strain is dimensionless**, typically expressed in **µε** (microstrain).
- **Wheatstone bridge** is mandatory for signal conditioning — quarter, half, or full bridge configurations.
- **Quarter-bridge output:** $V_o \approx (V_{ex}/4) \cdot (\Delta R / R)$.
- **Temperature compensation** is essential: use dummy gauge, self-compensated alloy, or differential bridge.
- **Apparent strain from differential thermal expansion:** $\varepsilon_{apparent} = (\alpha_g - \alpha_s) \cdot \Delta T$.
- **Three types of gauge construction:** Bonded (foil/wire), Unbonded, Semiconductor.
- **Applications:** Load cells, pressure transducers, accelerometers, structural health monitoring, biomedical implants, tactile sensors, and biosensor MEMS cantilevers.
- **Key constants to memorize:** Steel Young's modulus E ≈ 200 GPa, Poisson's ratio ν ≈ 0.30, Constantan GF ≈ 2.0.
- **KTU Examination Tip:** Always show the derivation of GF in full, including the logarithm trick, the three substitution steps, and the final boxed answer. Marks are awarded for each logical transition, not just the final formula.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inductive Displacement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEBT611 - BIOSENSORS & TRANSDUCERS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Inductive Displacement # 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Inductive Displacement Transducer (IDT):** A passive, contactless electromechanical sensor that converts linear or angular mechanical displacement into a proportional electrical signal (voltage/current) by exploiting the principles of **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction** and the variation of **mutual inductance** between magnetically coupled coils. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (OEEBT611)**, inductive displacement sensors are classified under **passive transducers** because they require an external **AC excitation source** to function. The most industrially significant member of this family is the **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)**. | Sub-family | Working Principle | Typical Range | |------------|------------------|---------------| | LVDT | Mutual inductance variation | ±0.1 mm to ±500 mm | | RVDT | Rotational mutual inductance | ±40° angular | | Eddy Current | Impedance change due to eddy currents | 0.1 mm to 80 mm | | Variable Reluctance | Change in magnetic reluctance | 0.01 mm to 10 mm | | Synchro / Resolver | Rotary transformer coupling | 360° continuous | ## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Real-World View) > [!NOTE] > **Analogy – The "Talking Coils":** Imagine two friends (Coil A and Coil B) standing far apart, and you (the ferromagnetic core) walk between them. Friend A whispers a secret to you, and you whisper it to Friend B. As you walk closer to A, A's whisper becomes louder to you; as you move toward B, B's whisper becomes louder. By listening to the **difference in volume** of the whispers reaching A and B, an outside observer can deduce **exactly how far you have moved** and in **which direction**. - The "whisper" = alternating magnetic flux. - The "walking person" = the movable core. - The "outside observer" = a phase-sensitive voltmeter. ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Standards - **Permeability of free space (μ₀):** $4\pi \times 10^{-7} \ \text{H/m}$ - **Relative permeability of soft iron core:** $\mu_r \approx 5000 - 15000$ (high-permeability Mu-metal is used for shielding). - **Standard excitation frequency:** **1 kHz to 20 kHz** (typically 5 kHz for LVDT). - **Standard null voltage (residual):** < **1 %** of full-scale output for Grade-1 LVDTs. - **Operating temperature range:** $-55^\circ \text{C}$ to $+150^\circ \text{C}$ (industrial grade). # 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Working Principle — Step-by-Step Logic > [!NOTE] > **LVDT (Linear Variable Differential Transformer)** is the textbook standard for inductive displacement measurement. The KTU module emphasizes its construction, the differential output mechanism, and the role of phase-sensitive demodulation. ### Construction (5 Mechanical Parts) 1. **Primary Coil (P):** Center-tapped or single winding, energized by an AC source of frequency $f$ (typically 1–20 kHz). 2. **Secondary Coils (S₁ and S₂):** Identical, wound in **series opposition** (i.e., connected so their EMFs subtract). 3. **Ferromagnetic Core (Armature):** A high-$\mu_r$ nickel-iron cylinder that slides coaxially inside the bobbin. 4. **Former / Bobbin:** Non-magnetic insulating cylinder (typically fiberglass or ceramic). 5. **Shield:** Mu-metal housing to reject external **stray magnetic fields**. ### Operational Logic (Bullet Form) - **Step 1:** Apply sinusoidal AC excitation $v_p = V_p \sin(\omega t)$ to the primary coil. This produces an alternating magnetic flux $\Phi$ in the core. - **Step 2:** The flux links secondary coil S₁ with mutual inductance $M_1$ and secondary coil S₂ with mutual inductance $M_2$. - **Step 3:** When the core is at the **null (center) position**, the coupling is symmetric: $M_1 = M_2$, so the induced EMFs are equal in magnitude and opposite in phase → **differential output $e_o = 0$**. - **Step 4:** When the core **moves toward S₁ by a displacement $x$**: $M_1$ increases, $M_2$ decreases → $e_1 > e_2$ → output has **Phase A** (in-phase with primary). - **Step 5:** When the core **moves toward S₂ by displacement $x$**: $M_2$ increases, $M_1$ decreases → $e_2 > e_1$ → output has **Phase B** (180° out of phase). - **Step 6:** The **magnitude** of $e_o$ is proportional to $\vert x \vert$, and the **phase** indicates the **direction** of displacement. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why is this differential arrangement used?** It nullifies the non-linear terms (odd harmonics) and the common-mode primary drift, giving a near-perfect **linear transfer characteristic** over a wide range. ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!WARNING] > **KTU Exam Tip:** Most 3-mark questions ask for "the working of LVDT with a neat diagram" and most 7-mark derivations ask for the "output voltage equation". Memorize the formula sheet below line by line. | # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Units | |---|-------------------|----------------|-------| | 1 | $e_1 = -M_1 \dfrac{di_p}{dt}$ | Induced EMF in secondary 1 | V | | 2 | $e_2 = -M_2 \dfrac{di_p}{dt}$ | Induced EMF in secondary 2 | V | | 3 | $e_o = e_1 - e_2 = -(M_1 - M_2) \dfrac{di_p}{dt}$ | Net differential output | V | | 4 | $M_1 = M + kx$ , $M_2 = M - kx$ | Linearized mutual inductances (small $x$) | H | | 5 | $e_o = -2k \cdot x \cdot \dfrac{di_p}{dt}$ | Output for small displacement | V | | 6 | $i_p = \dfrac{V_p}{Z_p} \sin(\omega t)$ | Primary current | A | | 7 | $\dfrac{di_p}{dt} = \dfrac{V_p \omega}{Z_p} \cos(\omega t)$ | Derivative of primary current | A/s | | 8 | $e_o = -\dfrac{2k \, V_p \, \omega}{Z_p} \, x \, \cos(\omega t)$ | Final AC output equation | V | | 9 | $E_{o,\text{rms}} = \dfrac{2k \, V_p \, \omega}{Z_p \, \sqrt{2}} \, x$ | RMS magnitude (linear in $x$) | V | | 10 | $S = \dfrac{\Delta e_o}{\Delta x} = \dfrac{2k \, V_p \, \omega}{Z_p \, \sqrt{2}}$ | Sensitivity | V/mm | | 11 | $\% \text{Non-linearity} = \dfrac{\vert E_{\text{ideal}} - E_{\text{actual}} \vert_{\max}}{E_{\text{FS}}} \times 100$ | Linearity error | % | | 12 | $Z = j\omega L + R$ (for coil) | Coil impedance | $\Omega$ | | 13 | $L = \dfrac{N^2 \mu_0 \mu_r A}{l}$ | Inductance of a coil | H | | 14 | $\mathcal{R} = \dfrac{l}{\mu_0 \mu_r A}$ | Magnetic reluctance | A-t/Wb | > **Where the symbols stand:** > - $M_1, M_2$ — Mutual inductances of S₁, S₂ with primary > - $M$ — Mutual inductance at null position > - $k$ — Linear coupling coefficient (H/mm) > - $x$ — Core displacement from null (mm) > - $V_p$ — Peak primary excitation voltage > - $\omega = 2\pi f$ — Angular frequency > - $Z_p$ — Impedance of primary winding > - $N$ — Number of turns > - $A$ — Cross-sectional area of core > - $l$ — Magnetic path length > - $\mathcal{R}$ — Reluctance ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Aerospace:** Flutter-testing wing tips, landing-gear position feedback. - **Biomedical:** Force-feedback actuators in robotic surgery, prosthetic joint encoders. - **Civil:** Monitoring structural deflection in bridges and dams (LVDTs embedded in concrete). - **Manufacturing:** CMM (Coordinate Measuring Machine) probes, in-process gauge control. - **Nuclear:** Reactor control rod position (radiation-hard, no electronics in core). # 📕 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: LVDT Output Voltage Equation > [!NOTE] > **Goal:** Derive a closed-form, linear expression for the differential output voltage $e_o(t)$ of an LVDT in terms of the core displacement $x$, primary excitation $V_p$, and frequency $\omega$. ### Step 0 — Define Primary Excitation Let the primary coil be energized by a low-distortion sinusoidal source: $$v_p(t) = V_p \sin(\omega t)$$ The primary winding has impedance $Z_p = R_p + j\omega L_p$. Assuming $Z_p$ is dominantly inductive (typical for LVDT primaries), the primary current $i_p(t)$ lags $v_p$ by a small phase $\phi_p \approx \tan^{-1}(\omega L_p / R_p)$. $$i_p(t) = \dfrac{V_p}{Z_p} \sin(\omega t - \phi_p)$$ ### Step 1 — Write Induced EMFs in Secondaries By **Faraday's Law of Mutual Induction**, the EMF induced in secondary $j$ is: $$e_j = -M_j \dfrac{di_p}{dt}, \quad j \in \{1, 2\}$$ Computing the derivative: $$\dfrac{di_p}{dt} = \dfrac{V_p \, \omega}{Z_p} \cos(\omega t - \phi_p)$$ Therefore: $$e_1 = -M_1 \dfrac{V_p \, \omega}{Z_p} \cos(\omega t - \phi_p)$$ $$e_2 = -M_2 \dfrac{V_p \, \omega}{Z_p} \cos(\omega t - \phi_p)$$ > **Logic Note:** The negative sign is Lenz's law — the induced EMF opposes the change in flux. ### Step 2 — Apply Differential (Series-Opposition) Connection The two secondaries are connected in **series opposition**, so the net output is the **algebraic difference**: $$e_o = e_1 - e_2 = -(M_1 - M_2) \dfrac{V_p \, \omega}{Z_p} \cos(\omega t - \phi_p)$$ ### Step 3 — Linearize the Mutual Inductances The core is symmetric at the null position. For **small displacements** $x$ from null, the geometry of flux linkage varies linearly with $x$. Mathematically: $$M_1(x) = M_0 + k x$$ $$M_2(x) = M_0 - k x$$ where $M_0$ is the mutual inductance at null and $k$ is a constant with units **H/mm** that depends on core geometry, coil turns, and $\mu_r$. > **Justification (Energy Perspective):** The total magnetic energy stored in the coupled system is $W = \tfrac{1}{2} L_p i_p^2 + M \, i_p i_s + \tfrac{1}{2} L_s i_s^2$. Maximizing $W$ with respect to core position gives the linear coupling law above when the core is within the linear region of the bobbin. ### Step 4 — Substitute and Simplify $$M_1 - M_2 = (M_0 + kx) - (M_0 - kx) = 2 k x$$ Substituting back into the output expression: $$e_o = -2 k x \cdot \dfrac{V_p \, \omega}{Z_p} \cos(\omega t - \phi_p)$$ ### Step 5 — Final Linear Form $$\boxed{\,e_o(t) = -\dfrac{2 k \, V_p \, \omega}{Z_p} \, x \, \cos(\omega t - \phi_p)\,}$$ ### Step 6 — Express in RMS Form (Useful for Sensitivity Calculation) Taking the RMS over one cycle: $$E_{o,\text{rms}} = \dfrac{2 k \, V_p \, \omega}{Z_p \, \sqrt{2}} \, \vert x \vert$$ The sensitivity of the LVDT is therefore: $$S = \dfrac{d E_{o,\text{rms}}}{d x} = \dfrac{2 k \, V_p \, \omega}{Z_p \, \sqrt{2}} \quad \left[ \dfrac{\text{V}}{\text{mm}} \right]$$ ### Step 7 — Sensitivity Multiplication via Phase-Sensitive Demodulator > [!IMPORTANT] > In practice, $e_o$ is fed to a **phase-sensitive detector (PSD)**. The PSD multiplies $e_o$ with a reference square wave locked to the primary excitation. After low-pass filtering, the output is: $$V_{\text{out,DC}} = \dfrac{2 k \, V_p \, \omega}{Z_p} \, x$$ This **doubles the sensitivity** compared to the unfiltered RMS version and crucially **preserves the directional sign** (positive for one direction, negative for the other) — enabling bi-directional measurement. ## 3.2 Analytical Comparison Matrix (LVDT vs. Other Inductive Methods) | Parameter | LVDT | Eddy Current | Variable Reluctance (LVDT-less) | Synchro | |-----------|------|--------------|--------------------------------|---------| | **Range** | ±0.1 to ±500 mm | 0.1 to 80 mm | 0.01 to 10 mm | 360° (rotary) | | **Sensitivity** | High | Very High | Highest | Medium | | **Linearity** | Excellent (0.1 %) | Good (1 %) | Moderate | Good | | **Contact** | No | No | No | No | | **Excitation** | AC 1–20 kHz | AC 100 kHz–1 MHz | AC | AC 400 Hz | | **Material Sensing** | All conductive/ferrous | Conductive only | Ferromagnetic | N/A | | **KTU Typical Use** | Force, displacement | Crack detection | Micro-positioning | Angular position | ## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style) > **Problem:** An LVDT has the following specifications: $V_p = 5 \ \text{V}$ (peak), $f = 5 \ \text{kHz}$, $Z_p = 500 \ \Omega$, coupling constant $k = 1.5 \ \mu\text{H/mm}$. The core is displaced by $x = 4 \ \text{mm}$ from the null. > **Find:** (a) Peak output voltage, (b) Sensitivity, (c) RMS output. **(a) Peak output voltage:** $$\omega = 2\pi f = 2\pi \times 5000 = 31415.93 \ \text{rad/s}$$ $$e_{o,\text{peak}} = \dfrac{2 k V_p \omega}{Z_p} \cdot \vert x \vert$$ Substituting: $$e_{o,\text{peak}} = \dfrac{2 \times (1.5 \times 10^{-6}) \times 5 \times 31415.93}{500} \times 4$$ $$e_{o,\text{peak}} = \dfrac{2 \times 1.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 157079.6}{500} \times 4$$ $$e_{o,\text{peak}} = \dfrac{0.4712}{500} \times 4 = 0.000942 \times 4 = 0.00377 \ \text{V} \approx 3.77 \ \text{mV}$$ **(b) Sensitivity:** $$S = \dfrac{2 k V_p \omega}{Z_p \, \sqrt{2}} = \dfrac{0.4712}{500 \times 1.4142} = 6.66 \times 10^{-4} \ \text{V/mm} = 0.666 \ \text{mV/mm}$$ **(c) RMS output:** $$E_{o,\text{rms}} = S \times \vert x \vert = 0.666 \ \text{mV/mm} \times 4 \ \text{mm} = 2.665 \ \text{mV}$$ > **Self-Check:** The RMS value must satisfy $E_{o,\text{rms}} = e_{o,\text{peak}} / \sqrt{2} = 3.77 / 1.4142 = 2.666 \ \text{mV}$. ✓ Matches. # 📙 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 LVDT Construction — Block-Level Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR A[AC Excitation Source
1 kHz to 20 kHz] -->|V_p sin omega t| B(Primary Coil P
N_p turns) B --> C{Magnetic Core
Ferromagnetic Armature} C -->|Flux Phi 1| D[Secondary Coil S1

No Phase Information] D[Core Displaced Toward S1] -->|x positive| E[M1 greater than M2] E --> F[Output e_o IN PHASE with Vp
