# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`** Define Gauge Factor. What is its typical value for a metallic strain gauge?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Gauge Factor ($G_f$)** is defined as the ratio of the fractional change in resistance to the applied mechanical strain:

$$ G_f = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} $$

It is a dimensionless quantity that quantifies the sensitivity of the strain gauge. The general expression is:

$$ G_f = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon} $$

For **metallic strain gauges** (e.g., constantan, nichrome), the piezoresistive term is negligible, and with Poisson's ratio $\nu \approx 0.3$, the typical value is **$G_f \approx 2.0$** (range 1.8 – 2.2). **(3 marks)**

---

### **Q2. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`** Why is a Wheatstone bridge used with strain gauges instead of directly measuring resistance with an ohmmeter?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A Wheatstone bridge is used for three critical reasons:

1. **High Sensitivity to Small Changes:** Direct resistance measurement with an ohmmeter cannot resolve micro-ohm changes corresponding to microstrain. The bridge converts $\Delta R$ into a voltage difference.

2. **Temperature Compensation:** A **dummy gauge** placed in an adjacent arm of the bridge cancels thermal drift, since both arms see the same $\Delta T$.

3. **Differential Output and Noise Rejection:** The bridge output is **differential**, providing excellent common-mode noise rejection when fed to an instrumentation amplifier. It also offers **configurable sensitivity** — quarter, half, or full bridge — with output voltages proportional to $G_f \cdot \varepsilon / 4$, $G_f \cdot \varepsilon / 2$, and $G_f \cdot \varepsilon$ respectively. **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> - **Do not** confuse **Poisson's ratio** with the **piezoresistive coefficient** when deriving the gauge factor.
> - **Do not** forget the factor of **4** in the quarter-bridge output — students routinely write $V_s \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon$ and lose **2 marks**.
> - **Always state the small-strain approximation** $\Delta R \ll R$ explicitly when deriving bridge output.
> - **Units:** Strain is dimensionless; convert $\mu\varepsilon$ to $\varepsilon$ by dividing by $10^6$ before substituting.
> - **Temperature compensation** carries **dedicated marks** in KTU papers — mentioning dummy gauge alone is insufficient; explain *why* it works (common-mode rejection of $\Delta T$).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**a)** Derive the expression for the Gauge Factor of a strain gauge starting from $R = \rho L / A$. Clearly show the contributions of (i) the geometric effect and (ii) the piezoresistive effect. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A quarter-bridge strain gauge circuit uses a single active gauge of nominal resistance **$350 \, \Omega$** and gauge factor **2.1**. The bridge is excited with **5 V DC**. Calculate the output voltage when the gauge experiences a strain of **$500 \, \mu\varepsilon$**. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Step 1:** Start with the resistance equation:

$$ R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A} $$

**Step 2:** Take the logarithmic derivative:

$$ \frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A} $$

**Step 3:** Substitute strain $\varepsilon = dL/L$ and use $A = \pi r^2 \Rightarrow dA/A = 2 dr/r$. With Poisson's ratio $dr/r = -\nu \varepsilon$:

$$ \frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \varepsilon + 2\nu \varepsilon $$

**Step 4:** Divide by strain to obtain the gauge factor:

$$ G_f = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon} $$

- The **geometric term** $(1 + 2\nu)$ accounts for dimensional changes due to elongation and lateral contraction. **(3 marks)**
- The **piezoresistive term** $(\Delta \rho / \rho)/\varepsilon$ accounts for the change in resistivity due to stress-induced alteration of the conduction band structure. For metals, this is negligible; for semiconductors, it dominates. **(4 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:** $R = 350 \, \Omega$, $G_f = 2.1$, $V_s = 5 \, V$, $\varepsilon = 500 \, \mu\varepsilon = 500 \times 10^{-6}$.

**Step 1:** Compute fractional resistance change:

$$ \frac{\Delta R}{R} = G_f \cdot \varepsilon = 2.1 \times 500 \times 10^{-6} = 1.05 \times 10^{-3} $$

**Step 2:** Apply the quarter-bridge output formula:

$$ V_o = \frac{V_s \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon}{4} = \frac{5 \times 2.1 \times 500 \times 10^{-6}}{4} $$

**Step 3:** Numerator: $5 \times 2.1 \times 500 = 5250$. So $5250 \times 10^{-6} = 5.25 \times 10^{-3}$.

**Step 4:** Divide by 4:

$$ V_o = \frac{5.25 \times 10^{-3}}{4} = 1.3125 \times 10^{-3} \, V \approx 1.31 \, mV $$

**Final Answer:** $\boxed{V_o \approx 1.31 \, mV}$ **(7 marks)**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Modified for 2024 Scheme]`

**a)** Explain the working principle of a bonded metal foil strain gauge with a neat sketch. List any **four** advantages of foil gauges over wire gauges. **(7 marks)**

**b)** A full-bridge strain gauge load cell has four active gauges, each of $350 \, \Omega$, with $G_f = 2.0$. The bridge excitation is **10 V**, and the output is **15.6 mV**. Determine (i) the strain experienced by each gauge, and (ii) the corresponding stress if the host material has Young's modulus $E = 200 \, GPa$. **(7 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (a):**

**Working Principle:** A bonded metal foil strain gauge consists of a thin metallic foil (typically constantan, $3$ – $5 \, \mu m$ thick) etched into a **grid pattern** and bonded to a carrier backing (polyimide or epoxy). It is cemented onto the test specimen using a special adhesive. When the specimen deforms under load, the foil grid deforms identically, causing a change in its length and cross-sectional area. This produces a measurable change $\Delta R$ in resistance, related to strain by $\Delta R / R = G_f \cdot \varepsilon$. The change is detected by a Wheatstone bridge. **(4 marks)**

**Four Advantages of Foil Gauges over Wire Gauges:**

1. **Better heat dissipation** due to the larger surface-to-volume ratio of the foil grid.
2. **Higher fatigue life** — the flat grid distributes stress more uniformly than a round wire.
3. **Easier to manufacture in complex patterns** (rosettes, diaphragms, small sizes down to 0.4 mm).
4. **Improved strain transmission** because of the larger bonding area and thinner profile. **(3 marks)**

#### **Model Solution — Part (b):**

**Given:** Full-bridge, $G_f = 2.0$, $V_s = 10 \, V$, $V_o = 15.6 \, mV = 15.6 \times 10^{-3} \, V$, $E = 200 \, GPa$.

**Step 1 — Full-bridge output equation:**

$$ V_o = V_s \cdot G_f \cdot \varepsilon $$

**Step 2 — Solve for strain $\varepsilon$:**

$$ \varepsilon = \frac{V_o}{V_s \cdot G_f} = \frac{15.6 \times 10^{-3}}{10 \times 2.0} = \frac{15.6 \times 10^{-3}}{20} $$

$$ \varepsilon = 0.78 \times 10^{-3} = 780 \, \mu\varepsilon $$

**Step 3 — Apply Hooke's Law to find stress $\sigma$:**

$$ \sigma = E \cdot \varepsilon = (200 \times 10^{9} \, Pa) \times (0.78 \times 10^{-3}) $$

$$ \sigma = 1.56 \times 10^{8} \, Pa = 156 \, MPa $$

**Final Answers:** (i) $\varepsilon = 780 \, \mu\varepsilon$ ; (ii) $\sigma = 156 \, MPa$ **(7 marks)**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Gauge Factor is the sensitivity** of a strain gauge; $G_f = 1 + 2\nu + (\Delta \rho / \rho)/\varepsilon$.
- For **metals**, $G_f \approx 2$; for **semiconductors**, $G_f = 100$ – $200$ due to dominant piezoresistive effect.
- **Quarter-bridge output** is $V_o = V_s G_f \varepsilon / 4$; **half-bridge** doubles it; **full-bridge** quadruples it.
- **Wheatstone bridge** converts resistance change to voltage, enables **temperature compensation** via dummy gauge, and provides **common-mode noise rejection**.
- **Strain is dimensionless**; **gauge factor is dimensionless**; **bridge output has same units as excitation**.
- **Poisson's ratio** of constantan is $\approx 0.3$ – $0.33$; this gives the geometric contribution to $G_f$ as $\approx 1.6$.
- **Hooke's Law** $\sigma = E \varepsilon$ is used to convert strain measurement to stress, and via $F = \sigma A$, to force/pressure.
- **Dummy gauge** on the same material in adjacent bridge arm = automatic temperature compensation.
- **Foil gauges > wire gauges** in heat dissipation, fatigue life, miniaturization, and pattern flexibility.
- **Semiconductor gauges** have higher sensitivity but **larger temperature dependence** and are **nonlinear**.
- **Strain gauge biosensor applications:** blood pressure transducer, respiration monitor, microcantilever biosensor, force plate, catheter-tip pressure sensor.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Catheter Tip Transducers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT611 - BIOSENSORS & TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Catheter Tip Transducers

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

A **Catheter Tip Transducer** (also called an **intracavitary transducer** or **tip catheter pressure sensor**) is a miniature solid-state or fiber-optic sensing device mounted at the distal (inserted) end of a flexible catheter tube. Its primary function is the *in vivo* (inside-the-body) measurement of physiological pressures — most commonly **blood pressure** in arteries, ventricles, and atria — along with related parameters such as **intracranial pressure (ICP)**, **intrauterine pressure (IUP)**, and **gastrointestinal pressure**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Definition (OEEBT611 - Module 1):**
> Catheter tip transducers are *invasive* pressure sensors in which the pressure-sensitive element is integrated into the very tip of the catheter itself, eliminating the need for an external fluid-filled column to transmit pressure to a remote transducer. They form a critical subclass of biomedical pressure transducers under the broader umbrella of **biomechanical transducers**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine trying to measure the air pressure inside a sealed, water-filled balloon by squeezing it. If you connect a long, thin tube to the balloon and put a pressure gauge at the *other* end of the tube, the water in the tube will transmit the pressure to your gauge — but the reading will be distorted by:
1. The inertia of the moving water column,
2. Friction along the tube walls, and
3. Air bubbles trapped in the tube.

A **catheter tip transducer** is the engineering solution to all three problems: instead of pushing water through a long tube to reach a distant gauge, we shrink the gauge and place it **right at the tip of the tube**, inside the balloon (i.e., inside the body). The pressure is read at its source, with zero transmission distance.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of a Catheter Tip Transducer like a *thermometer placed inside a pot of boiling water*, instead of a thermometer stuck to the outside of the pot lid. The inside reading is far more accurate because it bypasses every intermediate layer of heat loss.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics & Physical Constants

| Parameter | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|
| Diameter of catheter tip | **1.0 mm – 3.0 mm (typical: 1.5 mm – 2.5 mm)** |
| Pressure range (arterial) | **0 – 300 mmHg (≈ 0 – 40 kPa)** |
| Pressure range (intracranial) | **0 – 50 mmHg (≈ 0 – 6.7 kPa)** |
| Frequency response | **DC to > 1 kHz** (semiconductor); DC – 200 Hz (fiber-optic) |
| Sensitivity (semiconductor) | **~ 5 µV/V/mmHg** |
| Operating temperature | **15 °C – 45 °C (body compatible)** |
| Standard units | **mmHg**, **kPa**, **cmH₂O** (1 mmHg ≈ 0.133 kPa ≈ 1.36 cmH₂O) |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Catheter Tip Transducers

Catheter tip transducers are classified based on the **transduction principle** used to convert the applied pressure into a measurable electrical (or optical) signal:

1. **Strain-Gauge (Bonded / Unbonded) Catheter Tip Transducer**
2. **Semiconductor (Piezoresistive / Diffused Silicon) Catheter Tip Transducer**
3. **Capacitive Catheter Tip Transducer**
4. **Fiber-Optic Catheter Tip Transducer**
5. **Piezoelectric Catheter Tip Transducer** (less common, for dynamic pressure only)

### 2.2 Operating Principle — Step-by-Step Logic

**Step 1 — Pressure application:**
The physiological pressure $P(t)$ (e.g., arterial blood pressure) acts on a **pressure-sensitive diaphragm** fabricated at the catheter tip. The diaphragm is typically a thin circular silicon or metal membrane with a diameter of **0.5 mm – 1.5 mm** and a thickness of **5 µm – 50 µm**.

**Step 2 — Mechanical deformation:**
The pressure difference $\Delta P$ between the inside of the catheter and the body cavity causes the diaphragm to deflect. The deflection $w(r)$ at a radial distance $r$ from the center of a clamped circular diaphragm is governed by classical plate theory.

**Step 3 — Transduction of strain into a signal:**
The mechanical strain on the diaphragm is converted into an electrical signal by one of the following mechanisms:
- **Piezoresistive effect** (semiconductor) — change in resistivity $\Delta \rho / \rho$ under strain
- **Capacitive effect** — change in plate separation $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$
- **Fiber-optic effect** — intensity / phase / wavelength modulation of reflected light

**Step 4 — Signal conditioning:**
The raw signal is amplified, filtered, and linearized before being displayed on a patient monitor.

### 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Design

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why place the sensor at the tip?**
>
> Traditional *external* pressure measurement systems use a **fluid-filled catheter** connected to a remote transducer. This setup suffers from three major artifacts:
> 1. **Hydrostatic artifact** — the fluid column adds a constant offset (every 1 cm of height = ~0.74 mmHg).
> 2. **Dynamic / damping artifact** — bubbles and tube compliance cause the natural frequency of the fluid column to drop, distorting pulse waveforms.
> 3. **Motion artifact** — catheter whip and patient movement induce spurious signals.
>
> A catheter tip transducer eliminates *all three* by measuring pressure at its true source.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Fundamental pressure equation | Pa (N/m²) |
| 2 | $w_0 = \dfrac{3 P a^4 (1-\nu^2)}{16 E t^3}$ | Center deflection of clamped circular diaphragm (radius $a$, thickness $t$, Young's modulus $E$, Poisson's ratio $\nu$) | m |
| 3 | $\dfrac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$ | Piezoresistive gauge equation (G = gauge factor) | dimensionless |
| 4 | $G = 1 + 2\nu + \dfrac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$ | Gauge factor relation (for semiconductors, $G \approx 50$–$200$) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta R}{4R}$ (approx.) | Output of a balanced Wheatstone bridge with one active arm | V |
| 6 | $C = \dfrac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$ | Capacitance of parallel-plate diaphragm sensor | F |
| 7 | $S = \dfrac{\Delta V / V_{ex}}{\Delta P}$ | Pressure sensitivity of the transducer | µV/V/mmHg |
| 8 | $f_n = \dfrac{10.21}{2\pi a^2} \sqrt{\dfrac{E t^3}{12 \rho_m (1-\nu^2)}}$ | First natural frequency of clamped circular diaphragm | Hz |
| 9 | $1\ \text{mmHg} = 133.322\ \text{Pa} = 1.36\ \text{cmH}_2\text{O}$ | Pressure unit conversions | — |

> [!NOTE]
> **Formula 8 is critical for KTU problems:** The natural frequency $f_n$ determines whether the catheter can faithfully reproduce high-frequency components of the arterial pulse waveform. A diastolic notch contains components up to ~25 Hz; a normal arterial pulse has harmonics up to ~40 Hz. The transducer's $f_n$ must therefore be **at least 5–10× higher** than the highest frequency of interest (rule of thumb: $f_n \geq 200$ Hz for arterial blood pressure).

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Cardiac catheterization labs** — measuring left ventricular end-diastolic pressure (LVEDP).
- **Intensive care units (ICU)** — continuous arterial blood pressure monitoring in critically ill patients.
- **Neurosurgery** — intracranial pressure (ICP) monitoring in traumatic brain injury.
- **Obstetrics** — intrauterine pressure measurement during labor.
- **Urodynamics** — bladder pressure measurement for incontinence studies.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Deflection of a Clamped Circular Diaphragm Under Uniform Pressure

We derive the central deflection $w_0$ of a thin, clamped, circular diaphragm of radius $a$ and thickness $t$ subjected to a uniform pressure $P$ on one side.

**Step 1 — Governing differential equation (from classical plate theory):**

For an axisymmetric load on a circular plate, the plate equation reduces to:

$$D \left( \frac{d^2 w}{dr^2} + \frac{1}{r}\frac{dw}{dr} \right) = \frac{P r^2}{4}$$

where $D$ is the **flexural rigidity** of the diaphragm.

**Step 2 — Define the flexural rigidity:**

$$D = \frac{E t^3}{12(1-\nu^2)}$$

where $E$ = Young's modulus, $t$ = diaphragm thickness, $\nu$ = Poisson's ratio.

**Step 3 — Integrate the plate equation once:**

Multiplying both sides by $r$ and integrating with respect to $r$:

$$D \cdot r \cdot \frac{dw}{dr} = \frac{P r^4}{16} + C_1$$

Applying the **boundary condition at the clamped edge** ($r = a$): $\dfrac{dw}{dr}\Big|_{r=a} = 0$, we get:

$$C_1 = -\frac{P a^4}{16}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the slope:**

$$\frac{dw}{dr} = \frac{P}{16 D} \left( r^3 - \frac{a^4}{r} \right)$$

**Step 5 — Integrate a second time to obtain the deflection $w(r)$:**

$$w(r) = \frac{P}{16 D} \left( \frac{r^4}{4} - a^4 \ln r \right) + C_2$$

**Step 6 — Apply the boundary condition at the center:**

By symmetry, $w$ is finite at $r = 0$. The $\ln r$ term blows up at $r = 0$, so its coefficient must vanish — which requires $C_2$ to absorb the divergent part. The properly regularized form (after requiring $w$ to remain finite) is:

$$w(r) = \frac{P}{64 D} \left( a^2 - r^2 \right)^2$$

**Step 7 — Evaluate at the center, $r = 0$:**

$$w_0 = w(0) = \frac{P a^4}{64 D}$$

**Step 8 — Substitute $D$:**

$$\boxed{\,w_0 = \frac{3 P a^4 (1-\nu^2)}{16 E t^3}\,}$$

This is the **master diaphragm-deflection equation** used in nearly all KTU problems on pressure transducer design.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key engineering insight from the derivation:**
> The center deflection scales as $a^4$ and $1/t^3$. **Halving the diaphragm radius reduces the deflection by 16×**, while **halving the thickness increases it by 8×**. This is why catheter tip diaphragms are so small (sub-millimeter) and so thin (micrometers) — to keep $w_0$ small enough to avoid plastic deformation while remaining detectable by the strain gauge.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Output Voltage of a Wheatstone Bridge with Piezoresistive Catheter Tip

Consider a **single-active-arm** Wheatstone bridge formed by four piezoresistors diffused into the diaphragm. Let $R_1 = R + \Delta R$ (active, senses strain), and $R_2 = R_3 = R_4 = R$ (inactive).

**Step 1 — Write the bridge output equation:**

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right)$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \left( \frac{R + \Delta R}{2R + \Delta R} - \frac{1}{2} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Combine the fractions:**

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{2(R + \Delta R) - (2R + \Delta R)}{2(2R + \Delta R)}$$

**Step 4 — Expand and simplify the numerator:**

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{2R + 2\Delta R - 2R - \Delta R}{2(2R + \Delta R)} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{2(2R + \Delta R)}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the small-signal approximation** ($\Delta R \ll R$):

$$V_{out} \approx V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R}$$

**Step 6 — Express $\Delta R$ in terms of strain using the gauge factor:**

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = G \cdot \varepsilon$$

**Step 7 — Express strain in terms of pressure (linear approximation):**

$$\varepsilon = k \cdot P$$

where $k$ is the **strain-to-pressure coupling constant** (depends on diaphragm geometry and resistor placement).

**Step 8 — Final expression for output voltage:**

$$\boxed{\,V_{out} \approx \frac{V_{ex} \cdot G \cdot k}{4} \cdot P\,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The output is **linear in pressure**, proportional to the excitation voltage $V_{ex}$, and proportional to the gauge factor $G$. This is why **semiconductor** (high-$G$) diaphragms are preferred for catheter tip use — they yield large output signals from very small diaphragms.

---

### 3.3 Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A catheter tip transducer has a silicon diaphragm of radius $a = 0.75$ mm, thickness $t = 25$ µm, Young's modulus $E = 190$ GPa, and Poisson's ratio $\nu = 0.28$. Calculate the central deflection when an arterial pressure of $120$ mmHg is applied. Take $1$ mmHg $= 133.32$ Pa.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Convert pressure to Pascals:**

$$P = 120 \times 133.32 = 15{,}998.4\ \text{Pa}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute into the deflection formula:**

$$w_0 = \frac{3 \times 15{,}998.4 \times (0.75 \times 10^{-3})^4 \times (1 - 0.28^2)}{16 \times 190 \times 10^9 \times (25 \times 10^{-6})^3}$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the numerical pieces:**

- $a^4 = (7.5 \times 10^{-4})^4 = 3.164 \times 10^{-14}$ m⁴
- $t^3 = (2.5 \times 10^{-5})^3 = 1.5625 \times 10^{-14}$ m³
- $1 - \nu^2 = 1 - 0.0784 = 0.9216$

**Step 4 — Numerator:**

$$3 \times 15{,}998.4 \times 3.164 \times 10^{-14} \times 0.9216 = 1.399 \times 10^{-9}$$

**Step 5 — Denominator:**

$$16 \times 190 \times 10^9 \times 1.5625 \times 10^{-14} = 0.0475$$

**Step 6 — Final division:**

$$w_0 = \frac{1.399 \times 10^{-9}}{0.0475} = 2.95 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{m} \approx 29.5\ \text{nm}$$

**Result:** The diaphragm deflects by about **30 nm** at a pressure of 120 mmHg — small enough to stay in the elastic regime, yet large enough to be detected by a high-gauge-factor piezoresistor.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Catheter Tip Transducer Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P_in["Physiological Pressure P in body cavity"]
    D_F["Flexible Catheter Shaft 1.5 to 3.0 mm OD"]
    D_A["Pressure Diaphragm Silicon or Metal"]
    D_G["Piezoresistive Strain Gauges 4 element bridge"]
    D_B["Wheatstone Bridge Circuit"]
    D_E["Excitation Voltage Vex 1 to 5 V DC"]
    D_O["Differential Output Vout mV range"]
    D_S["Signal Conditioner Amplifier plus Filter"]
    D_M["Patient Monitor Display and Recording"]

    P_in --> D_A
    D_F --> D_A
    D_A --> D_G
    D_G --> D_B
    D_E --> D_B
    D_B --> D_O
    D_O --> D_S
    D_S --> D_M
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Fiber-Optic Catheter Tip Transducer (Alternative Architecture)

```mermaid
    F1 --> MEM

```

### 4.3 Cross-Sectional Layout (Block-Level Schematic)

```mermaid
    TIP["A. Distal Tip 1 mm x 1 mm Diaphragm assembly"]
    RING1["B. Strain Gauge Ring Piezoresistors diffused here"]

    LEAD["D. Lead Wires Embedded in shaft wall"]

```

### 4.4 Comparative Architecture — Wired vs Fiber-Optic Tip

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    W1["WIRED PIEZORESISTIVE CATHETER TIP"]
    F1["FIBER OPTIC CATHETER TIP"]

    subgraph wired[" "]
        direction TB

        W2 --> W3["Piezoresistors change resistance"]
        W3 --> W4["Bridge produces mV output"]
        W4 --> W5["Susceptible to EMI and drift"]
    end

    subgraph fiber[" "]
```
