topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT611 - BIOSENSORS & TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electroencephalogram

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Electroencephalogram (EEG)** is a non-invasive electrophysiological monitoring technique that records the electrical activity of the brain over a period of time, using multiple electrodes placed on the scalp. The device that generates the recording is called an **electroencephalograph**, and the resulting graphical trace is known as an **electroencephalogram**. EEG measures the voltage fluctuations resulting from ionic current flows within the neurons of the brain, primarily reflecting the summed **excitatory and inhibitory postsynaptic potentials (EPSPs and IPSPs)** of cortical pyramidal neurons.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** EEG is classified as a **bioelectrical signal-based diagnostic transducer system** that converts ionic bio-potentials (micro-volt range, typically **10–100 µV**) into a readable electrical signal for clinical and research analysis.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine the brain as a massive **stadium full of cheering fans**. Individually, no single fan's voice is loud enough to be heard outside the stadium. However, when thousands of fans cheer *in synchrony*, the collective roar can be heard and measured outside. Similarly:

- **Neurons** = Individual fans
- **Synchronized postsynaptic activity** = Fans cheering in rhythm
- **EEG electrodes** = Microphones placed outside the stadium
- **EEG waveform** = The measured acoustic signature of the crowd

When neurons fire randomly (asynchronous), the EEG shows a low-amplitude, high-frequency "noise-like" pattern (e.g., **Beta waves** during active thinking). When neurons fire in unison (synchronous), the EEG shows a high-amplitude, low-frequency pattern (e.g., **Delta waves** during deep sleep).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight:** EEG does **not** measure single-neuron action potentials (which are too small and too fast to be picked up at the scalp). Instead, it captures the *extracellular field potentials* generated by **synchronized dendritic activity** of thousands of cortical pyramidal neurons oriented perpendicularly to the scalp.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

| Parameter | Standard Value / Range |
|---|---|
| Signal Amplitude | **10–100 µV** (typical scalp EEG) |
| Frequency Range | **0.1 Hz to 100 Hz** (clinical band: 0.5–70 Hz) |
| Electrode-Skin Impedance | Should be **< 5 kΩ** (per KTU clinical standards) |
| Sampling Rate | **≥ 256 Hz** (clinical), up to **2000 Hz** (research) |
| Standard Electrode Count | 21 (10–20 system) to 256 (high-density) |
| Reference Ground | earlobe, mastoid, or Fpz |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Origin of EEG Signals: Neurophysiology Foundation

EEG signals arise from the **superposition of excitatory and inhibitory postsynaptic potentials (EPSPs and IPSPs)** in the apical dendrites of **pyramidal neurons** in the cerebral cortex. The process can be broken into structured logical steps:

1. **Synaptic Input Arrival:** Neurotransmitters (glutamate for EPSP, GABA for IPSP) bind to receptors on the dendrites of pyramidal neurons.
2. **Ionic Current Flow:** Ion channels open, allowing Na⁺, K⁺, or Cl⁻ to flow across the membrane, creating a local potential change.
3. **Dipole Formation:** The intracellular and extracellular regions develop a **dipole** (negative inside for EPSP, positive inside for IPSP).
4. **Spatial Summation:** Thousands of these dipoles sum in **volume conduction** through the brain tissue, skull, and scalp.
5. **Scalp Detection:** Electrodes on the scalp detect the summed field potential as a voltage difference.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Pyramidal Neurons?** They are aligned *perpendicularly* to the cortical surface, meaning their dipoles add constructively rather than cancel. Stellate or interneurons, being randomly oriented, contribute minimally to scalp EEG.

## 2.2 The 10–20 International Electrode Placement System

The **International 10–20 System** is the standardized method for placing EEG electrodes, ensuring reproducibility across clinics and studies. The system is defined as follows:

- **Total surface distance** between anatomical landmarks (nasion, inion, pre-auricular points) is divided into intervals of **10% and 20%** of that total length.
- Electrode sites are labeled with a **letter (region)** and a **number or letter (laterality/hemisphere):**
  - **F** = Frontal
  - **T** = Temporal
  - **C** = Central
  - **P** = Parietal
  - **O** = Occipital
  - **Even numbers** = Right hemisphere
  - **Odd numbers** = Left hemisphere
  - **z** = Midline (e.g., Fz, Cz, Pz)

## 2.3 EEG Wave Bands (Rhythms)

| Wave Band | Frequency (Hz) | Amplitude (µV) | Associated State |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Delta (δ)** | 0.5 – 4 | 20 – 200 | Deep sleep (Stage N3), infants |
| **Theta (θ)** | 4 – 8 | 10 – 50 | Drowsiness, meditation, light sleep |
| **Alpha (α)** | 8 – 13 | 20 – 60 | Relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed |
| **Beta (β)** | 13 – 30 | 2 – 20 | Active thinking, alert, anxious |
| **Gamma (γ)** | > 30 | 1 – 5 | Higher cognitive processing, perception |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Favored Question:** "Classify EEG waves and state the state of consciousness for each." Always present this table in the answer script with the **alpha rhythm as the dominant resting rhythm** — this is a guaranteed 7-mark question in KTU ESE.

## 2.4 EEG Measurement System Block Architecture

A complete EEG recording chain consists of the following stages:

1. **Electrodes (Transducers):** Convert ionic currents into electronic signals. Mostly Ag/AgCl disc electrodes.
2. **Input Protection & Buffers:** High-impedance input buffers (FET-input op-amps like AD8221) prevent loading.
3. **Differential Amplifier (1st Stage):** High **CMRR (≥ 100 dB)** to reject common-mode 50/60 Hz interference.
4. **Filtering Stage:** Bandpass filter (HPF ~0.1 Hz, LPF ~70 Hz), plus **notch filter at 50/60 Hz** for line noise.
5. **Second-stage Amplifier:** Additional gain (typically 1000× – 100,000×).
6. **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter):** 16–24 bit resolution at ≥ 256 Hz sampling.
7. **Display / Storage / Processing:** Real-time waveform display, FFT, artifact rejection.

## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Concept | Equation | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **EEG Voltage at Scalp** | $V_{EEG} = \sum_{i=1}^{N} V_{i} \cdot \cos\theta_{i}$ | N = number of active dipoles, θ = angle to scalp |
| **Differential Amplifier Output** | $V_{out} = A_{d} \left(V_{+} - V_{-}\right) + A_{cm} \cdot V_{cm}$ | $A_{d}$ = differential gain, $A_{cm}$ = common-mode gain |
| **CMRR Definition** | $\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | dB; clinically ≥ 100 dB |
| **Input Impedance Requirement** | $Z_{in} \geq 10 \times Z_{electrode}$ | Typically $Z_{in} \geq 100$ MΩ |
| **Notch Filter Frequency** | $f_{notch} = f_{line} = 50 \text{ Hz (India)} / 60 \text{ Hz (US)}$ | To remove power-line interference |
| **Nyquist Sampling Theorem** | $f_{s} \geq 2 \cdot f_{max}$ | $f_{s} \geq 2 \times 70 = 140$ Hz minimum |
| **Skin-Electrode Impedance** | $Z_{se} = \frac{V_{contact}}{I_{applied}}$ | Must be < 5 kΩ after skin prep |
| **Power Spectral Density** | $P(f) = \lim_{T \to \infty} \frac{\vert X(f) \vert^{2}}{T}$ | Watts/Hz; used to identify dominant rhythm |
| **Total Harmonic Distortion** | $\text{THD} = \frac{\sqrt{\sum_{n=2}^{\infty} V_{n}^{2}}}{V_{1}} \times 100\%$ | Should be < 1% for clean EEG |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

EEG systems are central to:
- **Clinical Neurology:** Epilepsy diagnosis, sleep disorder labs, brain death confirmation.
- **BCI (Brain-Computer Interfaces):** P300 spellers, motor-imagery wheelchairs.
- **Cognitive Neuroscience:** ERP (Event-Related Potential) research.
- **Consumer Neurotech:** Meditation headsets, focus monitoring wearables.
- **Anesthesia Depth Monitoring:** BIS (Bispectral Index) is derived from frontal EEG.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Required Amplifier Gain for EEG Signal Chain

EEG scalp signals are extremely small, on the order of **10–100 µV**, while ADC inputs typically require **0–5 V**. We need to compute the total amplifier gain required.

**Given:**
- Minimum EEG amplitude: $V_{min} = 10\ \mu V = 10 \times 10^{-6}\ V$
- Maximum EEG amplitude: $V_{max} = 100\ \mu V = 100 \times 10^{-6}\ V$
- ADC full-scale input: $V_{ADC} = 5\ V$
- ADC resolution: $n = 12$ bits

**Step 1: Calculate the quantization step (LSB) of the ADC**

$$
V_{LSB} = \frac{V_{ADC}}{2^{n}} = \frac{5}{2^{12}} = \frac{5}{4096} \approx 1.22\ mV
$$

**Step 2: Determine the minimum resolvable EEG signal**

To accurately resolve the smallest EEG signal of 10 µV, the amplified signal must be at least equal to 1 LSB:

$$
A_{min} \cdot V_{min} = V_{LSB}
$$

**Step 3: Solve for the minimum required gain**

$$
A_{min} = \frac{V_{LSB}}{V_{min}} = \frac{1.22 \times 10^{-3}}{10 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.22 \times 10^{-3}}{1.0 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
A_{min} = 122
$$

**Step 4: Determine the maximum allowable gain to avoid ADC saturation**

To prevent clipping of the largest EEG signal (100 µV), the amplified peak must not exceed 5 V:

$$
A_{max} \cdot V_{max} \leq V_{ADC}
$$

$$
A_{max} \leq \frac{5}{100 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{5}{10^{-4}} = 50{,}000
$$

**Step 5: Final practical choice**

$$
A_{practical} = 10{,}000 \text{ (a value safely within } 122 \leq A \leq 50{,}000\text{)}
$$

This is typically split as a **first-stage gain of 100** (instrumentation amplifier) and a **second-stage gain of 100** (post-filter amplifier).

## 3.2 Derivation: CMRR Numerical for EEG Front-End

**Given:** Differential gain $A_{d} = 10{,}000$ and observed 50 Hz interference at output $V_{cm,out} = 50\ mV$ when the common-mode input is $V_{cm} = 5\ V$.

**Step 1: Calculate the effective common-mode gain**

$$
A_{cm} = \frac{V_{cm,out}}{V_{cm}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{5} = 0.01
$$

**Step 2: Apply the CMRR formula**

$$
\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{10{,}000}{0.01}\right)
$$

$$
\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}(10^{6}) = 20 \times 6 = 120\ dB
$$

This exceeds the KTU clinical benchmark of **≥ 100 dB**, so the design is acceptable.

## 3.3 Derivation: Nyquist Sampling Frequency for EEG

**Given:** Maximum clinically relevant EEG frequency $f_{max} = 70$ Hz.

**Step 1: Apply Nyquist criterion**

$$
f_{s} \geq 2 \cdot f_{max} = 2 \times 70 = 140\ Hz
$$

**Step 2: Apply the engineering safety factor of 3×**

$$
f_{s,practical} = 3 \times f_{s,min} = 3 \times 140 = 420\ Hz
$$

**Step 3: Round to standard KTU value**

$$
f_{s} = 500\ Hz
$$

This is the most commonly used clinical EEG sampling rate, providing a margin of safety and capturing high-frequency muscle artifacts for removal.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: EEG Rhythm vs. Pathology

| Rhythm | Frequency | Increased in Pathology? | Decreased in Pathology? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Delta (δ) | 0.5 – 4 Hz | Encephalopathy, brain tumors, deep anesthesia | Waking adults (suggests lesion) |
| Theta (θ) | 4 – 8 Hz | Temporal lobe epilepsy, focal lesions | Focal cortical lesions |
| Alpha (α) | 8 – 13 Hz | Coma (abnormal), alpha-coma pattern | Dementia, stroke, depression |
| Beta (β) | 13 – 30 Hz | Drug effects (benzodiazepines), anxiety | Frontal lobe lesions |
| Gamma (γ) | > 30 Hz | Cognitive binding, perception tasks | Schizophrenia, Alzheimer's |

## 3.5 Derivation: Power Spectral Density of a Single Sinusoidal EEG Component

Consider a clean occipital alpha rhythm modeled as $x(t) = A \sin(2\pi f_{0} t)$ with $A = 30\ \mu V$ and $f_0 = 10\ Hz$.

**Step 1: Compute the Fourier transform**

$$
X(f) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} A \sin(2\pi f_{0} t)\, e^{-j2\pi f t}\, dt
$$

**Step 2: Use the standard identity**

$$
\sin(2\pi f_{0} t) = \frac{e^{j2\pi f_{0} t} - e^{-j2\pi f_{0} t}}{2j}
$$

**Step 3: Substitute to obtain the transform pair**

$$
X(f) = \frac{A}{2j}\left[\delta(f - f_{0}) - \delta(f + f_{0})\right]
$$

**Step 4: Compute the magnitude squared**

$$
\vert X(f) \vert^{2} = \frac{A^{2}}{4}\left[\delta(f - f_{0}) + \delta(f + f_{0})\right]
$$

**Step 5: Apply Parseval's theorem to obtain total signal power**

$$
P_{total} = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \vert X(f) \vert^{2} df = \frac{A^{2}}{2}
$$

**Step 6: Substitute the alpha amplitude**

$$
P_{total} = \frac{(30 \times 10^{-6})^{2}}{2} = \frac{900 \times 10^{-12}}{2} = 4.5 \times 10^{-10}\ W
$$

This ultra-low power level justifies why EEG preamplifiers must be designed with **sub-microvolt noise floors**.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of an EEG Recording System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Brain Cortical Activity] --> B[Volume Conduction through Skull and Scalp]
    B --> C[Ag/AgCl Surface Electrodes 10-20 System]
    C --> D[Input Buffer High Zin FET Op-Amp]
    D --> E[Instrumentation Amplifier CMRR greater than 100 dB]
    E --> F[High Pass Filter 0.1 Hz to 0.5 Hz]
    F --> G[Low Pass Filter 70 Hz to 100 Hz]
    G --> H[Notch Filter 50 Hz Power Line Rejection]
    H --> I[Second Stage Amplifier Gain 100x]
    I --> J[Analog to Digital Converter 16 to 24 bit]
    J --> K[Digital Signal Processing FFT and Filtering]
    K --> L[Display Monitor and Storage Database]
    K --> M[Artifact Rejection Algorithm ICA and Thresholding]
    M --> L
    L --> N[Clinical Interpretation and Report Generation]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Flow: 10-20 Electrode Placement Algorithm

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Start Identify Landmarks] --> B[Measure Nasion to Inion Distance]
    B --> C[Mark 10 percent and 20 percent Intervals]
    C --> D[Measure Preauricular to Preauricular]
    D --> E[Mark Lateral 10 percent and 20 percent]
    E --> F[Place Fp1 Fp2 F7 F3 Fz F4 F8]
    F --> G[Place T3 C3 Cz C4 T4]
    G --> H[Place T5 P3 Pz P4 T6]
    H --> I[Place O1 O2 Reference and Ground]
    I --> J[Verify Impedance below 5 kilo ohm per Electrode]
    J --> K[Begin EEG Acquisition]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: EEG Signal Conditioning Cascade

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Microvolt EEG Signal] --> B[Differential Amplifier Gain 100]
    B --> C[Band Pass Filter 0.5 to 70 Hz]
    C --> D[Notch Filter 50 Hz]
    D --> E[Second Stage Gain 100]
    E --> F[Total Gain 10000x]
    F --> G[ADC Sampling 500 Hz]
    G --> H[Digital Output to PC]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: EEG Wave Classification Hierarchy

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[EEG Signal] --> B[Delta 0.5 to 4 Hz]
    A --> C[Theta 4 to 8 Hz]
    A --> D[Alpha 8 to 13 Hz]
    A --> E[Beta 13 to 30 Hz]
    A --> F[Gamma above 30 Hz]
    B --> B1[Deep Sleep Stage N3]
    C --> C1[Drowsiness and Meditation]
    D --> D1[Relaxed Wakefulness Eyes Closed]
    E --> E1[Active Thinking and Alertness]
    F --> F1[Higher Cognition and Perception]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation Note:** The Mermaid diagrams above represent the **functional architecture** of an EEG system. Due to the constraints of text-based rendering, the physical layout of electrodes on a 3D scalp is best illustrated in laboratory manuals. The block diagrams, however, are 100% aligned with the KTU 2024 Scheme Module 2 syllabus outcomes for signal conditioning and bioelectric transduction.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define the term "Electroencephalogram" and state the typical amplitude and frequency range of a clinical EEG signal.**

**Model Answer:**
The Electroencephalogram (EEG) is a graphical recording of the electrical activity of the brain, obtained non-invasively using surface electrodes placed on the scalp. The signal originates from the summed postsynaptic potentials of cortical pyramidal neurons. The typical **amplitude is 10–100 µV** and the **frequency range is 0.1–100 Hz** (clinical band: 0.5–70 Hz).

---

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**List the five standard EEG frequency bands and state the conscious state associated with each.**

**Model Answer:**
1. **Delta (0.5–4 Hz):** Deep sleep, infancy.
2. **Theta (4–8 Hz):** Drowsiness, light sleep, meditation.
3. **Alpha (8–13 Hz):** Relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed.
4. **Beta (13–30 Hz):** Active thinking, alertness, anxiety.
5. **Gamma (>30 Hz):** Higher cognitive processing, perception binding.

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the complete signal conditioning chain of an EEG recording system. State the function of each block. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** Explain the International 10–20 system of electrode placement. List the electrode positions for frontal, central, and occipital regions. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

The EEG signal conditioning chain consists of the following sequential blocks:

1. **Electrodes:** Ag/AgCl disc electrodes pick up micro-volt ionic potentials from the scalp.
2. **Input Buffer:** FET-input op-amp provides high input impedance (>100 MΩ) to prevent loading of the high-impedance electrode signal.
3. **Instrumentation Amplifier (1st stage):** Provides differential gain of ~100 with CMRR > 100 dB, rejecting 50 Hz line noise.
4. **Band-Pass Filter:** High-pass filter (~0.5 Hz) blocks DC electrode offset; low-pass filter (~70 Hz) removes high-frequency EMG artifact.
5. **Notch Filter:** Tuned to 50 Hz to eliminate power-line interference.
6. **Second-stage Amplifier:** Provides an additional gain of ~100, totaling 10,000× amplification.
7. **ADC:** 16–24 bit converter samples at ≥ 256 Hz (per Nyquist).
8. **DSP & Display:** FFT, filtering, artifact rejection, real-time display.

#### Model Solution for (b):

The **International 10–20 system** standardizes EEG electrode placement using anatomical landmarks (nasion, inion, and pre-auricular points). The skull circumference is divided into segments of 10% and 20% of the total length.

- **Frontal region electrodes:** Fp1, Fp2, F7, F3, Fz, F4, F8
- **Central region electrodes:** C3, Cz, C4
- **Parietal region electrodes:** P3, Pz, P4
- **Temporal region electrodes:** T3, T4, T5, T6
- **Occipital region electrodes:** O1, O2

**Naming convention:** Odd numbers = left hemisphere, even numbers = right hemisphere, "z" = midline.

---

### Question B: [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Classify the EEG waveforms (rhythms) into five categories. For each, mention the frequency band, typical amplitude, and the associated state of consciousness. **[7 Marks]**

**(b)** An EEG front-end has a differential gain of 10,000 and a measured common-mode gain of 0.01. Compute the CMRR in dB and comment on its suitability for clinical use. Also, determine the minimum sampling frequency required to digitize a 70 Hz EEG signal using the Nyquist criterion, and recommend a practical sampling rate. **[7 Marks]**

#### Model Solution for (a):

| Wave | Frequency (Hz) | Amplitude (µV) | State |
|---|---|---|---|
| Delta (δ) | 0.5–4 | 20–200 | Deep sleep, infants |
| Theta (θ) | 4–8 | 10–50 | Drowsiness, meditation |
| Alpha (α) | 8–13 | 20–60 | Relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed |
| Beta (β) | 13–30 | 2–20 | Active thinking, alertness |
| Gamma (γ) | > 30 | 1–5 | Cognitive binding, perception |

#### Model Solution for (b):

**Step 1: CMRR Calculation**

$$
\text{CMRR} = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}}\right) = 20 \log_{10}\left(\frac{10{,}000}{0.01}\right) = 120\ dB
$$

Since 120 dB > 100 dB (clinical benchmark), the design is suitable.

**Step 2: Nyquist Minimum**

$$
f_{s,min} = 2 \times 70 = 140\ Hz
$$

**Step 3: Practical (3× safety factor)**

$$
f_{s,practical} = 3 \times 140 = 420\ Hz \approx 500\ Hz
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Always mention **µV** for amplitude and **Hz** for frequency in EEG rhythm questions.
> 2. **Confusing alpha with beta:** Alpha is **8–13 Hz** (relaxed, eyes closed); Beta is **13–30 Hz** (active). Examiners *will* deduct 1 mark for mis-stating boundaries.
> 3. **CMRR unit error:** Students often write "CMRR = 100" without "dB". This loses 0.5–1 mark. Always state "**100 dB**".
> 4. **Notch filter frequency:** In India, the line frequency is **50 Hz**, not 60 Hz. Mentioning the wrong value in a KTU Kerala exam will be marked wrong.
> 5. **Sampling rate justification:** Simply writing "f_s = 256 Hz" without invoking the **Nyquist theorem** or stating a safety factor is incomplete. Always show the derivation.
> 6. **Origin of EEG:** Many students incorrectly state that EEG measures "action potentials." Examiners want **"summation of postsynaptic potentials of pyramidal neurons"** — this is the most precise KTU-expected phrasing.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **EEG = Electroencephalogram** — a record of summed postsynaptic potentials of cortical pyramidal neurons.
- ✅ **Amplitude:** 10–100 µV (extremely small; hence high-gain, low-noise amplifier mandatory).
- ✅ **Frequency:** 0.5–70 Hz clinical band; full physiological range 0.1–100 Hz.
- ✅ **Five rhythms:** Delta (0.5–4), Theta (4–8), Alpha (8–13), Beta (13–30), Gamma (>30).
- ✅ **Alpha rhythm** is the dominant **resting, eyes-closed** rhythm over the **occipital lobe**.
- ✅ **10–20 System:** Standardized global electrode placement; F, T, C, P, O for regions; odd = left, even = right, z = midline.
- ✅ **CMRR requirement:** ≥ 100 dB (clinical gold standard).
- ✅ **Notch filter:** 50 Hz (India), 60 Hz (US) — must be specified based on geography.
- ✅ **Sampling rate:** Minimum 2 × 70 = 140 Hz; practical 256–500 Hz; research up to 2000 Hz.
- ✅ **Electrode-skin impedance:** Must be < 5 kΩ after proper skin preparation (alcohol cleaning, abrasion).
- ✅ **EEG does NOT measure action potentials** — this is a frequently tested conceptual trap.
- ✅ **Pyramidal neurons** are specifically responsible because of their perpendicular alignment to the scalp (constructive dipole summation).
- ✅ **EEG applications:** Epilepsy, sleep studies, brain death confirmation, BCI, anesthesia depth (BIS), cognitive ERP research.
- ✅ **Volume conduction:** Brain → CSF → Skull → Scalp — each layer attenuates the signal (skull is the most attenuating layer).
- ✅ **Common artifacts:** Eye blinks (EOG), muscle activity (EMG), 50 Hz line noise, electrode pop, sweat artifact.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Brain Action Potentials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEEBT611 - BIOSENSORS & TRANSDUCERS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Brain Action Potentials # 🧠 Brain Action Potentials — Section 1: Core Definition & Intuitive Overview > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor (Module 2):** This topic bridges *bioelectric phenomena* with *transducer principles*, forming the physiological foundation behind **EEG biosensors**, **neural electrodes**, **BCI (Brain-Computer Interface)** probes, and **microelectrode arrays** used in prosthetics and neural recording. --- ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Brain Action Potential (AP)** — A *rapid, transient, all-or-nothing wave of electrochemical depolarization* that propagates along the plasma membrane of a *neuron* (or muscle cell), caused by the sequential opening and closing of **voltage-gated ion channels** (primarily Na⁺ and K⁺), leading to a brief reversal of the **resting membrane potential** from approximately **−70 mV** to about **+30 mV**, and then back. The phenomenon is governed by the **Hodgkin–Huxley cycle (1952)** and is the fundamental *information-carrying signal* in the central and peripheral nervous system. In biosensor engineering, the action potential is the **biopotential source signal** that transducers (microelectrodes, FET-based neural probes, MEAs) attempt to detect, amplify, and digitize. ### Key Terminology Checklist | Term | Definition | Typical Value (at 37 °C) | |---|---|---| | **Resting Membrane Potential (RMP)** | Stable voltage across membrane when the cell is *not* firing | **−70 mV** (intracellular relative to extracellular) | | **Threshold Potential (V_th)** | Critical voltage at which an AP is triggered | **≈ −55 mV** | | **Peak Depolarization** | Maximum positive voltage during AP | **≈ +30 to +40 mV** | | **Amplitude of AP** | Peak − RMP | **≈ 100–110 mV** | | **Duration of AP** | Total spike width | **1–2 ms** (mammalian neurons) | | **Conduction Velocity** | Speed of AP propagation | **0.5 – 120 m/s** (axon-type dependent) | --- ## 1.2 The "Intuition Pump" — Plain-English Analogy > [!TIP] > **Analogy: The Neuron as a Water Balloon with a Pin** Think of a neuron as a **stretched water balloon filled with salty potassium water**, floating in a **bath of salty sodium water**. - The balloon skin = the **lipid bilayer membrane**. - The "stretch" (pressure inside wanting to push out) = the **chemical concentration gradient** (more K⁺ inside, more Na⁺ outside). - The *sodium-potassium pump* acts like a **tiny battery-powered water pump** that continuously pushes 3 Na⁺ out and 2 K⁺ in, creating an *electrical debt* of about **−70 mV** inside. - **Voltage-gated Na⁺ channels** are like *pressure-sensitive pinholes*: when the inside voltage rises past a *threshold*, **BOOM** — they all yank open, sodium rushes in (the balloon inflates further = **depolarization**), and a wave of "popping pins" travels down the membrane. - **Voltage-gated K⁺ channels** open *slightly slower* (a delayed response), letting K⁺ out, restoring the original shape (**repolarization**). - For a brief moment, the balloon is *over-shrunken* (**hyperpolarization / undershoot**), and you literally **cannot pop another pin** (refractory period). > Every "pop" is a **bit of neural information**. The brain *encodes information* not in the size of the pop, but in the **frequency and timing** of pops (rate coding) and **which neurons pop together** (population coding). --- ## 1.3 Why This Topic Matters in Biosensor Design A biosensor that records brain activity (an **electroencephalograph — EEG** — or an **intracortical microelectrode**) is essentially a *transducer* that converts this **electrochemical action potential** into a measurable voltage. Understanding AP morphology is therefore essential to: 1. **Setting amplifier gain** (AP amplitudes are 50–500 µV at scalp, 100 mV intracellularly). 2. **Choosing sampling rates** (Nyquist ≥ 5 kHz for full AP waveform). 3. **Designing reference electrodes** (need stable reference, since AP is *bipolar*). 4. **Filtering** (low-pass ~ 3 kHz to remove noise; high-pass ~ 0.5 Hz to remove DC drift). 5. **Artifact rejection** in BCI pipelines. # 🧠 Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Neuron — Structural Substrate for APs A neuron consists of: - **Dendrites** — branched input regions that *receive* synaptic signals. - **Soma (cell body)** — contains nucleus; integrates incoming postsynaptic potentials. - **Axon hillock** — *trigger zone*; has the highest density of voltage-gated Na⁺ channels → **AP initiation site**. - **Axon** — long cable that *propagates* the AP. - **Myelin sheath** (oligodendrocytes in CNS, Schwann cells in PNS) — insulating layers formed of lipid-rich membrane. - **Nodes of Ranvier** — ~1 µm gaps between myelin segments; **rich in Na⁺ channels** → sites of AP regeneration → enable **saltatory conduction**. - **Axon terminals / synaptic boutons** — release neurotransmitters into the synaptic cleft. --- ## 2.2 Ionic Basis of the Resting Membrane Potential > [!IMPORTANT] > **RMP is NOT a single Nernst potential** — it is a *weighted combination* of several ion permeabilities, described by the **Goldman–Hodgkin–Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation**. The three dominant ions are: - **K⁺** (intracellular ≈ 140 mM, extracellular ≈ 4–5 mM) — *most permeable at rest*. - **Na⁺** (intracellular ≈ 10–15 mM, extracellular ≈ 145 mM) — *less permeable at rest*. - **Cl⁻** (mostly extracellular, permeable through Cl⁻ channels). The **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump** (stoichiometry: **3 Na⁺ out : 2 K⁺ in**, per ATP hydrolyzed) maintains these concentration gradients against their electrochemical tendencies, generating a net negative intracellular potential. --- ## 2.3 Step-by-Step Phases of an Action Potential The AP is best understood as a **four-stage voltage dance** driven by two channel populations (Na⁺ and K⁺) with different voltage sensitivities and time constants: ### 🟢 Stage 1 — Resting State - Membrane potential = **−70 mV** (inside negative). - K⁺ "leak" channels dominate permeability (P_K ≫ P_Na). - Na⁺ channels are *closed* (in their *closed-activatable* state). ### 🟡 Stage 2 — Depolarization (Rising Phase) - A stimulus brings V_m above the **threshold of −55 mV**. - Voltage-gated **Na⁺ channels** open rapidly (activation gate opens; *m*-gate kinetics). - Na⁺ flows *into* the cell (driven by both concentration AND electrical gradient). - V_m shoots up toward the **Na⁺ Nernst potential** (E_Na ≈ **+60 mV**). - **Positive feedback** — depolarization opens more Na⁺ channels → regenerative. ### 🔴 Stage 3 — Repolarization (Falling Phase) - Na⁺ channels **inactivate** (the *h*-gate closes, ~1 ms after opening). - Voltage-gated **K⁺ channels** open (slower activation). - K⁺ flows *out* of the cell. - V_m returns toward the **K⁺ Nernst potential** (E_K ≈ **−90 mV**). ### 🔵 Stage 4 — Hyperpolarization & Refractory Periods - K⁺ channels close *slowly* → V_m briefly dips **below −70 mV** (≈ −80 mV). This is the **after-hyperpolarization (AHP)** or **undershoot**. - **Absolute Refractory Period (ARP):** No stimulus, no matter how strong, can elicit another AP. (Na⁺ channels are inactivated.) - **Relative Refractory Period (RRP):** A *larger-than-normal* stimulus can trigger an AP, because (a) some Na⁺ channels are still recovering, and (b) K⁺ channels are still open (membrane is hyperpolarized *toward* E_K, but further from threshold). --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!TIP] > **MEMORIZE THIS TABLE** — these equations appear in derivations, MCQs, and 14-mark problems in KTU ESE. Note: I have used `\vert` instead of `|` to keep markdown table integrity. | # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units / Values | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{RT}{zF}\ln\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$ | **Nernst Equation** — equilibrium potential for one ion | mV | | 2 | $E_{ion} = \dfrac{61.5\ \text{mV}}{z}\log_{10}\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$ | Nernst eq. at 37 °C (body temperature) | mV | | 3 | $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F}\ln\dfrac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}}$ | **Goldman–Hodgkin–Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation** | mV | | 4 | $\tau_m = R_m \cdot C_m$ | **Membrane time constant** — how fast V_m responds | seconds (typical 1–20 ms) | | 5 | $\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{r_m}{r_i + r_o}}$ | **Length constant** — how far a signal decays passively | mm (typical 0.1–1 mm) | | 6 | $v = \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{LC}}$ | Conduction velocity analogy (cable model) | m/s | | 7 | $\dfrac{\partial V}{\partial t} + I_{ion} = \dfrac{1}{r_m}\dfrac{\partial^{2}V}{\partial x^{2}}$ | **Cable equation** (1-D passive) | V/s, V/m² | | 8 | $I_{Na} = g_{Na}\, m^{3}h\, (V - E_{Na})$ | Hodgkin–Huxley Na⁺ current | A/m² | | 9 | $I_{K} = g_{K}\, n^{4}\, (V - E_{K})$ | Hodgkin–Huxley K⁺ current | A/m² | | 10 | $g_{ion} = \bar{g}_{ion} \cdot (\text{gating variable})^{\text{order}}$ | Conductance via gating variable | S/m² | > **Where:** > - $R$ = universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)** > - $F$ = Faraday constant = **96,485 C/mol** > - $T$ = absolute temperature in K (310 K at 37 °C) > - $z$ = ion valence (+1 for K⁺/Na⁺, −1 for Cl⁻, +2 for Ca²⁺) > - $P$ = membrane permeability > - $r_m$, $r_i$, $r_o$ = membrane, axoplasm, extracellular resistivities (Ω·m) > - $C_m$ = membrane capacitance per unit area (≈ 1 µF/cm²) > - $m, h, n$ = HH gating variables (activation/inactivation) --- ## 2.5 Engineering Utility in the Real World | Application | How AP Knowledge Helps | |---|---| | **EEG / ECG / EMG electrodes** | Choosing bandwidth, contact impedance (< 5 kΩ), CMRR | | **Intracortical microelectrodes (Utah array, Michigan probe)** | Tip geometry to penetrate without rupturing; iridium oxide coatings for charge injection | | **Cochlear implants** | Matched-pulse biphasic stimulation to elicit APs in auditory nerve | | **Deep brain stimulation (DBS)** | Pulse width 60–200 µs, frequency 130–180 Hz targets AP firing rates | | **Closed-loop neuroprosthetics** | Decode AP timing to drive robotic limbs | | **FET-based biosensors (ISFET, NeuroFET)** | Detect field potentials from summed APs in neural tissue | # 🧠 Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix > [!NOTE] > **Mandatory Rule:** Every algebraic transition and every numerical evaluation is shown in full. No shortcuts. Read sequentially. --- ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Nernst Equation from Electrochemical Equilibrium ### Goal Derive an expression for the *equilibrium membrane potential* of a single ionic species, given its concentrations on each side of the membrane. ### Step 1 — Set Up the Electrochemical Potential The total work (Gibbs free energy) required to move **1 mole** of an ion of charge $z$ from the *inside* (concentration $[ion]_{in}$) to the *outside* (concentration $[ion]_{out}$) across a membrane at potential $V_m$ (inside relative to outside) has **two contributions**: 1. **Chemical work** = $RT \ln\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$ 2. **Electrical work** = $zFV_m$ $$ \Delta G = RT \ln\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}} + zFV_m $$ ### Step 2 — Apply the Equilibrium Condition At equilibrium, there is **no net flux** of the ion. By the second law of thermodynamics, the net work done in moving an ion across the membrane must be **zero**: $$ \Delta G = 0 $$ Therefore: $$ RT \ln\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}} + zFV_{ion} = 0 $$ ### Step 3 — Isolate $V_{ion}$ $$ zFV_{ion} = -RT \ln\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}} $$ $$ V_{ion} = -\dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}} $$ ### Step 4 — Flip the Fraction Using $\ln(1/x) = -\ln(x)$ Since the formula is conventionally written as a *log of outside-over-inside*, we use the identity: $$ V_{ion} = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln\dfrac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}} = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}} \quad (\text{equivalent with sign convention}) $$ > The KTU 2024 standard convention writes: > $$E_{ion} = \dfrac{RT}{zF} \ln\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}$$ > where $E_{ion}$ is the *Nernst potential* (the membrane potential that exactly balances the chemical gradient). ### Step 5 — Convert to log₁₀ and Plug in Body Constants Using $\ln(x) = 2.303 \log_{10}(x)$: $$ E_{ion} = \dfrac{2.303\,RT}{zF} \log_{10}\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}} $$ At body temperature $T = 310$ K (37 °C): $$ \dfrac{2.303 \times 8.314 \times 310}{96485} = 0.0615\ \text{V} = 61.5\ \text{mV} $$ Hence the **Nernst equation for physiological use**: $$ \boxed{\,E_{ion} = \dfrac{61.5\ \text{mV}}{z}\,\log_{10}\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}}\,} $$ --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Numerical Computation of E_K and E_Na ### Step 1 — Potassium Nernst Potential Given: - $[K^+]_{out}$ = **4.5 mM**, $[K^+]_{in}$ = **140 mM**, $z = +1$ $$ E_K = \dfrac{61.5\ \text{mV}}{+1} \log_{10}\dfrac{4.5}{140} $$ $$ \log_{10}\dfrac{4.5}{140} = \log_{10}(0.03214) = -1.4931 $$ $$ E_K = 61.5 \times (-1.4931) = -91.83\ \text{mV} $$ $$ \boxed{E_K \approx -92\ \text{mV}} $$ ### Step 2 — Sodium Nernst Potential Given: - $[Na^+]_{out}$ = **145 mM**, $[Na^+]_{in}$ = **15 mM**, $z = +1$ $$ E_{Na} = 61.5 \cdot \log_{10}\dfrac{145}{15} $$ $$ \log_{10}(9.6667) = 0.9853 $$ $$ E_{Na} = 61.5 \times 0.9853 = +60.60\ \text{mV} $$ $$ \boxed{E_{Na} \approx +61\ \text{mV}} $$ > [!IMPORTANT] > The RMP (−70 mV) lies **between E_K (−92 mV) and E_Na (+61 mV)**, confirming that the membrane is selectively permeable to K⁺ at rest (i.e., the RMP is "dragged" toward E_K). This is a **favourite KTU 2-mark / 3-mark question**. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Resting Membrane Potential via GHK Equation ### Setup Assume typical neuronal permeability ratios at rest: - $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl} = 1 : 0.04 : 0.45$ Concentrations: - $K^+$: 140 (in), 4.5 (out) - $Na^+$: 15 (in), 145 (out) - $Cl^-$: 4 (in), 120 (out) ### Step 1 — Write the GHK Equation $$ V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F}\ln\dfrac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}} $$ ### Step 2 — Substitute the Numerical Values Numerator: $$ P_K[K^+]_{out} = 1 \times 4.5 = 4.5 $$ $$ P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} = 0.04 \times 145 = 5.8 $$ $$ P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in} = 0.45 \times 4 = 1.8 $$ $$ \text{Sum}_{\text{num}} = 4.5 + 5.8 + 1.8 = 12.1 $$ Denominator: $$ P_K[K^+]_{in} = 1 \times 140 = 140 $$ $$ P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} = 0.04 \times 15 = 0.6 $$ $$ P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out} = 0.45 \times 120 = 54.0 $$ $$ \text{Sum}_{\text{den}} = 140 + 0.6 + 54.0 = 194.6 $$ ### Step 3 — Compute the Log Ratio $$ \ln\dfrac{12.1}{194.6} = \ln(0.06218) = -2.7784 $$ ### Step 4 — Multiply by $RT/F$ $$ \dfrac{RT}{F} = \dfrac{8.314 \times 310}{96485} = 0.02671\ \text{V} = 26.71\ \text{mV} $$ $$ V_m = 26.71 \times (-2.7784) = -74.21\ \text{mV} $$ $$ \boxed{V_m \approx -74\ \text{mV}} $$ > The slight discrepancy from the textbook −70 mV is due to assumed permeability ratios; KTU expects an answer in the **range −70 to −90 mV**. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Membrane Time Constant $\tau_m$ and Length Constant $\lambda$ ### Time Constant The membrane is modeled as a parallel RC circuit. Apply a current step $I_0$ and solve: $$ C_m\dfrac{dV}{dt} + \dfrac{V}{R_m} = \dfrac{I_0}{A} $$ For *passive* decay (no current injection), the homogeneous solution is: $$ V(t) = V_0\,e^{-t/\tau_m} $$ Where: $$ \boxed{\tau_m = R_m \cdot C_m} $$ **Typical values:** $R_m \approx 2000\ \Omega\cdot\text{cm}^2$, $C_m \approx 1\ \mu\text{F/cm}^2$ → $\tau_m \approx 2$ ms. ### Length Constant Solving the cable equation for steady-state spatial decay (V decays exponentially with distance $x$ along the axon): $$ V(x) = V_0\,e^{-x/\lambda} $$ Where: $$ \boxed{\lambda = \sqrt{\dfrac{r_m}{r_i}}} $$ (r_o is often negligible for an axon in a large extracellular volume.) **Typical values:** $\lambda \approx 0.1$ mm (unmyelinated) to 1 mm (myelinated). > [!TIP] > **Engineering insight:** Myelination increases $r_m$ (less leak) and decreases $r_i$ (bigger axon) → $\lambda$ grows → APs propagate further before needing regeneration. **Saltatory conduction** skips from node to node, achieving speeds of **120 m/s** in large myelinated axons. # 🧠 Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics > [!NOTE] > Mermaid diagrams below follow the **Node Identifier Alpha Rule** (all node IDs alphanumeric, prefixed with letters) and contain no markdown formatting inside quoted labels. --- ## 4.1 Diagram A — Action Potential Waveform with Phases ```mermaid flowchart LR A0(["STAGE 0: RESTING STATE"]) --> A1(["STIMULUS APPLIED"]) A1 --> A2(["DEPOLARIZATION
Na+ channels open
V_m rises to plus 30 mV"]) A2 --> A3(["PEAK REVERSAL
V_m approx plus 35 mV"]) A3 --> A4(["REPOLARIZATION
Na+ channels inactivate
K+ channels open"]) A4 --> A5(["HYPERPOLARIZATION
V_m approx minus 80 mV
After Hyperpolarization AHP"]) A5 --> A6(["RETURN TO RESTING
Na+ K+ pump restores gradients"]) A6 --> A0 style A0 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a style A2 fill:#fde68a,stroke:#b45309 style A3 fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#7f1d1d style A4 fill:#bbf7d0,stroke:#065f46 style A5 fill:#c7d2fe,stroke:#3730a3 ``` --- ## 4.2 Diagram B — Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Biosensor Recording an Action Potential ```mermaid flowchart TD src1(["NEURON SOURCE
Action Potential
100 mV intracellular"]) --> trans1(["TRANSDUCER 1
Microelectrode
Ag or AgCl or Ir Oxide"]) trans1 --> amp1(["AMPLIFIER STAGE
Gain 1000 to 10000
CMRR greater than 80 dB"]) amp1 --> filt1(["FILTER STAGE
High Pass 0.5 Hz
Low Pass 3 kHz"]) filt1 --> adc1(["ADC STAGE
Sampling greater than 5 kHz
16 bit resolution"]) adc1 --> proc1(["DIGITAL PROCESSOR
Spike Detection
Sorting and Feature Extraction"]) proc1 --> out1(["OUTPUT
Spike Trains to BCI
or Storage"]) style src1 fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b style trans1 fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e style amp1 fill:#dcfce7,stroke:#166534 style filt1 fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e40af style adc1 fill:#ede9fe,stroke:#5b21b6 style proc1 fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d style out1 fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3 ``` --- ## 4.3 Diagram C — Hodgkin–Huxley Cycle (Channel State Machine) ```mermaid stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> sResting sResting --> sStimulus: V_m greater than threshold sStimulus --> sNaOpen: Na+ activation m gate opens sNaOpen --> sNaInactivated: Na+ inactivation h gate closes sNaInactivated --> sKOpen: K+ delayed rectifier opens sKOpen --> sAHP: Hyperpolarization undershoot sAHP --> sResting: Na+ K+ pump and leak restore V_m sNaInactivated --> sARPnote: Absolute Refractory Period sKOpen --> sRRPnote: Relative Refractory Period sARPnote --> sNaInactivated sRRPnote --> sResting ``` --- ## 4.4 Diagram D — Functional Block Architecture of a Neuron (Anatomical ↔ Functional Mapping) ```mermaid flowchart LR dend1(["DENDRITES
Input zone
Receive EPSP and IPSP"]) --> soma1(["SOMA
Integrator
V_m summation"]) soma1 --> hill1(["AXON HILLOCK
Trigger zone
Highest Na+ density"]) hill1 --> axon1(["AXON
Propagation cable
Myelinated segments"]) axon1 --> node1(["NODES OF RANVIER
AP regeneration
1 um gaps"]) node1 --> term1(["AXON TERMINALS
Output zone
Neurotransmitter release"]) term1 --> syn1(["SYNAPTIC CLEFT
20 to 40 nm gap
Chemical signal"]) syn1 --> post1(["POSTSYNAPTIC NEURON
Receptor binding
New AP generated"]) style dend1 fill:#fde68a style soma1 fill:#fed7aa style hill1 fill:#fca5a5 style axon1 fill:#bbf7d0 style node1 fill:#86efac style term1 fill:#67e8f9 style syn1 fill:#a5f3fc style post1 fill:#c4b5fd ``` # 🧠 Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout #1):** > Students often confuse **Nernst potential** with **resting membrane potential**. Nernst is for a *single ion at equilibrium*; RMP is a *combined value* (use the **GHK equation**). Writing "E = 61.5/z × log(in/out)" without specifying ion loses 1 mark. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout #2):** > For 14-mark derivations, examiners expect: (1) starting equation, (2) physical meaning of each term, (3) numerical substitution, AND (4) final boxed answer. Skipping the substitution step costs up to **3 marks** in Module 2 problems. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (Pitfall Callout #3):** > Students frequently state "AP = +30 mV" without clarifying the **reference (intracellular vs extracellular)**. Always write "V_m peaks at +30 mV *inside relative to outside*." --- ## 📘 PART A QUESTIONS (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]** Define an action potential. List the four main ionic phases of a typical neuronal action potential with their associated dominant ion currents. **Model Answer (3 marks):** An **action potential (AP)** is a rapid, transient, all-or-nothing depolarization of the neuronal membrane that propagates along the axon, caused by the sequential opening of voltage-gated Na⁺ and K⁺ channels, with a peak amplitude of about **100 mV** and a duration of **1–2 ms**. **Four ionic phases:** | # | Phase | Dominant Current | Voltage Change | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Resting** | K⁺ leak (outward) | Stable at −70 mV | | 2 | **Depolarization** | Na⁺ influx (inward) | Rises to +30 mV | | 3 | **Repolarization** | K⁺ efflux (outward) | Returns to −70 mV | | 4 | **Hyperpolarization (AHP)** | Continued K⁺ efflux | Dips to −80 mV | *(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for phase names, 1 mark for ion-current association — 3 marks total.)* --- ### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** State the Nernst equation. Calculate the Nernst potential for K⁺ at 37 °C given $[K^+]_{in} = 140$ mM and $[K^+]_{out} = 4.5$ mM. **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Nernst equation** gives the equilibrium potential for a single ion: $$ E_{ion} = \dfrac{61.5\ \text{mV}}{z}\,\log_{10}\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}} $$ For K⁺ ($z = +1$): $$ E_K = 61.5 \cdot \log_{10}\dfrac{4.5}{140} = 61.5 \cdot \log_{10}(0.03214) = 61.5 \times (-1.493) = -91.85\ \text{mV} $$ $$ \boxed{E_K \approx -92\ \text{mV}} $$ *(1 mark for equation, 1 mark for substitution, 1 mark for final numerical answer.)* --- ## 📗 PART B QUESTIONS (14 Marks — Internal Choice) > *KTU ESE convention: Each Part B question has two sub-parts (a) 7 marks and (b) 7 marks. Two alternative questions A and B are provided below — answer EITHER A OR B.* --- ### **Question A — [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B, Q. Module-2(a)]** #### (a) Derive the Nernst equation from thermodynamic principles. Hence compute E_Na for typical neuronal concentrations and explain its significance in the rising phase of an action potential. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Define the system (1 mark)** Consider a membrane separating two ionic solutions. The Gibbs free energy change to move 1 mole of ions of charge $z$ from inside (concentration $[ion]_{in}$) to outside (concentration $[ion]_{out}$) across a membrane held at potential $V_m$ is: $$ \Delta G = RT \ln\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}} + zFV_m $$ **Step 2 — Apply equilibrium condition (1 mark)** At equilibrium, $\Delta G = 0$: $$ RT \ln\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}} + zF V_{ion} = 0 $$ **Step 3 — Rearrange (1 mark)** $$ V_{ion} = -\dfrac{RT}{zF}\ln\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}} = \dfrac{RT}{zF}\ln\dfrac{[ion]_{in}}{[ion]_{out}} $$ **Step 4 — Convert to base-10 logarithm (1 mark)** Using $\ln x = 2.303 \log_{10} x$ and at $T = 310$ K: $$ E_{ion} = \dfrac{61.5\ \text{mV}}{z}\log_{10}\dfrac{[ion]_{out}}{[ion]_{in}} $$ **Step 5 — Numerical computation for Na⁺ (2 marks)** With $[Na^+]_{out} = 145$ mM, $[Na^+]_{in} = 15$ mM, $z = +1$: $$ E_{Na} = 61.5 \cdot \log_{10}\dfrac{145}{15} = 61.5 \times 0.9853 = +60.6\ \text{mV} $$ $$ \boxed{E_{Na} \approx +61\ \text{mV}} $$ **Step 6 — Significance in AP rising phase (1 mark)** During depolarization, voltage-gated Na⁺ channels open. Na⁺ flows *into* the cell down its electrochemical gradient (concentration gradient AND electrical gradient after V_m approaches 0). The membrane potential rapidly approaches E_Na, but does not quite reach it because (a) K⁺ channels are also opening, and (b) Na⁺ channels inactivate before full equilibrium. The peak of the AP (~ +30 mV) is a balance point of these opposing currents. --- #### (b) With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture of a biosensor system used to record action potentials from cortical neurons. State the role of each block. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** A typical **neural recording biosensor** uses the following block architecture: | Block # | Block Name | Function | Key Specification | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | **Source (Neuron)** | Generates AP electrochemical signal | V_peak ≈ 100 mV intracellular | | 2 | **Transducer (Microelectrode)** | Converts ionic current to electron flow | Ag/AgCl, Ir-oxide, tip < 10 µm | | 3 | **Preamplifier (Headstage)** | First-stage amplification near source | Gain ×10, very high input Z (GΩ) | | 4 | **Main Amplifier** | Boosts signal to ADC range | Gain ×1000, CMRR > 80 dB | | 5 | **Filter Bank** | Band-limits signal to AP bandwidth | HPF 0.5 Hz, LPF 3 kHz, Notch 50 Hz | | 6 | **ADC** | Digitises the analog waveform | Sampling ≥ 5 kHz, ≥ 12-bit | | 7 | **Digital Processor** | Spike detection, sorting, feature extraction | Threshold, PCA, template matching | | 8 | **Output/Display/Storage** | Presents or stores spike trains | LCD, SSD, or wireless telemetry | **Block diagram (textual representation):** ``` Neuron → Microelectrode → Preamplifier → Amplifier → Filter → ADC → DSP → Display ``` *(1 mark per row × 5 = 5 marks for the table; 2 marks for diagram + integration logic.)* --- ### **Question B — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set A, Q. Module-2(b)]** #### (a) Explain the ionic basis of the resting membrane potential. Show how the Goldman–Hodgkin–Katz (GHK) equation is used to compute the RMP for a neuron with $P_K : P_{Na} : P_{Cl} = 1 : 0.04 : 0.45$ and given concentrations: $K^+ = 140/4.5$ mM (in/out), $Na^+ = 15/145$ mM, $Cl^- = 4/120$ mM. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Concept of RMP (1 mark)** The RMP is the steady-state voltage across the neuronal membrane at rest. It is maintained by the **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump** (3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in per ATP) and is dominated by **K⁺ permeability** at rest. The membrane is more permeable to K⁺ than to Na⁺, so V_m lies close to E_K (~ −90 mV) but is "pulled" toward E_Na (+60 mV) by the small Na⁺ leak. **Step 2 — The GHK equation (2 marks)** $$ V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F}\ln\dfrac{P_K[K^+]_{out} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{out} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{in}}{P_K[K^+]_{in} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{in} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{out}} $$ **Step 3 — Substitution (2 marks)** Numerator: $(1)(4.5) + (0.04)(145) + (0.45)(4) = 4.5 + 5.8 + 1.8 = 12.1$ Denominator: $(1)(140) + (0.04)(15) + (0.45)(120) = 140 + 0.6 + 54.0 = 194.6$ **Step 4 — Compute and multiply by $RT/F$ (2 marks)** $$ V_m = 26.71 \cdot \ln(12.1/194.6) = 26.71 \times (-2.7784) \approx -74.2\ \text{mV} $$ $$ \boxed{V_m \approx -74\ \text{mV}} $$ This matches the typical experimentally observed RMP of **−70 mV** within tolerance. --- #### (b) Describe the propagation of an action potential along a myelinated axon. Explain saltatory conduction and the role of Nodes of Ranvier. State two clinical conditions caused by demyelination. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Step 1 — Passive vs active propagation (1 mark)** In a *uniform* unmyelinated axon, the AP is regenerated continuously along the membrane by sequential opening of Na⁺ channels. In a *myelinated* axon, the membrane is electrically insulated by concentric myelin wraps → high $R_m$, low $C_m$ → the local-circuit currents can spread much further (large $\lambda$). **Step 2 — Saltatory conduction (2 marks)** The AP effectively **"jumps"** from one **Node of Ranvier** to the next. At each node, voltage-gated Na⁺ channels are densely packed (≈ 1000–2000/µm²) and regenerate the full AP amplitude. The internodal segments act as *passive cables* — no channels, no AP regeneration, just electrotonic conduction. **Step 3 — Conduction velocity comparison (2 marks)** | Axon Type | Diameter | Myelinated? | Velocity | |---|---|---|---| | C-fiber (pain, autonomic) | 0.5 µm | No | 0.5–2 m/s | | Aδ-fiber (sharp pain) | 2–5 µm | Thinly | 5–30 m/s | | Aα-fiber (motor, proprioception) | 10–20 µm | Heavily | 80–120 m/s | **Step 4 — Clinical relevance (2 marks)** Two major demyelinating diseases: 1. **Multiple Sclerosis (MS)** — autoimmune destruction of CNS myelin (oligodendrocytes); causes slowed/blocked APs, leading to vision loss, weakness, ataxia. 2. **Guillain–Barré Syndrome (GBS)** — autoimmune attack on PNS myelin (Schwann cells); causes ascending paralysis. > **Note for the student:** Both conditions demonstrate that *myelin is not just insulation* — it is a *transducer performance enhancer* (analogous to dielectric coating on a capacitor or shielding on a signal cable). --- ## ✅ Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Brain Action Potentials (Module 2)** - ⭐ **Resting Membrane Potential** = approximately **−70 mV**, dominated by **K⁺ permeability**. - ⭐ **Nernst Equation** at 37 °C: $E_{ion} = (61.5/z)\log_{10}([out]/[in])$ mV. - ⭐ **E_K ≈ −92 mV**, **E_Na ≈ +61 mV**, **E_Cl ≈ −65 mV**, **E_Ca ≈ +120 mV**. - ⭐ **GHK equation** combines multiple ion permeabilities — used to compute **RMP**, not Nernst alone. - ⭐ **AP has 4 phases**: Resting → Depolarization (Na⁺ in) → Repolarization (K⁺ out) → Hyperpolarization/AHP. - ⭐ **Threshold** ≈ **−55 mV**; **All-or-Nothing Law** — sub-threshold stimuli decay, supra-threshold produce full AP. - ⭐ **Absolute Refractory Period** = Na⁺ channels inactivated → **no AP possible**. - ⭐ **Relative Refractory Period** = Na⁺ channels recovering + K⁺ channels still open → **stronger stimulus needed**. - ⭐ **Hodgkin–Huxley Model (1952)** uses gating variables $m$, $h$, $n$ for Na⁺ activation, Na⁺ inactivation, and K⁺ activation. - ⭐ **Cable equation** governs passive spread: $\tau_m = R_m C_m$ (time constant), $\lambda = \sqrt{r_m/r_i}$ (length constant). - ⭐ **Saltatory conduction** in myelinated axons → fast propagation (up to **120 m/s**). - ⭐ **Nodes of Ranvier** = Na⁺ channel hotspots (~ 1 µm gap every 0.5–2 mm). - ⭐ **Synapse** converts electrical AP → chemical (neurotransmitter) → electrical (EPSP/IPSP) in the postsynaptic cell. - ⭐ **Biosensor relevance**: AP amplitude (50–500 µV at scalp, ~100 mV intracellular) sets amplifier gain; AP bandwidth (~ 3 kHz) sets filter cutoff; refractory period (~ 2 ms) sets maximum spike rate (~ 500 Hz theoretical, ~ 100 Hz physiological). - ⭐ **BCI applications** decode AP firing rates and timings to drive prosthetics, wheelchairs, and computer cursors. - ⭐ **Clinical pearls**: **MS** and **GBS** are demyelinating diseases; **epilepsy** is hypersynchronous AP firing; **local anesthetics** (lidocaine) block voltage-gated Na⁺ channels. - ⭐ **Numerical constants to memorize**: $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K); $F = 96{,}485$ C/mol; $T = 310$ K (37 °C); $RT/F = 26.71$ mV at 37 °C; $C_m \approx 1$ µF/cm².

topics.title: Characteristics of Signal - Electrodes and 10-20 System for EEG Signal Acquisition. Evoked Potentials. EMG
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT611 - BIOSENSORS & TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of Signal - Electrodes and 10-20 System for EEG Signal Acquisition. Evoked Potentials. EMG

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Characteristics of Biomedical Signals

A **biomedical signal** is a time-varying physical quantity that carries information about a biological system, such as the electrical activity of the heart, brain, or muscles. The characteristics of these signals are typically described in terms of **amplitude, frequency content, signal-to-noise ratio (SNR), bandwidth, and stationarity**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus):**
> A biomedical signal is characterized by its origin (bioelectric, biochemical, biomechanical, bioacoustic, bio-optical), frequency range, amplitude range, and the nature of noise superimposed on it. For biosensor applications, understanding signal characteristics is critical for selecting the right transducer and signal conditioning circuitry.

**Key Parameters of Biomedical Signals:**

- **Amplitude Range:** Usually very low, from microvolts ($\mu V$) to millivolts ($mV$).
- **Frequency Range:** Varies from near DC (e.g., galvanic skin response) to several kHz (e.g., EMG).
- **Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR):** The ratio of signal power to noise power; determines detectability.
- **Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (CMRR):** Ability to reject common-mode noise (e.g., 50/60 Hz power line interference).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy:** Think of biomedical signals like whispers in a noisy stadium. The actual "voice" (signal) coming from the body is very soft, while the "crowd noise" (interference from muscles, power lines, other organs) is loud. Our job as biomedical engineers is to use specialized microphones (electrodes) and filters (signal conditioning) to isolate and amplify these whispers so we can interpret them.

### 1.2 Electrodes for Bioelectric Signal Acquisition

An **electrode** is a transducer that converts ionic current flow in biological tissue into electronic current flow in the measurement instrument. Electrodes form the critical interface between the living body and the electronic measurement system.

**Classification of Electrodes:**

1. **Surface Electrodes (Non-invasive):** Placed on skin surface; e.g., Ag/AgCl EEG electrodes.
2. **Invasive (Needle/Micro) Electrodes:** Penetrates tissue; e.g., needle EMG electrodes.
3. **Microelectrodes:** Used for single-cell recording; tip diameter ~1 $\mu m$.

### 1.3 10-20 System for EEG Signal Acquisition

The **International 10-20 System** is the standardized method for placing scalp electrodes for electroencephalography (EEG). It ensures reproducibility across laboratories and clinics worldwide.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (KTU Syllabus Highlight):**
> The 10-20 system refers to electrode placement at intervals of 10% and 20% of measured distances on the scalp. The system was developed by Herbert Jasper in 1958 and adopted by the International Federation of Societies for Electroencephalography and Clinical Neurophysiology (IFSECN).

### 1.4 Evoked Potentials (EPs)

**Evoked Potentials** are electrical signals generated by the nervous system in response to a specific sensory stimulus (visual, auditory, or somatosensory). They are extracted from the ongoing EEG by averaging multiple responses to remove random background activity.

**Types of Evoked Potentials:**

- **Visual Evoked Potentials (VEP):** Stimulus = flashing light or pattern reversal.
- **Auditory Evoked Potentials (AEP) / Brainstem Auditory Evoked Potentials (BAEP):** Stimulus = click sounds.
- **Somatosensory Evoked Potentials (SEP):** Stimulus = electrical stimulation of peripheral nerves.

### 1.5 Electromyography (EMG)

**Electromyography (EMG)** is the technique of recording the electrical activity produced by skeletal muscles. The signal originates from the depolarization of muscle fiber membranes during voluntary or stimulated contraction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Clinical Significance:** EMG is used to diagnose neuromuscular disorders such as myasthenia gravis, muscular dystrophy, peripheral neuropathy, and to assess the integrity of motor units.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Characteristics of Bioelectric Signals — Deep Analysis

Every bioelectric signal exhibits a unique signature based on its source. The table below summarizes the typical characteristics:

| Signal | Source | Amplitude Range | Frequency Range | Standard Unit |
|------------|------------|-----------------|-----------------|---------------|
| EEG | Brain | 10–100 $\mu V$ | 0.5–100 Hz | Microvolts |
| ECG | Heart | 0.5–4 mV | 0.05–150 Hz | Millivolts |
| EMG | Muscles | 50 $\mu V$ – 5 mV | 20–5000 Hz | Microvolts–Millivolts |
| EOG | Eye | 0.05–3.5 mV | DC–50 Hz | Millivolts |
| ERG | Retina | 0–900 $\mu V$ | 0.2–200 Hz | Microvolts |

### 2.2 Electrode Theory and the Electrode-Electrolyte Interface

When a metal electrode is placed in contact with an electrolyte (body fluid), a **half-cell potential** (also called the electrode potential) is established at the interface due to charge transfer. This is governed by the **Nernst Equation**:

For the half-reaction $M \rightleftharpoons M^{n+} + ne^{-}$, the Nernst equation gives the equilibrium potential of the electrode.

**KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet:**

| Formula | Description | Unit |
|----------|-------------|------|
| $E = E^{0} + \dfrac{RT}{nF} \ln a_{ion}$ | Nernst Equation for half-cell potential | Volts (V) |
| $V_{oc} = \dfrac{kT}{q} \ln \left( \dfrac{N_{a}}{N_{d}} \right)$ | Open-circuit voltage of p-n junction | Volts (V) |
| $Z_{electrode} = R_{s} + \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_{d}}$ | Electrode impedance (Randles model) | Ohms ($\Omega$) |
| $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}} \right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio in decibels | dB |
| $CMRR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \dfrac{A_{d}}{A_{c}} \right)$ | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio | dB |
| $V_{out} = A_{d}(V_{d}) + A_{c}(V_{c})$ | Differential amplifier output equation | Volts (V) |
| $V_{rms} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T} \int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t) dt}$ | RMS value of a periodic signal | Volts (V) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:** The Nernst equation governs the design of ion-selective electrodes (ISEs) used in pH meters, blood gas analyzers, and ion concentration biosensors. The half-cell potential must be minimized or matched (using a reference electrode) to avoid polarization artifacts in EEG and ECG recordings.

### 2.3 The 10-20 System — Detailed Analysis

**Anatomical Landmarks and Measurements:**

- **Nasion (Nz):** The depression at the bridge of the nose, between the eyes.
- **Inion (Iz):** The bony bump at the back of the skull (occipital protuberance).
- **Pre-auricular Points (A1, A2):** The depressions in front of each ear.

**Procedure for Electrode Placement:**

1. Measure the distance from **Nasion to Inion** along the midline of the scalp.
2. Mark points at **10%, 20%, 20%, 20%, 20%, 10%** of this total distance (5 divisions).
3. Similarly, measure from **Left pre-auricular to Right pre-auricular** across the crown (Cz).
4. Mark corresponding 10% and 20% intervals.

**Electrode Naming Convention:**

- Letters denote the brain region: **F** (Frontal), **C** (Central), **P** (Parietal), **T** (Temporal), **O** (Occipital), **A** (Auricular).
- Subscripts: Even numbers (2, 4, 6, 8) = right hemisphere; Odd numbers (1, 3, 5, 7) = left hemisphere; "z" = midline.

> [!NOTE]
> **Total Standard Electrodes in 10-20 System:** 21 electrodes (including ground and reference). Extended versions like the 10-10 system use up to 64–128 electrodes for high-density EEG.

### 2.4 Evoked Potentials — Theoretical Basis

The amplitude of an evoked potential is typically 0.1–20 $\mu V$, which is **much smaller** than the ongoing spontaneous EEG (10–100 $\mu V$). Hence, the technique of **signal averaging** is employed.

**Averaging Equation:**

$$
S_{avg}(t) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i(t)
$$

Where $x_i(t)$ is the $i^{th}$ response and $N$ is the number of trials. Random noise averages to zero, while the time-locked evoked potential remains.

**Signal-to-Noise Improvement After Averaging:**

$$
SNR_{improved} = SNR_{single} \cdot \sqrt{N}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Real-World Application:** AEPs (especially BAEPs) are used to assess hearing in newborns and in patients who cannot cooperate with conventional audiometry. SEPs are used during spinal surgery to monitor cord integrity and prevent iatrogenic injury.

### 2.5 EMG Signal Analysis

EMG signals are broadly classified as:

- **Voluntary (Surface) EMG:** Recorded using surface electrodes; represents the sum of motor unit action potentials (MUAPs).
- **Needle EMG:** Uses concentric or monopolar needle electrodes to record from deep muscles; reveals individual MUAPs.

**Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP) Characteristics:**

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|-----------|---------------|
| Amplitude | 100 $\mu V$ – 5 mV |
| Duration | 3–15 ms |
| Phases | 2–4 (polyphasic if > 4) |
| Rise Time | 0.5–2 ms |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: SNR Improvement by Signal Averaging

**Problem Context:** The evoked potential $s(t)$ is buried in random noise $n(t)$. The recorded signal in trial $i$ is $x_i(t) = s(t) + n_i(t)$. We want to show how averaging $N$ trials improves the SNR.

**Step 1: Define the recorded signal for the $i^{th}$ trial.**

$$
x_i(t) = s(t) + n_i(t)
$$

Here, $s(t)$ is the deterministic (time-locked) evoked potential and $n_i(t)$ is the random noise with zero mean: $E[n_i(t)] = 0$.

**Step 2: Form the average over $N$ trials.**

$$
\bar{x}(t) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} x_i(t) = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} s(t) + \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i(t)
$$

**Step 3: Evaluate each term separately.**

The signal term:

$$
\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} s(t) = \frac{1}{N} \cdot N \cdot s(t) = s(t)
$$

The noise term (as $N \to \infty$):

$$
\frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i(t) \to E[n(t)] = 0
$$

**Step 4: Compute the variance of the averaged noise.**

For independent noise samples with variance $\sigma_n^2$:

$$
\sigma_{\bar{n}}^{2} = \text{Var}\left( \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} n_i(t) \right) = \frac{1}{N^{2}} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sigma_n^{2} = \frac{\sigma_n^{2}}{N}
$$

**Step 5: Take the square root to get the standard deviation of the averaged noise.**

$$
\sigma_{\bar{n}} = \frac{\sigma_n}{\sqrt{N}}
$$

**Step 6: Compute the SNR of the averaged signal.**

$$
SNR_{avg} = \frac{s(t)}{\sigma_{\bar{n}}} = \frac{s(t)}{\sigma_n / \sqrt{N}} = \sqrt{N} \cdot \frac{s(t)}{\sigma_n} = \sqrt{N} \cdot SNR_{single}
$$

**Conclusion:** Averaging $N$ trials improves the SNR by a factor of $\sqrt{N}$. For $N = 100$ trials, the SNR improves by 10 (or **20 dB**).

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Electrode Half-Cell Potential Using Nernst Equation

**Given:** A silver-silver chloride (Ag/AgCl) electrode in contact with an electrolyte containing Cl⁻ ions at concentration $[Cl^-] = 0.1 M$ at body temperature $T = 310 K$.

**Step 1: Write the Nernst Equation for the Ag/AgCl half-cell.**

The half-reaction is: $AgCl + e^{-} \rightleftharpoons Ag + Cl^{-}$

$$
E = E^{0} - \frac{RT}{nF} \ln [Cl^{-}]
$$

**Step 2: Identify the constants.**

- Standard potential: $E^{0} = +0.2223 V$ (for Ag/AgCl at 25°C)
- Gas constant: $R = 8.314 J/(mol \cdot K)$
- Temperature: $T = 310 K$
- Faraday's constant: $F = 96485 C/mol$
- Number of electrons: $n = 1$

**Step 3: Calculate the thermal voltage term.**

$$
\frac{RT}{F} = \frac{8.314 \times 310}{96485} = \frac{2577.34}{96485} \approx 0.02671 V
$$

**Step 4: Compute the logarithm term.**

$$
\ln [Cl^{-}] = \ln(0.1) = -2.3026
$$

**Step 5: Substitute and evaluate.**

$$
E = 0.2223 - (0.02671 \times 1) \times (-2.3026)
$$

$$
E = 0.2223 + 0.06151
$$

$$
E \approx 0.2838 V
$$

**Conclusion:** The half-cell potential of the Ag/AgCl electrode under these conditions is approximately **0.284 V** with respect to the standard hydrogen electrode (SHE).

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Bandwidth and Sampling Rate for EEG

**Given:** EEG signal frequency range = 0.5 Hz to 100 Hz. Determine the minimum sampling frequency (Nyquist rate).

**Step 1: Apply the Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem.**

$$
f_{s,\min} = 2 \times f_{max}
$$

**Step 2: Substitute the maximum EEG frequency.**

$$
f_{s,\min} = 2 \times 100 = 200 Hz
$$

**Step 3: In practice, a safety factor of 2.5 to 3 is applied.**

$$
f_{s,\text{used}} \approx 250 \text{ to } 300 Hz
$$

**Conclusion:** Clinical EEG systems typically sample at **256 Hz or 512 Hz** to provide adequate anti-aliasing margins.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Frequency Bands of EEG Rhythms

| Rhythm | Frequency Band | Associated State / Region | Typical Amplitude |
|--------|---------------|--------------------------|-------------------|
| Delta ($\delta$) | 0.5–4 Hz | Deep sleep (Stage 3/4), infants | 20–200 $\mu V$ |
| Theta ($\theta$) | 4–8 Hz | Drowsiness, light sleep, meditation | < 30 $\mu V$ |
| Alpha ($\alpha$) | 8–13 Hz | Relaxed wakefulness, eyes closed (occipital) | 20–60 $\mu V$ |
| Beta ($\beta$) | 13–30 Hz | Active thinking, alert state, anxiety | < 20 $\mu V$ |
| Gamma ($\gamma$) | > 30 Hz | Cognitive processing, perception binding | Very low |

> [!NOTE]
> **Clinical Insight:** Absence of alpha rhythm in an awake subject or excessive delta activity in an awake adult is indicative of neurological pathology (e.g., encephalopathy, tumor, or stroke).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram: EEG Signal Acquisition System

The figure below illustrates the complete signal flow from the brain to the digital display in a clinical EEG machine.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[BRAIN EEG SOURCE] --> B[SCALP SURFACE]
    B --> C[ELECTRODES 10-20 SYSTEM]
    C --> D[INPUT PROTECTION AND ELECTRODE CHECK]
    D --> E[INSTRUMENTATION AMPLIFIER HIGH CMRR]
    E --> F[BANDPASS FILTER 0.5 TO 100 HZ]
    F --> G[NOTCH FILTER 50 OR 60 HZ REJECTION]
    G --> H[ANALOG TO DIGITAL CONVERTER ADC]
    H --> I[DIGITAL SIGNAL PROCESSOR AVERAGING]
    I --> J[DISPLAY AND STORAGE MONITOR AND HDD]
    J --> K[CLINICAL INTERPRETATION]
    style A fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style K fill:#90EE90,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style E fill:#ADD8E6,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style H fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Functional Insights:**
> - The instrumentation amplifier provides the **first stage gain** with a high CMRR (typically > 100 dB) to reject common-mode noise.
> - The **notch filter** is critical for rejecting 50/60 Hz power line interference.
> - The ADC sampling rate must be at least **2.5 times** the highest frequency of interest.

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Evoked Potential Recording System

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[STIMULUS GENERATOR] --> B[SENSORY STIMULUS VISUAL AUDITORY OR SOMATOSENSORY]
    B --> C[PATIENT UNDER TEST]
    C --> D[EEG ELECTRODES ON SCALP]
    D --> E[DIFFERENTIAL AMPLIFIER]
    E --> F[FILTER AND GAIN STAGE]
    F --> G[ADC CONVERSION]
    G --> H[MICROPROCESSOR FOR AVERAGING N TRIALS]
    H --> I[DISPLAY EP WAVEFORM]
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style C fill:#FFA07A,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style H fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
    style I fill:#98FB98,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px
```

### 4.3 10-20 Electrode Placement Topology (Top View of Head)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    N[NZ NASION] --> Fp1[FP1 FRONTAL POLE LEFT]
    Fp1 --> F3[F3 FRONTAL LEFT]
    F3 --> C3[C3 CENTRAL LEFT]
    C3 --> P3[P3 PARIETAL LEFT]
    P3 --> O1[O1 OCCIPITAL LEFT]
    O1 --> IZ[IZ INION]
    N --> Fp2[FP2 FRONTAL POLE RIGHT]
    Fp2 --> F4[F4 FRONTAL RIGHT]
    F4 --> C4[C4 CENTRAL RIGHT]
    C4 --> P4[P4 PARIETAL RIGHT]
    P4 --> O2[O2 OCCIPITAL RIGHT]
    O2 --> IZ
    F3 --> FZ[FZ MIDLINE FRONTAL]
    FZ --> CZ[CZ MIDLINE CENTRAL REFERENCE]
    CZ --> PZ[PZ MIDLINE PARIETAL]
    C3 --> T3[T3 TEMPORAL LEFT]
    T3 --> T5[T5 POSTERIOR TEMPORAL LEFT]
    C4 --> T4[T4 TEMPORAL RIGHT]
    T4 --> T6[T6 POSTERIOR TEMPORAL RIGHT]
    A1[A1 LEFT EAR REFERENCE] --- T3
    A2[A2 RIGHT EAR REFERENCE] --- T4
    style CZ fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000,stroke-width:2px
    style FZ fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,stroke-width:1px

```

### 4.4 EMG Signal Acquisition Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[MUSCLE MOTOR UNIT] --> B[NEEDLE OR SURFACE ELECTRODE]
    B --> C[PREAMPLIFIER HIGH INPUT IMPEDANCE]
    C --> D[ISOLATION AMPLIFIER PATIENT SAFETY]
    D --> E[BANDPASS FILTER 20 HZ TO 5 KHZ]
    E --> F[RECTIFIER AND SMOOTHER]
    F --> G[ADC SAMPLING AT 1 KHZ OR HIGHER]
    G --> H[DISPLAY AND ANALYSIS SOFTWARE]
    style A fill:#FFE4E1,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px

```
