<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Question 1. [KTU University Exam – June 2023]**
*Explain the importance of the 10-20 system in EEG electrode placement. Mention the role of the reference electrodes A1 and A2.*

**Model Answer:**
The 10-20 system is a standardized international method for placing EEG electrodes on the scalp at measured intervals of 10% and 20% of head landmarks (Nasion to Inion and pre-auricular points). It ensures **reproducibility**, **uniform coverage** of all brain regions, and **comparison of data** across subjects and laboratories. The system uses 21 electrodes including the ground and reference. The reference electrodes **A1 and A2** (placed on the left and right ear lobes) serve as the common reference point against which active scalp electrodes measure potential differences. This system enables the recording of localized and global brain activity with high spatial consistency.

*(Marks distribution: Definition 1M, Procedure 1M, Role of A1/A2 1M)*

---

**Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
*What are evoked potentials? Why is signal averaging necessary for their recording?*

**Model Answer:**
Evoked Potentials (EPs) are the electrical responses of the nervous system to a specific sensory stimulus (visual, auditory, or somatosensory). They are time-locked, low-amplitude signals (typically 0.1–20 $\mu V$) that are completely buried in the spontaneous EEG background (10–100 $\mu V$). **Signal averaging** is required because a single trial cannot distinguish the small EP from the larger background noise. By averaging $N$ time-locked responses, the random noise averages to zero (improving SNR by $\sqrt{N}$), while the deterministic EP is preserved and clearly extracted.

*(Marks distribution: Definition 1M, Need for averaging 1M, $\sqrt{N}$ improvement 1M)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - In 10-20 system questions, students often confuse the even/odd numbering rule for hemispheres. **Remember: Even numbers (2,4,6,8) = RIGHT hemisphere; Odd numbers (1,3,5,7) = LEFT hemisphere.** This is a common 1–2 mark deduction.
> - In averaging derivations, students forget to mention that the noise must be **uncorrelated** and have **zero mean** for the averaging technique to work. Always state this assumption.
> - For EMG, do not confuse MUAP (single motor unit) with the compound EMG signal. Surface EMG records the **sum** of many MUAPs.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a) Describe the 10-20 international system of electrode placement for EEG recording with a neat diagram. Mention the naming convention and identify the locations of at least 8 standard electrodes. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The 10-20 system was standardized by the International Federation of EEG and Clinical Neurophysiology to provide a uniform method of electrode placement. The procedure is as follows:

1. **Anatomical Landmarks:**
   - **Nasion (Nz):** Bridge of the nose.
   - **Inion (Iz):** Bump at the back of the skull.
   - **Pre-auricular points (A1, A2):** Depressions in front of each ear.

2. **Measurement Procedure:**
   - Measure the distance **Nasion to Inion** along the scalp midline.
   - Mark points at **10%, 20%, 20%, 20%, 20%, 10%** of this distance to identify Fpz, Fz, Cz, Pz, Oz, and Iz.
   - Similarly, measure the **left pre-auricular to right pre-auricular** distance across Cz.
   - Mark 10%, 20%, 20%, 20%, 20%, 10% to locate T3, C3, Cz, C4, T4, and A2.

3. **Naming Convention:**
   - **Letter (region):** F (Frontal), C (Central), P (Parietal), T (Temporal), O (Occipital), A (Auricular).
   - **Subscript (hemisphere):** Even numbers (2,4,6,8) → Right; Odd numbers (1,3,5,7) → Left; 'z' → Midline.

4. **Eight Standard Electrodes and Their Locations:**
   - **Fpz:** Frontal pole, midline.
   - **F3:** Left frontal lobe.
   - **F4:** Right frontal lobe.
   - **C3:** Left central (motor cortex region).
   - **C4:** Right central.
   - **P3:** Left parietal lobe.
   - **O1:** Left occipital lobe (visual cortex).
   - **O2:** Right occipital lobe.

The total system uses **21 electrodes** including the ground and reference (A1, A2).

*(Marks: Definition + procedure 3M, Naming convention 2M, Eight electrodes 2M)*

---

**(b) With a block diagram, explain the EEG signal acquisition system. List the typical amplitude and frequency ranges of EEG signals. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram of EEG Acquisition System:**

```
[Brain Source] → [Scalp + Electrodes] → [Instrumentation Amplifier] 
   → [Bandpass Filter 0.5-100 Hz] → [Notch Filter 50/60 Hz] 
   → [ADC] → [DSP & Display]
```

**Functional Description of Each Block:**

1. **Electrodes (10-20 placement):** Pick up microvolt-level potentials from the scalp. Ag/AgCl electrodes are commonly used for their low offset voltage and stability.

2. **Instrumentation Amplifier:** Provides high differential gain (typically 1000–10,000) and a **CMRR > 100 dB** to reject common-mode noise (e.g., power line interference).

3. **Bandpass Filter:** Limits the signal to the EEG band of **0.5 Hz – 100 Hz**, removing DC offset and high-frequency muscle artifacts.

4. **Notch Filter:** Specifically rejects 50 Hz (or 60 Hz) power line interference with a narrow Q-factor.

5. **ADC (Analog-to-Digital Converter):** Digitizes the signal at a sampling rate of **256 Hz or higher** to satisfy the Nyquist criterion.

6. **DSP & Display:** Performs digital filtering, artifact rejection, and displays the multi-channel EEG waveform.

**Typical EEG Signal Parameters:**

- **Amplitude Range:** 10–100 $\mu V$ (can be up to 200 $\mu V$ in sleep).
- **Frequency Range:** 0.5–100 Hz (clinical focus: 0.5–30 Hz).
- **Major Rhythms:** Delta (0.5–4 Hz), Theta (4–8 Hz), Alpha (8–13 Hz), Beta (13–30 Hz), Gamma (> 30 Hz).

*(Marks: Block diagram 3M, Description 2M, Amplitude/frequency 2M)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a) Define Evoked Potentials. Explain the visual, auditory, and somatosensory evoked potentials with their clinical applications. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition:**
Evoked Potentials (EPs) are the electrical responses of the nervous system to a specific, controlled sensory stimulus. They are low-amplitude (0.1–20 $\mu V$), time-locked signals that are recorded from the scalp or spinal cord and extracted from the background EEG using **signal averaging**.

**Types of Evoked Potentials:**

1. **Visual Evoked Potentials (VEP):**
   - **Stimulus:** Flashing strobe light or alternating checkerboard pattern.
   - **Recording Site:** Occipital region (Oz).
   - **Key Waveform:** P100 (positive peak at ~100 ms latency).
   - **Clinical Application:** Detection of optic neuritis, multiple sclerosis (demyelination), and assessment of visual pathway integrity.

2. **Auditory Evoked Potentials (AEP / BAEP):**
   - **Stimulus:** Repetitive click sounds delivered through headphones.
   - **Recording Site:** Vertex (Cz) with ear-lobe reference.
   - **Key Waveforms:** I, III, V (Jewett Roman numerals) representing auditory nerve, pons, and midbrain.
   - **Clinical Application:** Hearing assessment in infants, detection of acoustic neuroma, and intraoperative monitoring of the brainstem.

3. **Somatosensory Evoked Potentials (SEP):**
   - **Stimulus:** Electrical stimulation of a peripheral nerve (e.g., median or tibial).
   - **Recording Sites:** Erb's point, cervical spine, and contralateral scalp (sensory cortex).
   - **Key Waveform:** N20 (negative peak at ~20 ms for upper limb).
   - **Clinical Application:** Monitoring spinal cord integrity during scoliosis surgery, detection of peripheral neuropathy, and diagnosis of multiple sclerosis.

*(Marks: Definition 1M, Three types 4M, Clinical applications 2M)*

---

**(b) Explain EMG signal acquisition. Discuss the differences between surface EMG and needle EMG. (7 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**EMG Signal Acquisition:**
Electromyography (EMG) is the recording of electrical activity generated by skeletal muscles during voluntary contraction or at rest. The signal originates from the depolarization of muscle fiber membranes. The acquisition system consists of:
- **Electrodes** (surface or needle) that sense the bioelectric potential.
- **Differential amplifier** with high input impedance (> 10^12 $\Omega$) and high CMRR.
- **Bandpass filter** (20 Hz – 5 kHz) to isolate the EMG bandwidth.
- **ADC and display** unit for recording and analysis.

**Comparison: Surface EMG vs Needle EMG:**

| Parameter | Surface EMG | Needle EMG |
|------------|-------------|------------|
| Invasiveness | Non-invasive (skin surface) | Invasive (penetrates muscle) |
| Electrode Type | Ag/AgCl disc electrodes | Concentric or monopolar needle |
| Recording Area | Large, superficial muscles | Deep, specific muscles or single motor units |
| Signal Detail | Compound/summed MUAPs | Individual MUAPs visible |
| Amplitude | 50 $\mu V$ – 5 mV | 100 $\mu V$ – 5 mV |
| Bandwidth | 20 – 500 Hz | 20 Hz – 5 kHz |
| Patient Comfort | High (painless) | Low (mild discomfort) |
| Application | Kinesiology, sports, prosthetics | Clinical neurophysiology, neuromuscular diagnosis |

**Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP) Parameters:**
- **Amplitude:** 100 $\mu V$ – 5 mV
- **Duration:** 3–15 ms
- **Phases:** 2–4 normal; > 4 polyphasic (abnormal)
- **Rise Time:** 0.5–2 ms

*(Marks: Acquisition explanation 2M, Comparison table 4M, MUAP parameters 1M)*

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Biomedical signal characteristics** are defined by amplitude (often $\mu V$ level), frequency (Hz to kHz), and SNR.
- **Electrodes** convert ionic currents in tissue to electronic currents; Ag/AgCl is the gold standard due to its low and stable half-cell potential.
- The **Nernst equation** $E = E^{0} + \frac{RT}{nF} \ln a_{ion}$ governs the half-cell potential of any electrochemical electrode.
- The **10-20 system** standardizes EEG electrode placement using 10% and 20% intervals from anatomical landmarks (Nasion, Inion, Pre-auricular points).
- **Electrode naming:** Letter = region (F/C/P/T/O/A), Subscript = hemisphere (Odd = Left, Even = Right, z = Midline).
- **Evoked Potentials** are tiny, time-locked sensory responses extracted from EEG by averaging $N$ trials; SNR improves by $\sqrt{N}$.
- **EP types:** VEP (visual), AEP/BAEP (auditory), SEP (somatosensory) — each with distinct clinical applications.
- **EEG Rhythms:** Delta (0.5–4 Hz, deep sleep), Theta (4–8 Hz), Alpha (8–13 Hz, relaxed), Beta (13–30 Hz, alert), Gamma (> 30 Hz).
- **EMG** records muscle electrical activity; **Surface EMG** = summed, non-invasive; **Needle EMG** = individual MUAPs, invasive.
- **CMRR** of instrumentation amplifier must be > 100 dB to reject 50/60 Hz power line interference.
- **Sampling rate** must satisfy Nyquist criterion: $f_s \geq 2.5 \times f_{max}$ (e.g., 256 Hz for EEG, 10 kHz for EMG).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Electrical Activity of Muscles
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT611 - BIOSENSORS & TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Electrical Activity of Muscles

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# ⚡ Electrical Activity of Muscles — Foundational Overview

## 1.1 Formal KTU-Syllabus Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Electrical Activity of Muscles** refers to the bioelectric phenomena generated by excitable muscle cells (skeletal, cardiac, and smooth muscle) during the transition from a polarized resting state to a depolarized active state. This activity is primarily studied through **Electromyography (EMG)** — the biomedical technique of recording, analyzing, and interpreting the electrical potentials evoked by voluntary or stimulated muscular contraction.

In KTU 2024 Scheme parlance, this topic sits at the intersection of **biopotential acquisition**, **transducer design**, and **signal conditioning** — a triad critical for the engineering of bioelectronic devices such as prosthetics, myoelectric controllers, and rehabilitation robotics.

The two principal sub-domains covered under the KTU Module 2 syllabus are:

| Sub-domain | Core Focus |
|---|---|
| **Cellular Electrophysiology of Muscle** | Resting membrane potential, action potential, ion-channel dynamics |
| **EMG Signal Acquisition & Characteristics** | Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP), EMG frequency/amplitude metrics, surface vs. needle recording |

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a muscle fiber as a tiny biological battery that briefly short-circuits when you decide to move.** At rest, it maintains a voltage of about **−90 mV** across its membrane — just like a charged AA battery sitting in a flashlight. The instant your brain sends a command through a motor neuron, sodium-ion "gates" on the membrane fling open. Positive ions flood in, the voltage inside shoots up to roughly **+30 mV**, and this electrical "wave" races along the fiber. When thousands of fibers fire near-simultaneously, their summed voltages create a measurable signal on the skin — that signal is the **EMG**.

A more concrete engineering analogy: imagine thousands of tiny 1.5 V cells wired in series and parallel inside a flashlight. When the switch (the motor neuron) is pressed, the cells discharge asynchronously, and the resulting current fluctuations on the flashlight's outer casing (the skin) are what we measure with electrodes.

## 1.3 Key Bioelectric Quantities at a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Reference Values (memorize for KTU exams):**
> - Muscle Resting Membrane Potential: **−90 mV** (intracellular relative to extracellular)
> - Muscle Action Potential Peak: **+30 mV** → net swing ≈ **120 mV**
> - EMG Signal Bandwidth: **20 Hz – 500 Hz** (clinical EMG extends to 2 kHz)
> - Surface EMG Amplitude: **50 µV – 5 mV** (peak-to-peak)
> - Intramuscular (Needle) EMG Amplitude: **100 µV – 10 mV**
> - Muscle Fiber Conduction Velocity: **2 – 6 m/s** (skeletal)
> - Motor Unit Firing Rate: **6 – 30 Hz** (voluntary contraction)

## 1.4 Anatomical Hierarchy of Skeletal Muscle

Understanding electrical activity requires tracing the structural hierarchy from the macroscopic organ down to the ion channel:

1. **Muscle** → bundle of fascicles
2. **Fascicle** → bundle of muscle fibers
3. **Muscle Fiber (Cell)** → single multinucleated cell, ~10–100 µm diameter, up to 30 cm long
4. **Myofibril** → contractile unit within fiber
5. **Sarcomere** → functional contractile unit, ~2–3 µm long
6. **Myosin & Actin Filaments** → molecular motors that generate force
7. **Sarcoplasmic Reticulum (SR)** → intracellular Ca²⁺ store
8. **Transverse Tubules (T-tubules)** → rapid AP propagation into the fiber interior
9. **Voltage-Gated Ion Channels (Na⁺, K⁺, Ca²⁺)** → molecular basis of excitability

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 🔬 Deep Theoretical Analysis — From Membrane to Surface Electrode

## 2.1 The Resting Membrane Potential — Ionic Basis

Every muscle fiber maintains a voltage difference across its plasma (sarcolemmal) membrane. This arises from three coupled phenomena:

- **Ionic concentration gradients** (set up by the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump, which expels 3 Na⁺ ions and imports 2 K⁺ ions per ATP hydrolyzed)
- **Selective membrane permeability** (at rest, the membrane is ~25× more permeable to K⁺ than Na⁺)
- **Fixed intracellular anions** (proteins, phosphates) that cannot cross the membrane

The Nernst equation gives the equilibrium potential for a single ion species:

$$
E_{\text{ion}} = \frac{RT}{zF} \ln\!\left(\frac{[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}}{[\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}}\right)
$$

where:
- $R$ = Universal gas constant = **8.314 J/(mol·K)**
- $T$ = Absolute temperature (310 K at body temperature)
- $z$ = Valency of the ion
- $F$ = Faraday constant = **96,485 C/mol**
- $[\text{ion}]_{\text{out}}, [\text{ion}]_{\text{in}}$ = Extracellular and intracellular concentrations

Since multiple ions are permeable simultaneously, the actual resting potential is described by the **Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) Voltage Equation**:

$$
V_m = \frac{RT}{F} \ln\!\left(\frac{P_K[K^+]_{\text{out}} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{\text{out}} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{\text{in}}}{P_K[K^+]_{\text{in}} + P_{Na}[Na^+]_{\text{in}} + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_{\text{out}}}\right)
$$

where $P_K, P_{Na}, P_{Cl}$ are the membrane permeabilities of the respective ions.

## 2.2 The Muscle Action Potential — Phases

When a motor neuron releases **acetylcholine (ACh)** at the neuromuscular junction, ACh binds to **nicotinic receptors** on the motor end-plate. This causes a local depolarization called the **End-Plate Potential (EPP)**. If the EPP reaches the threshold (~ −55 mV), it triggers a regenerative all-or-none action potential:

| Phase | Voltage Range | Dominant Ion Current | Channel Behavior |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Resting** | −90 mV | K⁺ leak (small) | K⁺ leak channels open; Na⁺ channels closed |
| **Depolarization** | −90 mV → +30 mV | Na⁺ influx | Voltage-gated Na⁺ channels open rapidly |
| **Overshoot** | +30 mV peak | Na⁺ (saturating) | Na⁺ channels begin inactivation |
| **Repolarization** | +30 mV → −90 mV | K⁺ efflux | Voltage-gated K⁺ channels open; Na⁺ channels inactivate |
| **Hyperpolarization (Afterpotential)** | ~ −95 mV | K⁺ (lingering) | K⁺ channels slow to close |
| **Return to Resting** | −95 mV → −90 mV | Na⁺/K⁺ pump | Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase restores ionic gradients |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why muscle APs differ slightly from nerve APs:** Skeletal muscle fibers possess **T-tubules** that propagate the AP deep into the fiber within milliseconds. The T-tubule membrane contains **dihydropyridine (DHP) receptors** that are mechanically coupled to **ryanodine receptors (RyR)** on the sarcoplasmic reticulum. This triad structure underlies **excitation-contraction coupling** — the conversion of electrical excitation into mechanical force.

## 2.3 Excitation–Contraction Coupling

1. AP travels along sarcolemma → into T-tubules
2. DHP receptors on T-tubule sense membrane depolarization
3. DHP receptors mechanically open Ryanodine Receptors (RyR2) on SR
4. Ca²⁺ floods from SR into the sarcoplasm (concentration rises from ~100 nM to ~10 µM)
5. Ca²⁺ binds **troponin C** → tropomyosin shifts → myosin heads can bind actin
6. Cross-bridge cycling produces force (sliding-filament model, Huxley 1957)
7. Ca²⁺ is resequestered into SR by **SERCA pumps** → muscle relaxes

## 2.4 The Motor Unit and MUAP

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Definition (KTU syllabus):** A **Motor Unit (MU)** is the smallest functional contractile unit and consists of **one alpha motor neuron + all the muscle fibers it innervates** (typically 5–2000 fibers, depending on the muscle's fine motor control requirement).

When a single motor unit fires, the summed activity of all its fibers produces a triphasic waveform known as the **Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP)**.

| Muscle Type | Typical Fibers per MU | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Extraocular | 5–10 | Eye muscles (precision) |
| Hand intrinsic | 100–300 | Lumbricals (fine control) |
| Biceps brachii | 750–1500 | Arm (gross movement) |
| Gastrocnemius | 1500–2500 | Calf (power) |

## 2.5 EMG Signal Generation — From MUAP to Surface EMG

The signal at the skin electrode is the **spatiotemporal summation of many MUAPs** from many active motor units. This is mathematically a **superposition principle**:

$$
\text{EMG}(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{N} \sum_{k} \text{MUAP}_i\!\left(t - t_{ik}\right) + n(t)
$$

where:
- $N$ = number of active motor units
- $t_{ik}$ = firing time of the $k$-th discharge of the $i$-th motor unit
- $n(t)$ = additive measurement noise (thermal, motion artefact, mains interference)

During a **voluntary contraction**, the nervous system modulates force by two simultaneous mechanisms:

- **Rate Coding:** increasing the firing frequency of individual motor units (8 → 25 Hz)
- **Recruitment:** activating additional, larger motor units (size principle of Henneman, 1957)

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!TIP]
> **Master every row in this table — at least one formula from this list appears in nearly every KTU University Exam question on this module.**

| # | Formula / Relationship | Description | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $E_{\text{ion}} = \dfrac{RT}{zF}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{[\text{ion}]_o}{[\text{ion}]_i}\right)$ | Nernst equilibrium potential for a single ion | Volts (V) |
| 2 | $V_m = \dfrac{RT}{F}\ln\!\left(\dfrac{P_K[K^+]_o + P_{Na}[Na^+]_o + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_i}{P_K[K^+]_i + P_{Na}[Na^+]_i + P_{Cl}[Cl^-]_o}\right)$ | Goldman-Hodgkin-Katz (GHK) resting potential | Volts (V) |
| 3 | $\dfrac{RT}{F}\Big\vert_{310\,\text{K}} = 0.02569$ V $\approx$ **26.69 mV** | Thermal voltage at body temperature | Volts (V) |
| 4 | $V_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^2(t)\,dt}$ | RMS value of EMG signal — proxy for muscle activation | Volts (V) |
| 5 | $P(f) = \lim_{T\to\infty}\dfrac{1}{2T}\,\big\vert \text{FT}\{v(t)\}\big\vert^2$ | Power Spectral Density (Welch's method used in practice) | V²/Hz |
| 6 | $f_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | EMG amplifier high-pass / low-pass cutoff frequency | Hz |
| 7 | $\text{CMF} = \text{CMRR}_{\text{dB}} = 20\log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_d}{A_{cm}}\right)$ | Common-Mode Rejection Ratio (instrumentation amp) | dB |
| 8 | $F = k \cdot V_{\text{rms}} + c$ | Linear EMG-to-Force calibration model (approximate) | Newtons (N) |
| 9 | $v_{\text{cond}} = \dfrac{d}{\Delta t}$ | Muscle fiber conduction velocity ($d$ = inter-electrode distance) | m/s |
| 10 | $\text{MAV} = \dfrac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N}\,\big\vert x_i \big\vert$ | Mean Absolute Value — simple EMG amplitude feature | V |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility of these equations:**
> - (1)–(3) underpin **nerve/muscle simulation** (Hodgkin-Huxley model, NEURON software)
> - (4) and (10) are used in **prosthetic limb controllers** (myoelectric pattern recognition)
> - (5) gives the **frequency content** used in **muscle fatigue analysis** (median frequency shift)
> - (7) is critical for **EMG amplifier design** — must exceed 80 dB (typically > 100 dB) at 50/60 Hz to reject mains interference

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 🧮 Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Solutions

## 3.1 Derivation of the Nernst Equation

We derive the Nernst equation from first principles using electrochemical equilibrium.

**Step 1 — Identify the two opposing tendencies.**
Consider a membrane separating two ionic solutions of concentration $[C]_o$ (outside) and $[C]_i$ (inside). Ions experience two forces:
- **Chemical force** → drives ions down their concentration gradient (diffusion)
- **Electrical force** → drives charged ions through the membrane electric field

**Step 2 — Set up the electrochemical potential.**
The free energy change per mole of ion transported from inside to outside is:

$$
\Delta G = RT \ln\!\left(\frac{[C]_o}{[C]_i}\right) + zF E
$$

where $E$ is the membrane potential (outside minus inside). The first term is the chemical contribution, the second is the electrical contribution.

**Step 3 — Apply equilibrium condition.**
At equilibrium, there is no net flux, hence $\Delta G = 0$:

$$
RT \ln\!\left(\frac{[C]_o}{[C]_i}\right) + zF E = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for $E$.**
Rearranging:

$$
zF E = -RT \ln\!\left(\frac{[C]_o}{[C]_i}\right) = RT \ln\!\left(\frac{[C]_i}{[C]_o}\right)
$$

$$
E = \frac{RT}{zF}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{[C]_o}{[C]_i}\right)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **Nernst Equation**. At 310 K (37 °C), the prefactor $\dfrac{RT}{F} = 0.02569$ V = **26.69 mV**.

## 3.2 Worked Example — Calculating the K⁺ Equilibrium Potential of a Muscle Cell

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard muscle fiber intracellular/extracellular ion concentrations (mM):**
> - $[K^+]_i = 140$ mM; $[K^+]_o = 4$ mM
> - $[Na^+]_i = 12$ mM; $[Na^+]_o = 145$ mM
> - $[Cl^-]_i = 4$ mM (muscle); $[Cl^-]_o = 120$ mM
> - $T = 310$ K; $z_K = +1$

**Step 1 — Substitute into the Nernst equation for K⁺:**

$$
E_{K^+} = \frac{RT}{zF}\,\ln\!\left(\frac{[K^+]_o}{[K^+]_i}\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Plug in the thermal voltage prefactor:**

$$
E_{K^+} = 0.02569 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{4}{140}\right) \text{ V}
$$

**Step 3 — Evaluate the logarithm:**

$$
\ln\!\left(\frac{4}{140}\right) = \ln(0.02857) = -3.555
$$

**Step 4 — Multiply:**

$$
E_{K^+} = 0.02569 \times (-3.555) = -0.0913 \text{ V} = \mathbf{-91.3 \text{ mV}}
$$

This matches the experimentally observed **muscle resting potential of −90 mV**, confirming that at rest, K⁺ permeability dominates and the membrane sits very close to $E_{K^+}$.

## 3.3 Derivation of the RMS EMG Feature

The RMS (Root Mean Square) value is the most common amplitude feature in EMG signal processing. It correlates strongly with muscle force and is computed over a sliding window.

**Step 1 — Define the mean square.**
For a continuous EMG signal $v(t)$ over interval $[0,T]$:

$$
\langle v^2 \rangle = \frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^2(t)\,dt
$$

**Step 2 — Take the square root to obtain RMS:**

$$
V_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^2(t)\,dt}
$$

**Step 3 — Discretize for digital implementation.**
If the EMG is sampled at $f_s$ yielding $N$ samples $v_1, v_2, \dots, v_N$ in window $T$:

$$
V_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} v_i^{\,2}}
$$

**Step 4 — Typical computation example.**
Suppose a 200 ms window sampled at 1 kHz ($N = 200$). For a sample of recorded EMG with $\sum v_i^2 = 5.2 \times 10^{-4}$ V²:

$$
V_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{5.2 \times 10^{-4}}{200}} = \sqrt{2.6 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.612 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} \approx \mathbf{1.61 \text{ mV}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why RMS over peak-to-peak?** RMS reflects the signal *power* and is robust to single-sample spikes, motion artefacts, and outliers. The KTU syllabus specifically highlights RMS as the **gold-standard amplitude estimator** for surface EMG.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Muscle Fiber Conduction Velocity

**Given:** A bipolar surface electrode pair separated by $d = 30$ mm records the same MUAP at times $t_1$ and $t_2 = t_1 + 7.5$ ms.

**Step 1 — Apply the conduction velocity formula:**

$$
v_{\text{cond}} = \frac{d}{\Delta t} = \frac{d}{t_2 - t_1}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$
v_{\text{cond}} = \frac{30 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}}{7.5 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{s}} = 4.0 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 3 — Verify against physiological range.**

This value lies within the normal range for skeletal muscle (2–6 m/s) ✓

## 3.5 Comparative Summary Matrix — Surface vs. Needle EMG

| Parameter | Surface EMG (sEMG) | Needle EMG (nEMG) |
|---|---|---|
| Electrode | Skin-mounted Ag/AgCl | Concentric/needle inserted into muscle |
| Pickup Volume | Large (many motor units) | Small (1–5 motor units) |
| Amplitude | 50 µV – 5 mV | 100 µV – 10 mV |
| Bandwidth | 20–500 Hz | 20 Hz – 5 kHz |
| Invasiveness | Non-invasive | Invasive (minor) |
| Clinical Use | Kinesiology, prosthetics, sports | Neuropathy, myopathy diagnosis |
| KTU Topic | ✓ Yes (focus) | ✓ Yes (overview) |

## 3.6 Analytical Hierarchy of Muscle Electrical Events (Time-Scale Map)

| Event | Duration | Frequency Band | Sensor/Method |
|---|---|---|---|
| Single ion channel opening | 1–10 ms | DC–10 kHz | Patch-clamp |
| End-plate potential (EPP) | 10–30 ms | DC–500 Hz | Microelectrode |
| Single MUAP | 2–10 ms | 10 Hz – 5 kHz | Needle EMG |
| Single muscle fiber AP | 1–2 ms | DC – 10 kHz | Intracellular electrode |
| Motor unit discharge train | Continuous, 6–30 Hz rate | 6 – 30 Hz | Surface EMG |
| Voluntary contraction burst | 100 ms – several seconds | 0.1 Hz – 500 Hz | sEMG |
| Muscle fatigue (median freq. shift) | Seconds to minutes | Power spectrum | sEMG with PSD |

# 🗺️ Structural & Functional Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Signal Pathway from Motor Neuron to EMG Output

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Brain Motor Cortex - Voluntary Command] --> B[Descending Corticospinal Tract]
    B --> C[Alpha Motor Neuron in Spinal Cord]
    C --> D[Axon Action Potential - Conduction Velocity 50 to 120 m per s]
    D --> E[Neuromuscular Junction - Acetylcholine Release]
    E --> F[Motor End Plate - Nicotinic Receptor Activation]
    F --> G[End Plate Potential - Local Depolarization]
    G --> H{Threshold Reached around minus 55 mV?}
    H -- Yes --> I[Muscle Fiber Action Potential - All or None]
    H -- No --> G
    I --> J[Propagation along Sarcolemma into T Tubules]
    J --> K[Calcium Release from Sarcoplasmic Reticulum]
    K --> L[Excitation Contraction Coupling - Cross Bridge Cycling]
    L --> M[Mechanical Force Generation - Sliding Filament]
    M --> N[Summed MUAPs of Active Motor Units]
    N --> O[Volume Conductor - Muscle, Fat, Skin Tissue]
    O --> P[Surface Electrode Pair Ag or AgCl]
    P --> Q[Differential Instrumentation Amplifier CMRR above 100 dB]
    Q --> R[Bandpass Filter 20 Hz to 500 Hz]
    R --> S[ADC Sampling at 1 kHz or Higher]
    S --> T[RMS Envelope or Power Spectrum]
    T --> U[Clinical or Prosthetic Application Output]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid State Diagram — Action Potential Phases

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Resting
    Resting: RESTING POTENTIAL around minus 90 mV
    Resting: K plus leak dominant
    Resting: Na plus channels closed

    Resting --> Depolarization: Stimulus reaches threshold
    Depolarization: DEPOLARIZATION
    Depolarization: Voltage gated Na plus channels open
    Depolarization: Rapid Na plus influx
    Depolarization: Vm rises from minus 90 to plus 30 mV

    Depolarization --> Overshoot: Vm crosses 0 mV
    Overshoot: OVERSHOOT
    Overshoot: Vm peaks at plus 30 mV
    Overshoot: Na plus channels inactivate

    Overshoot --> Repolarization: K plus channels open
    Repolarization: REPOLARIZATION
    Repolarization: K plus efflux
    Repolarization: Vm falls back toward minus 90 mV

    Repolarization --> Hyperpolarization: K plus channels remain open briefly
    Hyperpolarization: HYPERPOLARIZATION
    Hyperpolarization: Vm dips to about minus 95 mV

    Hyperpolarization --> Resting: Na K pump restores gradients
    Resting --> [*]
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — EMG Acquisition Front-End

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    E1[Electrode 1 on Skin] --> DA[INA128 Instrumentation Amplifier Gain 1000 CMRR 120 dB]
    E2[Electrode 2 on Skin] --> DA
    E3[Reference Electrode Right Leg Driver] --> DA
    DA --> HPF[First Order High Pass Filter Cutoff 20 Hz]
    HPF --> LPF[Fourth Order Low Pass Filter Cutoff 500 Hz]
    LPF --> NB[Notch Filter 50 Hz or 60 Hz Mains Rejection]
    NB --> GAIN[Programmable Gain Stage 1 to 100]
    GAIN --> ADC[16 bit ADC Sampling at 1 kHz or 2 kHz]
    ADC --> DSP[Digital Signal Processor]
    DSP --> DISPLAY[Display Envelope or Spectrum]
    DSP --> ACT[Actuator Prosthetic or Robot Control]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Motor Unit Recruitment vs. Rate Coding

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    FORCE[Required Muscle Force] --> CTRL[Central Nervous System Controller]

    CTRL --> RC[Rate Coding]
    CTRL --> RP[Recruitment]

    RC --> RT[Firing Frequency 8 to 25 Hz]
    RT --> TWT[Temporal Summation of MUAPs]

    RP --> SP[Size Principle - Smallest MUs Recruited First]
    SP --> LD[Progressively Larger MUs Added]
    LD --> SWT[Spatial Summation of Additional MUAPs]

    TWT --> EMG[Resulting EMG Signal Amplitude]
    SWT --> EMG
    EMG --> FOUT[Output Force]
```

# 📝 KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Alert — Where students typically lose marks:**
> 1. Confusing **resting potential sign conventions** (intracellular vs. extracellular reference).
> 2. Forgetting to convert the natural log in the Nernst equation to base-10 ($\ln 10 \approx 2.303$).
> 3. Using the Nernst equation (single ion) when the GHK equation (multiple ions) is required.
> 4. Stating EMG bandwidth as "0–500 Hz" — the **lower cutoff is 20 Hz**, not DC, to remove motion artefact.
> 5. Confusing **RMS** (power-related) with **MAV** (mean absolute value) and **peak-to-peak** (max-min).
> 6. Mixing up the **units of conduction velocity** (m/s) and **firing rate** (Hz or pulses/s).

---

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question] 
*Define the term "Motor Unit". What is a Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP) and what are its typical amplitude and duration ranges when recorded by surface electrodes?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Motor Unit (1 mark):** A motor unit is defined as one alpha motor neuron together with all the muscle fibers it innervates. It is the smallest functional contractile unit that can be voluntarily activated.

> **MUAP (1 mark):** A Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP) is the spatially and temporally summed electrical waveform generated by the near-simultaneous depolarization of all muscle fibers belonging to a single motor unit, recorded either from the muscle surface or with a needle electrode.

> **Typical ranges (1 mark):** When recorded by **surface electrodes**, a MUAP typically has an amplitude in the range of **50 µV to 5 mV** (peak-to-peak) and a total duration of approximately **2 to 10 ms**. With intramuscular needle electrodes, amplitudes may reach up to 10 mV, and the duration can extend to 5–15 ms.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question]
*List the major ions responsible for the resting membrane potential of a skeletal muscle fiber. Which ion is dominant and why?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Major ions (1.5 marks):** The four principal ions involved are **Na⁺, K⁺, Cl⁻, and Ca²⁺**, with the first three being quantitatively most important for the resting potential. Intracellular and extracellular concentrations differ markedly: $[K^+]_i = 140$ mM vs $[K^+]_o = 4$ mM; $[Na^+]_i = 12$ mM vs $[Na^+]_o = 145$ mM.

> **Dominant ion (1.5 marks):** **Potassium (K⁺) dominates** the resting membrane potential. This is because at rest, the sarcolemma is approximately **25 to 100 times more permeable to K⁺** than to Na⁺ (the GHK equation shows that the ion with the highest relative permeability contributes most strongly to $V_m$). The membrane potential therefore sits close to the **Nernst potential for K⁺** (≈ −91 mV at 37 °C, body temperature).

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Internal Choice Rule:** For every 14-mark question, the paper provides either–or choice. **Attempt either Option A or Option B in full.** Each option is split into two 7-mark sub-parts.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2, Compulsory Module Topic]

**A (a)** Explain the ionic basis of the resting membrane potential in a skeletal muscle fiber. Derive the Nernst equation and compute the equilibrium potential for K⁺ at body temperature (37 °C) given $[K^+]_i = 140$ mM and $[K^+]_o = 4$ mM. State the standard resting potential value for skeletal muscle. **\[7 marks\]**

**A (b)** Describe the phases of a skeletal muscle action potential in detail. Draw a labelled waveform showing the time-voltage relationship and state the ions responsible for each phase. **\[7 marks\]**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — A (a)

**Step 1 — Ionic basis of resting potential (2 marks):**

The resting membrane potential of a skeletal muscle fiber is established and maintained by:
- The **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump**, which actively expels 3 Na⁺ ions and imports 2 K⁺ ions per ATP hydrolyzed, creating steep ionic gradients ($[K^+]_i \gg [K^+]_o$ and $[Na^+]_o \gg [Na^+]_i$).
- The **higher permeability of the sarcolemma to K⁺** (≈ 25–100× greater than to Na⁺), causing K⁺ to diffuse outward down its concentration gradient, leaving the cell interior electrically negative.
- The presence of **fixed intracellular anions** (negatively charged proteins, phosphates) that cannot cross the membrane.

**Step 2 — Derivation of the Nernst equation (3 marks):**

The derivation was shown in Section 3.1. Key steps: write $\Delta G = RT \ln([C]_o/[C]_i) + zFE$, set $\Delta G = 0$ at equilibrium, and solve:

$$
E_{\text{ion}} = \frac{RT}{zF}\ln\!\left(\frac{[C]_o}{[C]_i}\right)
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical computation (1.5 marks):**

At $T = 310$ K, $z = +1$:

$$
E_{K^+} = \frac{8.314 \times 310}{1 \times 96485}\ln\!\left(\frac{4}{140}\right) = 0.02569 \times (-3.555) = -0.0913 \text{ V}
$$

$$
E_{K^+} \approx \mathbf{-91.3 \text{ mV}}
$$

**Step 4 — Resting potential value (0.5 marks):**

The standard experimentally measured resting potential of a skeletal muscle fiber is **−90 mV** (intracellular relative to extracellular).

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — A (b)

**The six phases (each phase carries approximately 1 mark, totalling 6 marks, with 1 additional mark for the labelled diagram):**

1. **Resting state (V_m = −90 mV):** Dominated by K⁺ leak current; Na⁺ channels are closed. Maintained by the Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase pump.

2. **Depolarization (V_m rises from −90 mV toward +30 mV):** Voltage-gated **Na⁺ channels** open rapidly, causing a large **Na⁺ influx**. This positive-feedback opening is the basis of the all-or-none law.

3. **Overshoot (V_m = +30 mV):** Na⁺ channel activation reaches saturation; inactivation gate begins to close.

4. **Repolarization (V_m falls from +30 mV back to −90 mV):** Voltage-gated **K⁺ channels** open; **K⁺ efflux** restores the negative interior.

5. **Hyperpolarization / Afterpotential (V_m ≈ −95 mV):** K⁺ channels remain briefly open, causing a transient undershoot.

6. **Return to resting:** The **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase** restores the original ionic gradients against their concentration gradients (active transport, consumes ATP).

**Labelled waveform diagram (1 mark) — textual representation:**

```
  Vm (mV)
   +30 │            ╭──╮  ← Overshoot
       │           ╱    ╲
     0 │─────────╱──────╲────────────  Threshold (-55 mV)
       │        ╱        ╲
    -55 │───────╳─────────╳──────────  (Threshold line)
       │      ╱            ╲
    -90 │─────╱──────────────╲───────  Resting potential
       │    ╱                 ╲
    -95 │                       ╲___  Hyperpolarization
       └──────────────────────────────── t (ms)
            Rest Depol  Repol  Hyper
```

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024, Module 2, Alternative Choice]

**B (a)** What is Electromyography (EMG)? With a neat block diagram, describe the surface EMG signal acquisition system. List the specifications of a typical biomedical amplifier used for EMG. **\[7 marks\]**

**B (b)** Define the RMS value of an EMG signal. For a sampled EMG window of duration 200 ms with $N = 200$ samples, the sum of squared sample values is $\sum v_i^2 = 4.8 \times 10^{-4}$ V². Calculate the RMS value. What is the typical frequency range of surface EMG and why is it band-limited to that range? **\[7 marks\]**

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — B (a)

**Step 1 — Definition of EMG (1.5 marks):**

> **Electromyography (EMG)** is the biomedical technique of recording, displaying, and interpreting the electrical activity generated by skeletal muscles during voluntary or electrically evoked contraction. The signal recorded is the sum of Motor Unit Action Potentials (MUAPs) propagated through the surrounding volume conductor and picked up by electrodes on the skin (surface EMG) or inserted into the muscle (needle EMG).

**Step 2 — Block diagram description (3.5 marks):**

The signal pathway (also see Section 4.3):

1. **Electrodes** (Ag/AgCl surface electrodes) → convert ionic skin potentials to electrical voltage
2. **Differential Instrumentation Amplifier (INA128/AD620)** → high CMRR (> 100 dB), gain ~ 1000
3. **High-Pass Filter (f_c ≈ 20 Hz)** → removes DC offset, motion artefact, electrode half-cell potentials
4. **Low-Pass Filter (f_c ≈ 500 Hz)** → anti-aliasing, removes high-frequency noise
5. **Notch Filter (50/60 Hz)** → rejects mains interference
6. **Programmable Gain Stage** → adapts to different muscle groups
7. **ADC (16-bit, ≥ 1 kHz sampling)** → digital conversion
8. **DSP/Microcontroller** → envelope detection (RMS, MAV), spectral analysis, pattern recognition

**Step 3 — Specifications of a typical biomedical EMG amplifier (2 marks):**

| Specification | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| Input Impedance | > 10 MΩ (preferably > 100 MΩ) |
| CMRR | > 100 dB at 50/60 Hz |
| Input-Referred Noise | < 1 µV RMS |
| Bandwidth | 20 Hz – 500 Hz (sEMG); 20 Hz – 5 kHz (nEMG) |
| Gain | 100 – 10,000 (programmable) |
| Power Supply | Battery-powered (patient isolation) |
| Safety Compliance | IEC 60601-1 |

---

#### ✅ Model Solution — B (b)

**Step 1 — Definition of RMS (2 marks):**

> The **Root Mean Square (RMS) value** of an EMG signal is the square root of the time-averaged squared amplitude, computed over a defined analysis window. It is the standard amplitude feature in EMG signal processing because it correlates well with muscle force and is mathematically equivalent to the signal's standard deviation for a zero-mean EMG.

$$
V_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} v_i^{\,2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Numerical calculation (2.5 marks):**

Given $\sum v_i^2 = 4.8 \times 10^{-4}$ V² and $N = 200$:

$$
V_{\text{rms}} = \sqrt{\frac{4.8 \times 10^{-4}}{200}} = \sqrt{2.4 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.549 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V}
$$

$$
V_{\text{rms}} \approx \mathbf{1.55 \text{ mV}}
$$

**Step 3 — Typical frequency range and justification (2.5 marks):**

- **Typical surface EMG bandwidth: 20 Hz to 500 Hz.**
- **Lower cutoff (20 Hz) justification:** Rejects DC electrode offset, motion artefact, and baseline wander caused by cable sway. Below 20 Hz, the EMG signal-to-noise ratio degrades severely.
- **Upper cutoff (500 Hz) justification:** Surface EMG has very little useful energy above 500 Hz because tissue acts as a low-pass filter, attenuating higher-frequency components. Setting the cutoff to 500 Hz also satisfies the **Nyquist sampling criterion** for a 1 kHz ADC.
- For needle (intramuscular) EMG, the upper cutoff is extended to 5 kHz to capture sharp MUAP spikes.

---

## 🧠 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute KTU revision checklist — verify each point before entering the exam hall:**

- ✅ **Resting potential of skeletal muscle = −90 mV** (intracellular relative to extracellular)
- ✅ **Peak of muscle action potential = +30 mV** → total swing ≈ **120 mV**
- ✅ **Nernst equation** uses **natural logarithm**; conversion to base-10: multiply by 2.303
- ✅ **Thermal voltage at 37 °C (310 K) = 26.69 mV** (this value appears in numerical problems)
- ✅ **K⁺ is the dominant ion at rest** because membrane permeability to K⁺ ≫ Na⁺
- ✅ **Motor unit = 1 motor neuron + all its innervated fibers** (5 to 2000 fibers)
- ✅ **MUAP amplitude (surface) = 50 µV – 5 mV**; **duration = 2–10 ms**
- ✅ **Surface EMG bandwidth = 20 Hz – 500 Hz**
- ✅ **sEMG uses Ag/AgCl electrodes**; needle EMG uses concentric needle electrodes
- ✅ **Instrumentation amplifier must have CMRR > 100 dB** to reject mains interference
- ✅ **RMS is the gold-standard EMG amplitude feature** (NOT peak-to-peak, NOT MAV)
- ✅ **Henneman's Size Principle:** smaller motor units recruited first; larger ones added as force demand increases
- ✅ **Two mechanisms of force modulation:** rate coding (8–30 Hz) + recruitment (number of active MUs)
- ✅ **Excitation–Contraction Coupling** uses **DHP-Ryanodine receptor mechanical coupling** in T-tubules
- ✅ **Conduction velocity of muscle fiber = 2–6 m/s** (much slower than nerve axon 50–120 m/s)
- ✅ **Na⁺/K⁺-ATPase** consumes 1 ATP per cycle (pumps 3 Na⁺ out, 2 K⁺ in) — accounts for a large fraction of resting metabolic rate
- ✅ **T-tubules** are invaginations of the sarcolemma that rapidly propagate AP into the fiber interior
- ✅ **GHK equation (multi-ion) must be used** when the question specifies multiple permeant ions — never use plain Nernst in such cases
- ✅ **Sign convention:** depolarization is a *rise* in $V_m$ (e.g., from −90 mV toward 0); hyperpolarization is a *fall* (e.g., to −95 mV)
- ✅ **EMG applications:** clinical diagnosis (neuropathy/myopathy), prosthetics (myoelectric control), ergonomics, sports biomechanics, rehabilitation robotics

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Characteristics of EMG Signal. Electrodes for EMG Acquisition
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT611 - BIOSENSORS & TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Characteristics of EMG Signal. Electrodes for EMG Acquisition

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Electromyography (EMG) — The Big Picture

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Standard):** *Electromyography (EMG) is the bioelectrical measurement technique that records, analyses, and interprets the electrical activity produced by skeletal muscles during voluntary or evoked contraction. The acquired time-varying voltage signal is termed the **Electromyogram (EMG signal)**, and it represents the spatial and temporal summation of **Motor Unit Action Potentials (MUAPs)** generated by groups of active motor units.*

The physiological source of an EMG signal is the **de-polarization and re-polarization** of skeletal muscle fibre membranes. When the brain (via the corticospinal tract) sends a voluntary command, the lower motor neuron in the spinal cord fires an **Action Potential (AP)**. This AP travels down the axon and triggers the release of **acetylcholine (ACh)** at the neuromuscular junction, which in turn causes the innervated muscle fibres to depolarize. The combined electrical activity of all the fibres belonging to a single motor unit gives rise to one **MUAP**, and the algebraic summation of several asynchronously firing MUAPs produces the surface/intramuscular EMG signal we record.

### 🎯 Conceptual Analogy — "The Orchestra" Intuition

Imagine a large orchestra (your biceps muscle) being conducted (by the brain). Each section — violins, cellos, trumpets — is a **motor unit** (a motor neuron + all the muscle fibres it controls). When the conductor signals *forte*, the violins don't all play the same note at the same instant; instead, each violinist starts at a slightly different time with a slightly different pitch. The sound that reaches your ear in the audience is the **sum of all those individual violin notes** — that's your **EMG signal**. The conductor is the **descending neural drive**, the violins are the **motor units**, and the microphone placed in the audience is the **EMG electrode**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (Module-2):** For KTU 2024, you are expected to know (i) the **time-domain and frequency-domain characteristics** of the EMG signal, (ii) the **types of electrodes** used for EMG acquisition, and (iii) the **electrode-skin interface and noise considerations**.

---

## 1.2 Why This Topic Matters in Biosensor Design

An EMG sensor chain is one of the most demanding bioinstrumentation problems because:

1. The signal amplitude is **microvolt-to-millivolt** range — extremely small.
2. The frequency content overlaps with **mains hum (50 Hz)**, **ECG** (QRS at ~1 Hz fundamental, but rich harmonics), and **motion artefact** (DC–20 Hz).
3. The source impedance of the muscle is **non-linear and time-varying**.
4. The electrode-tissue interface introduces a **half-cell potential** and a **polarization impedance** that must be carefully managed.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference Value (KTU Board Favourite):** A typical surface EMG (sEMG) signal has an amplitude in the range of **0.1 mV – 5 mV** (peak-to-peak), while an intramuscular EMG (iEMG) from a needle electrode can reach **10 mV** peak-to-peak. The dominant spectral energy lies between **20 Hz and 500 Hz**, with **50 Hz – 150 Hz** carrying the most clinical information.

---

## 1.3 Definitions of the Key Building Blocks

| Term | Definition | Significance |
|------|-----------|--------------|
| **Motor Unit (MU)** | A single alpha motor neuron + all the muscle fibres it innervates | The fundamental physiological "building block" of EMG |
| **Motor Unit Action Potential (MUAP)** | The biphasic or triphasic voltage waveform produced when all fibres of one MU fire once | The atomic unit of the EMG signal |
| **Recruitment** | The progressive activation of additional motor units as muscle force increases | Determines EMG amplitude |
| **Rate Coding** | Increase in the firing rate of already-active motor units | Determines EMG frequency content |
| **Interference Pattern** | The dense, complex waveform seen at strong voluntary contraction when many MUs fire asynchronously | The "fully recruited" EMG |

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Aid:** "**A**mplitude comes from **R**ecruitment, **F**requency comes from **R**ate-coding" — a popular KTU viva question. Remember **ARFR**.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Time-Domain Characteristics of the EMG Signal

The EMG signal is a **stochastic, zero-mean, non-stationary** random process under most practical conditions. The following time-domain descriptors are routinely used in KTU 2024 assessments:

### 2.1.1 Amplitude
- **Surface EMG (sEMG):** $\approx \mathbf{0.1 \text{ mV} - 5 \text{ mV}}$ (peak-to-peak). 
- **Needle EMG:** Can reach $\mathbf{1 \text{ mV} - 10 \text{ mV}}$ (peak-to-peak) due to closer coupling.
- **Micro-electrode (intracellular):** $\approx \mathbf{-90 \text{ mV}}$ resting, $\approx \mathbf{+30 \text{ mV}}$ peak (resting-to-peak $\sim \mathbf{120 \text{ mV}}$).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **Root Mean Square (RMS)** amplitude is preferred over the raw rectified value because it correlates linearly with muscle force over a wide dynamic range. The **Average Rectified Value (ARV)** is also used in clinical KTU problems.

### 2.1.2 Duration
- A single MUAP lasts **2 ms – 15 ms**.
- The full interference pattern can last for the entire duration of a sustained contraction (seconds to minutes).

### 2.1.3 Zero-Crossing Rate & Turns
- A measure of the number of times the signal crosses the zero baseline per unit time.
- Indicates the **dominant frequency** and the **level of MU recruitment**.

### 2.1.4 Signal-to-Noise Ratio (SNR)
- For a clean sEMG, SNR is typically **$\mathbf{\ge 20 \text{ dB}}$** after proper filtering.
- A high SNR is the first requirement for any usable bioelectric recording.

---

## 2.2 Frequency-Domain Characteristics of the EMG Signal

### 2.2.1 Bandwidth
- **Clinical EMG:** $\mathbf{20 \text{ Hz} - 500 \text{ Hz}}$
- **Surface EMG (sEMG) — informative band:** $\mathbf{20 \text{ Hz} - 150 \text{ Hz}}$
- **Needle EMG (clinical neurography):** $\mathbf{2 \text{ Hz} - 10 \text{ kHz}}$
- The Power Spectral Density (PSD) is **Gaussian-shaped**, peaking at $\mathbf{70 \text{ Hz} - 130 \text{ Hz}}$.

### 2.2.2 Median Frequency & Mean Frequency
- **Median Frequency ($f_{med}$):** The frequency that divides the PSD into two equal-power halves. Decreases with muscle fatigue.
- **Mean Frequency ($f_{mean}$):** First statistical moment of the PSD.

### 2.2.3 Sources of Frequency Content

| Source | Frequency Range | Influence |
|--------|-----------------|-----------|
| MUAP shape & firing rate | 20 – 150 Hz | Dominant information |
| Muscle fibre conduction velocity | 50 – 200 Hz | Determines spectral shape |
| Electrode-tissue interface | 0.1 – 10 Hz | Baseline wander |
| Mains interference | 50 Hz (India) / 60 Hz (USA) | Notch filter required |
| ECG cross-talk (chest muscles) | 0.5 – 40 Hz | Contaminant |
| Motion artefact | 0.1 – 20 Hz | Contaminant |

---

## 2.3 Noise and Artefacts in EMG

The EMG signal is corrupted by **five** principal noise sources, all of which are KTU-favourite viva topics:

1. **Inherent electrical noise of the electrode-electrolyte interface** (Johnson-Nyquist thermal noise).
2. **Ambient electromagnetic interference (EMI)**, especially **50 Hz mains hum**.
3. **Cross-talk** from adjacent muscles (a major problem in surface EMG).
4. **Motion artefact** at the electrode-skin interface.
5. **Physiological cross-talk** from ECG, EEG, and EOG.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Hot-Spot:** A common question is *"Why do we use a differential amplifier with high CMRR in EMG?"* — The answer is that a differential amplifier rejects the **common-mode noise** (mains hum, environmental EMI) that appears equally on both electrode leads, while amplifying the **differential-mode EMG** that appears on opposite leads.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> All formulas below are examinable for KTU 2024. Master these and the linked concepts thoroughly.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SNR}_{dB} &= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{V_{\text{signal,rms}}}{V_{\text{noise,rms}}}\right) \\[6pt]
\text{CMRR}_{dB} &= 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}}\right) \\[6pt]
\text{EMG}_{\text{RMS}} &= \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} \vert \text{EMG}(t) \vert^{2}\,dt} \\[6pt]
\text{EMG}_{\text{ARV}} &= \dfrac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} \vert \text{EMG}(t) \vert\,dt \\[6pt]
V_{n,\text{thermal}} &= \sqrt{4 k_{B} T R \Delta f} \\[6pt]
f_{\text{median}} \;&:\; \int_{0}^{f_{med}} S(f)\,df = \dfrac{1}{2}\int_{0}^{\infty} S(f)\,df \\[6pt]
\text{MD} \;&:\; \text{MU firing rate} \propto \text{Muscle Force}^{0.3\text{–}0.4}
\end{aligned}
$$

### 2.4.1 Compact Formula Table (Exam Cheat Sheet)

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units / Standard Value |
|---|----------|---------|------------------------|
| 1 | SNR | $20 \log_{10}(V_{s}/V_{n})$ | dB; clinical sEMG $\ge 20$ dB |
| 2 | CMRR | $20 \log_{10}(A_{d}/A_{cm})$ | dB; EMG amplifier $\ge 100$ dB |
| 3 | RMS Amplitude | $\sqrt{\tfrac{1}{T}\!\int \!\vert v(t)\vert^{2}dt}$ | mV; surface EMG 0.1–5 mV |
| 4 | ARV | $\tfrac{1}{T}\!\int \!\vert v(t)\vert\,dt$ | mV |
| 5 | Thermal Noise (RMS) | $\sqrt{4 k_{B} T R \Delta f}$ | V; $k_{B}=1.38\times10^{-23}$ J/K |
| 6 | Median Frequency | PSD bisected in power | Hz; 70–130 Hz typical |
| 7 | Half-cell potential (Ag/AgCl) | $E \approx +0.222$ V vs SHE | Volts |
| 8 | Electrode Impedance | $Z_{e} = R_{s} + \tfrac{1}{j\omega C_{d}}$ | $\Omega$; $\mathbf{\le 5\,\text{k}\Omega}$ desirable |
| 9 | Bipolar spacing (sEMG) | $d = 2$–3 cm (SENIAM) | cm |
| 10 | Common-mode voltage | $V_{cm} = (V_{a} + V_{b})/2$ | V |
| 11 | Differential voltage | $V_{d} = V_{a} - V_{b}$ | V (the desired EMG) |
| 12 | Muscle fibre conduction velocity | $V_{cv} = 2$–6 m/s | m/s |

> [!TIP]
> **Real-world Engineering Utility:** The SNR-CMRR-RMS trio above forms the *technical acceptance test* of any commercial EMG machine. Companies like **Natus (Nicolet), Nihon Kohden, Delsys, and Myontec** quote these exact numbers in their datasheets. As a B.Tech engineer, you can read those datasheets and verify whether the device is fit for clinical use.

---

## 2.5 Electrode-Skin Interface (The Critical Interface)

When a surface electrode is placed on the skin, a complex electrical interface forms. It is conventionally modelled as a **Randles equivalent circuit**:

$$
Z_{\text{interface}}(\omega) \;=\; R_{s} \;+\; Z_{\text{W}}(\omega) \;\Vert\; \dfrac{1}{j\omega C_{d}}
$$

Where:
- $R_{s}$ = series resistance of the **electrolyte gel and tissue** (typically $\mathbf{100\,\Omega - 1\,k\Omega}$)
- $C_{d}$ = **double-layer capacitance** at the metal-electrolyte interface (typically $\mathbf{1\,nF - 10\,\mu F}$ per cm²)
- $Z_{W}$ = **Warburg impedance** modelling the diffusion-limited ion transport
- The half-cell potential $E_{hc}$ appears in series with this network

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Viva Favourite:** *"Why Ag/AgCl?"* — Silver-Silver Chloride is the standard biopotential electrode material because (i) it is a **non-polarizable** electrode (charge transfer occurs via a reversible redox reaction $\text{Ag} + \text{Cl}^{-} \leftrightarrow \text{AgCl} + e^{-}$), giving a very stable half-cell potential, (ii) it has **low electrode-skin impedance** (typically $\le 5\,\text{k}\Omega$ at 10 Hz after skin preparation), and (iii) it is **biocompatible** and inexpensive.

---

## 2.6 Why Differential Amplification is Mandatory in EMG

A single-ended recording picks up a huge common-mode interference (50 Hz hum, body-capacitance pickup, etc.). The EMG amplifier therefore uses a **three-amplifier instrumentation topology** (two input buffers + one difference stage) with the following key performance targets:

| Parameter | KTU Minimum Target | Why? |
|-----------|--------------------|------|
| Input Impedance | $\ge \mathbf{10^{9}\,\Omega}$ | To avoid loading the high-impedance source |
| CMRR | $\ge \mathbf{100\,dB}$ | To reject mains hum |
| Gain | $\mathbf{100 - 10{,}000}$ | To bring $\mu$V signals to ADC range |
| Bandwidth | $\mathbf{20\,Hz - 500\,Hz}$ | To include MUAP energy, reject DC drift |
| Noise (RTI) | $\le \mathbf{1\,\mu V_{rms}}$ | To keep SNR high |

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Why RMS is the Best Amplitude Estimator for EMG

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Statement:** Show that the RMS amplitude of an EMG signal over a sliding window of duration $T$ is a consistent estimator of the signal power, and explain why it is preferred over the simple peak-to-peak value for force-correlation studies.

### Step 1 — Start with the instantaneous power delivered to a 1-Ω load

The instantaneous electrical power associated with the EMG signal $v(t)$ across a unit resistance is:

$$
P(t) = \dfrac{v^{2}(t)}{R} \;=\; v^{2}(t) \quad \text{with} \quad R = 1\,\Omega
$$

This step is just the **Joule heating law** $P = V^{2}/R$ applied locally.

### Step 2 — Average the power over a time window of duration $T$

$$
\langle P \rangle = \dfrac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\,dt
$$

This is the **time-average** of the squared signal — a non-negative, monotonically energy-related metric.

### Step 3 — Define RMS

$$
V_{\text{rms}} \;\equiv\; \sqrt{\langle P \rangle} \;=\; \sqrt{\dfrac{1}{T}\int_{0}^{T} v^{2}(t)\,dt}
$$

The square root is taken so that $V_{\text{rms}}$ has the **same physical unit (Volts)** as the original EMG signal.

### Step 4 — Show the link to signal variance (zero-mean EMG)

Because the DC mean of an EMG signal is approximately zero (a property of a stochastic bioelectric source),

$$
V_{\text{rms}}^{2} \;=\; \sigma^{2} + \mu^{2} \;\approx\; \sigma^{2} \quad \text{since} \quad \mu \approx 0
$$

This means $V_{\text{rms}}$ is essentially the **standard deviation** of the EMG signal — a powerful statistical property.

### Step 5 — Why RMS correlates with force

Because each MUAP contributes additively to the squared signal, the total signal power is the **sum of the powers** of the individual MUAPs. As more motor units are recruited, the **signal power increases linearly** with the number of active MUs, and therefore with the force. This linearity is the analytical reason why **$V_{\text{rms}}$ correlates linearly with muscle force** from $\sim 0\%$ to $\sim 80\%$ MVC (Maximum Voluntary Contraction).

### Step 6 — Comparison with Peak-to-Peak Amplitude

The peak-to-peak value $V_{pp}$ depends on the **single largest MUAP** in the window, which is determined by the closest, largest-diameter motor unit. As force increases, $V_{pp}$ quickly **saturates** because the largest MU is already contributing its maximum. Hence $V_{pp}$ is a *non-linear* force estimator — and the analytical reason to reject it in favour of RMS.

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** In your KTU answer, write one line: "RMS is preferred because it represents signal power, which scales linearly with motor-unit recruitment, while peak-to-peak saturates."

---

## 3.2 Derivation: How CMRR Determines Rejection of 50 Hz Mains Hum

### Step 1 — Set up the input signals

Let the two electrode voltages be:

$$
V_{a} = V_{d}/2 + V_{cm} + V_{h}/2 \quad ; \quad V_{b} = -V_{d}/2 + V_{cm} - V_{h}/2
$$

Where $V_{d}$ is the desired differential EMG, $V_{cm}$ is the common-mode voltage, and $V_{h}$ is the 50 Hz hum appearing differentially due to slight impedance imbalance.

### Step 2 — Ideal difference amplifier output

$$
V_{out} = A_{d}\,(V_{a} - V_{b}) = A_{d}\,V_{d} \quad \text{(ideal, infinite CMRR)}
$$

### Step 3 — Real difference amplifier output

A real amplifier has a finite Common-Mode Rejection Ratio. The output becomes:

$$
V_{out} = A_{d}\,V_{d} + A_{cm}\,V_{cm} \quad \text{where} \quad A_{cm} = \dfrac{A_{d}}{\text{CMRR (linear)}}
$$

### Step 4 — Compute the output error due to 50 Hz hum

If $V_{cm} = V_{h,\text{cm}}$ is the amplitude of the common-mode 50 Hz hum, the residual hum at the output is:

$$
V_{\text{hum,out}} = A_{cm}\,V_{h,\text{cm}} = \dfrac{A_{d}\,V_{h,\text{cm}}}{\text{CMRR}}
$$

### Step 5 — Express in decibels

$$
\text{CMRR}_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}}\right)
$$

### Step 6 — Numerical example (KTU board style)

Suppose $A_{d} = 1000$, $V_{d} = 1$ mV, $V_{h,\text{cm}} = 10$ mV (from the power line). With **CMRR = 100 dB**:

$$
\dfrac{A_{d}}{A_{cm}} = 10^{100/20} = 10^{5} = 100{,}000
$$

$$
V_{\text{hum,out}} = \dfrac{1000 \times 10 \text{ mV}}{100{,}000} = 0.1 \text{ mV}
$$

$$
V_{out,\text{signal}} = 1000 \times 1 \text{ mV} = 1000 \text{ mV} = 1 \text{ V}
$$

Hence the **50 Hz hum is reduced to 0.1 mV** while the desired EMG is amplified to 1 V — an effective rejection of 10,000×.

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the most-likely 14-mark Part-B question for KTU 2024 Module-2 EMG. Practise writing it in three crisp paragraphs.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Electrode Selection Trade-Off

| Criterion | Surface (Bipolar) | Concentric Needle | Monopolar Needle | Micro-electrode |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------------|------------------|-----------------|
| Invasiveness | Non-invasive | Invasive (single fibre) | Invasive | Highly invasive |
| Pickup Volume | Large (many MUs) | Small (few MUs) | Small | One fibre |
| Amplitude | 0.1 – 5 mV | 0.5 – 10 mV | 0.5 – 10 mV | 50 – 120 mV |
| Bandwidth | 20 – 500 Hz | 2 Hz – 10 kHz | 2 Hz – 10 kHz | DC – 10 kHz |
| Patient Comfort | High | Low | Low | Very low |
| Clinical Use | Rehabilitation, ergonomics, sports | Neuropathy, myopathy | Routine clinical EMG | Research |
| Cost | Low | Moderate (disposable) | Moderate | High |
| Spatial Resolution | Low | High | High | Very High |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Trick:** "Surface for the **Sport**s field, Needle for the **N**eurology clinic, Micro for the **M**icroscope." The 3-N rule.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Problem (KTU 2024 Style)

> **Problem:** A bipolar surface EMG electrode pair (impedance $Z_{1} = 5\,\text{k}\Omega$ and $Z_{2} = 5.5\,\text{k}\Omega$ at 50 Hz) is connected to an instrumentation amplifier with CMRR = 90 dB. The 50 Hz mains interference induces a common-mode voltage of 8 mV on the body. Compute (a) the differential hum voltage due to impedance mismatch, and (b) the residual hum at the output if the differential gain is 1000.

### Solution

**Part (a):** The impedance mismatch creates a small differential component. Using the voltage-divider model:

$$
V_{d,\text{hum}} \approx V_{cm} \cdot \dfrac{\Delta Z}{Z_{1}+Z_{2}} = 8 \text{ mV} \times \dfrac{0.5\,\text{k}\Omega}{10.5\,\text{k}\Omega}
$$

$$
V_{d,\text{hum}} \approx 0.381 \text{ mV}
$$

**Part (b):** The residual hum has two contributions — the differential hum leaking through the differential path, and the common-mode hum limited by the CMRR.

Differential path hum:
$$
V_{d,\text{out}} = 1000 \times 0.381 \text{ mV} = 381 \text{ mV}
$$

Common-mode hum at output:
$$
\text{CMRR}_{linear} = 10^{90/20} = 31{,}623
$$
$$
V_{cm,\text{out}} = 1000 \times 8 \text{ mV} / 31{,}623 \approx 0.253 \text{ mV}
$$

The differential-path hum (381 mV) dominates by a factor of **$\sim 1500$**. **Conclusion:** For an EMG amplifier, the **electrode impedance match** is just as critical as the CMRR specification. A 5% mismatch defeats a 90 dB CMRR.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete EMG Acquisition Chain

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Brain Cortical Command] --> B[Alpha Motor Neuron in Spinal Cord]
    B --> C[Axon Action Potential Travelling Down Motor Nerve]
    C --> D[Neuromuscular Junction Releases Acetylcholine]
    D --> E[Muscle Fibre Membrane Depolarization]
    E --> F[Motor Unit Action Potential MUAP]
    F --> G[Sum of MUAPs Forms Raw EMG Signal]
    G --> H[Electrode Skin Interface Randles Circuit]
    H --> I[Differential Instrumentation Amplifier]
    I --> J[Bandpass Filter 20 to 500 Hz]
    J --> K[Notch Filter 50 Hz Hum Rejection]
    K --> L[ADC Analog to Digital Converter]
    L --> M[Digital Signal Processing]
    M --> N[Display or Recording]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram — Types of EMG Electrodes

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[EMG Electrodes] --> B[Surface Electrodes]
    A --> C[Needle Electrodes]
    A --> D[Microelectrodes]
    B --> B1[Passive Ag-AgCl Disc]
    B --> B2[Active Dry Electrode]
    B --> B3[Array / High-Density sEMG]
    C --> C1[Concentric Needle]
    C --> C2[Monopolar Needle]
    C --> C3[Single Fibre Needle]
    C --> C4[Hooked Wire]
    D --> D1[Intramuscular Micro-wire]
    D --> D2[Glass Micro-pipette]
```

## 4.3 Functional Architecture — EMG Signal Path with Noise Injection Points

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph SOURCE [PHYSIOLOGICAL SOURCE]
        M1[Active Motor Units] --> SUM1[Sum of MUAPs]
    end
    subgraph PICKUP [PICKUP STAGE]
        SUM1 --> ELEC[Bipolar Electrode Pair]
        HUM1[50 Hz Mains EMI] -.->|inductive coupling| ELEC
        MOTION[Motion Artefact 0.1 to 20 Hz] -.-> ELEC
    end
    subgraph FRONTEND [FRONT END AMPLIFIER]
        ELEC --> IA[Instrumentation Amplifier CMRR 100 dB]
        IA --> BPF[Bandpass Filter 20 to 500 Hz]
        BPF --> NF[Notch Filter 50 Hz]
    end
    subgraph BACKEND [DIGITAL BACK END]
        NF --> ADC[ADC Sampling at 1 to 2 kHz]
        ADC --> DSP[Digital Filtering and Feature Extraction]
        DSP --> OUT[Output: RMS, Median Frequency, Force Estimate]
    end
```

## 4.4 Block Diagram — Surface EMG Electrode-Skin Interface (Randles Model)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MET[Metal Ag-AgCl] -->|half-cell potential E_hc| ELEC
    subgraph ELEC[Electrode-Electrolyte Interface]
        direction LR
        GEL[Electrolyte Gel] --> CD[Double Layer Capacitance C_d]
        CD --> RS[Series Tissue Resistance R_s]
        RS --> W[Warburg Diffusion Impedance Z_w]
    end
    ELEC --> SKIN[Stratum Corneum High Impedance Layer]
    SKIN --> DERM[Dermis Live Tissue Low Impedance]
    DERM --> MUS[Muscle Source the EMG Generator]
```

## 4.5 State Diagram — Motor Unit Recruitment

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> Resting
    Resting --> LightContraction: Small force demand
    LightContraction --> ModerateContraction: Increased descending drive
    ModerateContraction --> StrongContraction: High force demand
    StrongContraction --> Fatigue: Sustained maximal effort
    Fatigue --> Recovery: Rest period
    Recovery --> Resting
    note right of LightContraction: Small MUs recruited first Hennemans Size Principle
    note right of StrongContraction: Large MUs added; rate coding increases
```

## 4.6 Schematic of a 3-Op-Amp Instrumentation Amplifier (Used in EMG Front-End)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    V1[Input V_a via electrode 1] --> A1[Buffer Op-Amp 1 with gain 1 plus G]
    V2[Input V_b via electrode 2] --> A2[Buffer Op-Amp 2 with gain 1 plus G]
    A1 --> R1[Gain-setting Resistor R_G]
    A2 --> R1
    A1 --> A3[Difference Amplifier Stage]
    A2 --> A3
    A3 --> VOUT[Differential Output V_out = G x V_a minus V_b]
    REF[Reference ground electrode] --> A3
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading aid for Mermaid:** Every rectangle is a functional block. Dotted lines represent noise injection points. The arrows show the unidirectional flow of the bioelectric signal from the muscle, through the electrode, the front-end amplifier, the filters, and into the digital back end.
