> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Nanowire-Based Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Nanowire-Based Sensors — Module 4 | OEEBT611

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition
A **Nanowire-Based Sensor (NWS)** is a one-dimensional (1-D) nanoscale transducer, typically with a diameter ranging from **1 nm to 200 nm** and a high aspect ratio (length-to-diameter ratio often exceeding **100:1**), whose electrical, optical, or electromechanical properties undergo a measurable change upon adsorption/binding of target analytes (gas molecules, biomolecules, ions, viruses) on its surface.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *A nanowire sensor is a chemiresistive or field-effect transducer exploiting the extreme surface-to-volume ratio and quantum-confined electron transport of 1-D nanostructures to convert molecular recognition events at the wire surface into quantifiable electrical signals (current, conductance, threshold-voltage shift, or photoluminescence).*

### 1.2 Classification of Nanowires Used in Sensing
| Class | Examples | Typical Transduction |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Semiconducting NW** | Si, Ge, InP, GaN, SiC | Field-Effect Transistor (FET) |
| **Metal-Oxide NW** | ZnO, SnO₂, TiO₂, WO₃, In₂O₃ | Chemiresistive |
| **Metallic NW** | Au, Ag, Pt, Cu, Ni | Plasmonic / Resistive |
| **Conducting Polymer NW** | Polypyrrole, PEDOT:PSS | Chemiresistive / Impedimetric |
| **Hybrid / Core-Shell** | Si/SiO₂, ZnO/PANI, Au/SiO₂ | Multimodal (optical + electrical) |
| **Carbon-Based 1-D** | CNT, Graphene Nanoribbon | FET / Chemiresistive |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Spinning Thread in the Wind"
> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine a thin, taut thread of silk hanging between two pillars. Even the faintest breath of wind striking its surface sets it into motion. The thread is so thin that the surface (which interacts with air) makes up almost all of its mass. Now, replace the silk with a **nanowire** and the wind with **target molecules (analytes)**. The molecules striking or binding to the wire's surface change its **electrical current** as surely as wind moves silk. The thinner the thread, the smaller the puff of air it can sense — exactly why nanowires (diameter ~ tens of nanometres) detect trace concentrations down to the **femtomolar (fM)** level.

### 1.4 Why Nanowires? The Three Engineering Pillars
1. **Extreme Surface-to-Volume Ratio (SVR):** For a cylindrical wire of diameter *d*, SVR = **4/d**. At *d* = 10 nm, SVR ≈ **0.4 nm⁻¹** — a value ~1000× greater than a planar electrode.
2. **Diameter Comparable to the Debye Length (λ_D):** When *d* ≤ 2λ_D, the entire conducting channel is electrostatically gated by surface charges, giving **maximum sensitivity**.
3. **Ballistic / Quasi-Ballistic Electron Transport:** Mean free path of electrons becomes comparable to wire length, yielding sharp, low-noise conductance signatures.

### 1.5 Key Physical Constants & Standard Metrics
- Electron charge: **e = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
- Boltzmann constant: **k_B = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
- Thermal voltage at 300 K: **V_T = k_BT/e ≈ 25.85 mV**
- Debye length in physiological buffer (1× PBS, I ≈ 0.15 M): **λ_D ≈ 0.7 – 0.8 nm**
- Typical NW diameter: **10 – 100 nm**
- Limit of detection (LoD) of state-of-the-art NW biosensors: **fM to aM** range

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principles of Nanowire Sensors

### 2.1.1 Chemiresistive Mechanism (Metal-Oxide & Metallic NWs)
When target gas molecules adsorb on the NW surface, they transfer charge (donate or capture electrons) and/or modulate the depletion region. The change in carrier density *n* translates directly into a change in electrical resistance.

**Step-by-step logic:**
1. NW surface is populated with chemisorbed oxygen ions (O⁻, O²⁻) in air.
2. Reducing gas (e.g., H₂, CO, ethanol) reacts with these ions, releasing trapped electrons **back into the conduction band** → resistance *decreases* for n-type oxides.
3. Oxidising gas (e.g., NO₂, O₃) captures electrons → resistance *increases*.

### 2.1.2 Field-Effect Transistor (FET) Mechanism (Semiconducting NWs)
A back-gate or electrolyte-gate voltage controls carrier density in the NW channel. Surface-bound charged analytes behave as an **additional virtual gate**, shifting the threshold voltage *V_th*:

1. Receptor molecules (antibodies, aptamers, DNA probes) are immobilised on the NW surface.
2. Target analyte binds to the receptor → introduces a sheet of fixed charge *N_s* (C/cm²).
3. The induced surface potential modulates the NW conductance — equivalent to applying a gate bias **ΔV_g,eff = qN_s/C_ox**.

### 2.1.3 Optical Mechanism (Plasmonic / Photoluminescent NWs)
- **Plasmonic shift:** Binding event changes the local refractive index around metallic NW (Au, Ag) → shift in LSPR (Localized Surface Plasmon Resonance) peak wavelength.
- **Photoluminescence quenching:** Quenching of NW band-edge emission by acceptor analytes.

### 2.1.4 Electromechanical (Resonance) Mechanism
Mass loading on a vibrating NW cantilever shifts its **resonant frequency**:

$$f_0 = \frac{\beta_n^2}{4\sqrt{3}} \cdot \frac{1}{L^2}\sqrt{\frac{E}{\rho}}$$

where *E* is Young's modulus, *ρ* density, *L* length, β_n a mode constant.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity / Concept | Governing Equation | Variables & Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **DC Conductance of a cylindrical NW** | $G = \dfrac{\sigma A}{L} = \dfrac{n q \mu \pi d^2}{4L}$ | σ [S/m], A [m²], L [m], d [m], μ [m²/V·s] |
| 2 | **Surface-to-Volume Ratio** | $\text{SVR} = \dfrac{4}{d}$ | d = diameter [m] |
| 3 | **Sensitivity (Chemiresistive)** | $S = \dfrac{\vert \Delta R \vert}{R_0} = \dfrac{\vert R_{\text{gas}} - R_{\text{air}} \vert}{R_{\text{air}}}$ | Dimensionless (often in %) |
| 4 | **Debye Length (electrolyte)** | $\lambda_D = \sqrt{\dfrac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 k_B T}{2 N_A e^2 I}}$ | I = ionic strength [M], ε_r relative permittivity |
| 5 | **Debye Length (1× PBS, 25 °C, water)** | $\lambda_D \approx \dfrac{0.304}{\sqrt{I}}\ \text{nm}$ | I in mol/L |
| 6 | **Threshold-Voltage Shift in NW-FET** | $\Delta V_{\text{th}} = -\dfrac{q N_s}{C_{\text{ox}}}$ | N_s [C/m²], C_ox [F/m²] |
| 7 | **Gate-Oxide Capacitance (per unit area)** | $C_{\text{ox}} = \dfrac{\varepsilon_{\text{ox}}}{d_{\text{ox}}}$ | ε_ox permittivity, d_ox oxide thickness |
| 8 | **Drain Current (linear regime)** | $I_D = \mu_n C_{\text{ox}} \dfrac{W}{L}\left[(V_{GS}-V_{\text{th}})V_{DS} - \dfrac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]$ | Standard MOSFET form |
| 9 | **Relative Current Response (NW-FET)** | $\dfrac{\Delta I_D}{I_{D0}} = \dfrac{\Delta V_{\text{th}}}{V_{GS}-V_{\text{th},0}}$ | — |
| 10 | **Grain/Surface Site Coverage** | $\theta = \dfrac{K \cdot C}{1 + K \cdot C}$ | Langmuir isotherm, K = adsorption const., C = concentration |
| 11 | **Mass-Loading Frequency Shift** | $\dfrac{\Delta f}{f_0} = -\dfrac{\Delta m}{2 m_{\text{eff}}}$ | Sauerbrey-like relation |
| 12 | **LSPR Peak Shift (plasmonic NW)** | $\Delta \lambda_{\max} = S \cdot \Delta n$ | S = sensitivity [nm/RIU], n = refractive index |

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Debye Length:** "**0.3 over root-I**" — the formula $\lambda_D = 0.304/\sqrt{I}$ nm is a high-yield line in KTU derivations.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Healthcare:** Ultra-sensitive label-free detection of cancer biomarkers (PSA, CEA), viruses (H1N1, SARS-CoV-2 spike protein), glucose.
- **Environmental Monitoring:** Trace heavy metals (Hg²⁺, Pb²⁺), NO₂/SO₂/H₂S in air quality networks.
- **Defence & Security:** Explosive vapour detection (TNT, RDX) via functionalised Si-NW arrays.
- **Food Safety:** Detection of *Salmonella*, *E. coli*, pesticides.
- **Process Control:** Real-time chemical reactor monitoring via SnO₂ NW sensor arrays (electronic nose).
- **Wearable Electronics:** ZnO NW piezoelectric nanogenerators coupled with chemiresistors for self-powered sweat sensors.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Conductance of a Cylindrical Nanowire and Its Surface-Charge Sensitivity

### Statement
Derive the expression for the conductance of a cylindrical semiconductor nanowire of diameter *d* and length *L*, and show how surface-adsorbed charge density *N_s* (C/m²) modifies the conductance.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Geometric Cross-Section**

For a cylindrical wire of diameter *d*, the conducting cross-sectional area is:

$$
A = \pi \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2} = \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4}
$$

**Step 2 — Bulk Drude Conductivity**

For a semiconductor with free-electron density *n* (m⁻³), the bulk conductivity is:

$$
\sigma = n \, q \, \mu_{n}
$$

where *q* is the elementary charge and μ_n the electron mobility.

**Step 3 — DC Conductance (Bulk)**

Using *G = σA/L*:

$$
G_{\text{bulk}} = \frac{\sigma A}{L} = \frac{n q \mu_{n} \pi d^{2}}{4 L}
$$

**Step 4 — Surface Depletion Width**

A layer of surface charge of density *N_s* (C/m²) creates a cylindrical depletion region of width *w* extending inward from the wire surface. Charge neutrality requires the depletion charge to balance the surface charge:

$$
q n \cdot \pi \left[ \left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2} - \left(\frac{d}{2} - w\right)^{2} \right] = q N_s \pi d
$$

**Step 5 — Simplify the Geometric Factor**

Expanding the bracket:

$$
\left(\frac{d}{2}\right)^{2} - \left(\frac{d}{2} - w\right)^{2} = d\,w - w^{2}
$$

For thin depletion widths (w ≪ d), the *w²* term is negligible, giving:

$$
d\, w \approx d \quad \Rightarrow \quad w \approx \frac{N_s}{n}
$$

But more rigorously, retaining the full quadratic:

$$
\pi (d w - w^{2}) = \pi d \quad \Rightarrow \quad w^{2} - d w + \frac{N_s}{n} d = 0
$$

Solving the quadratic in *w*:

$$
w = \frac{d}{2}\left[1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{4 N_s}{n d^{2}}}\right]
$$

**Step 6 — Effective Conducting Diameter**

The effective conducting diameter is reduced to:

$$
d_{\text{eff}} = d - 2w = d\left[\sqrt{1 - \frac{4 N_s}{n d^{2}}}\right]
$$

**Step 7 — Effective Conductance with Surface Charge**

Substituting *d* → *d_eff* in the conductance formula:

$$
G_{\text{eff}} = \frac{n q \mu_{n} \pi}{4 L}\left[d\sqrt{1 - \frac{4 N_s}{n d^{2}}}\right]^{2}
$$

$$
G_{\text{eff}} = G_{\text{bulk}}\left[1 - \frac{4 N_s}{n d^{2}}\right]
$$

**Step 8 — Sensitivity Expression**

The relative change in conductance (a direct measure of sensor response) is:

$$
\frac{\Delta G}{G_{0}} = \frac{G_{\text{eff}} - G_{\text{bulk}}}{G_{\text{bulk}}} = -\frac{4 N_s}{n d^{2}}
$$

**Engineering Insight:**
The absolute sensitivity $\vert \Delta G/G_0 \vert$ scales as **1/d²**. Decreasing the wire diameter from 100 nm to 10 nm yields a **100× enhancement in sensitivity** — the central reason semiconductor-NW FETs can detect sub-femtomolar analyte concentrations.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Threshold-Voltage Shift in a Nanowire FET Biosensor

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Surface-Bound Charge from Biomolecules**

Consider DNA probes immobilised on the NW surface, each 20-base-long, with surface density N_m (molecules/m²). Each hybridised target contributes approximately *Z* elementary charges. The equivalent sheet-charge density is:

$$
N_s = Z \, e \, N_m
$$

**Step 2 — Electrostatic Coupling**

This sheet charge sits atop a gate oxide of thickness *d_ox* and permittivity ε_ox. It induces a surface potential equivalent to a gate bias:

$$
\Delta V_{\text{th}} = -\frac{q N_s}{C_{\text{ox}}} = -\frac{Z \, e^{2} N_m \, d_{\text{ox}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{ox}}}
$$

The **negative sign** reflects that *for a p-type NW*, negative surface charge (anionic DNA) depletes holes, requiring a more negative gate to re-establish the channel.

**Step 3 — Translate to Drain-Current Modulation**

In the linear regime of the NW-FET, with applied *V_GS* and *V_DS*:

$$
\frac{\Delta I_D}{I_{D0}} = \frac{\Delta V_{\text{th}}}{V_{GS} - V_{\text{th},0}}
$$

**Step 4 — Numerical Illustration**

Take *d_ox* = 10 nm, ε_ox = 3.9 × ε_0 (SiO₂), N_m = 10¹² m⁻², Z = 100 e per DNA duplex.

$$
\Delta V_{\text{th}} = \frac{100 \times 1.6\times10^{-19} \times 10^{12} \times 10\times10^{-9}}{3.9 \times 8.85\times10^{-12}}
$$

$$
\Delta V_{\text{th}} \approx \frac{1.6\times10^{-13}}{3.45\times10^{-11}} \approx 4.6 \text{ mV}
$$

A **4.6 mV threshold shift** is easily resolved with a standard semiconductor parameter analyser — confirming the feasibility of label-free DNA detection at picomolar concentrations.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Debye Screening Constraint on Biosensor Sensitivity

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — The Screening Problem**

When a charged biomolecule binds to a NW surface immersed in an electrolyte, the induced potential is screened by dissolved ions over a characteristic length *λ_D*. Only the **portion of the bound charge lying within λ_D of the surface** is "visible" to the NW channel.

**Step 2 — Debye Length Expression**

The Debye length in a monovalent electrolyte is:

$$
\lambda_D = \sqrt{\frac{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 k_B T}{2 N_A e^{2} I}}
$$

For water at 25 °C (ε_r = 78.5), this simplifies to:

$$
\lambda_D = \frac{0.304}{\sqrt{I}}\ \text{nm},\quad I \text{ in mol/L}
$$

**Step 3 — Numerical Estimates for Common Buffers**

| Buffer | Ionic Strength *I* (M) | Debye Length λ_D (nm) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| Deionised water | 10⁻⁷ | **304** |
| 0.01× PBS | 0.0015 | **7.8** |
| 0.1× PBS | 0.015 | **2.5** |
| 1× PBS (physiological) | 0.15 | **0.78** |
| 10× PBS | 1.5 | **0.25** |

**Step 4 — Design Rule**

For effective electrostatic gating, the NW diameter must satisfy:

$$
d \lesssim 2 \lambda_D
$$

In 1× PBS, this means **d ≤ 1.5 nm** — practically unattainable for stable Si NWs. This explains why low-ionic-strength buffers (10–100 mM) are essential in NW-FET biosensing experiments. A pragmatic workaround is to use **short receptor probes (aptamers ~ 1 nm, ssDNA < 6 nm)** so the binding event occurs within the Debye length.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:** "**The Debye length is the biological gatekeeper of NW-FET sensitivity.**" A thicker double layer *hides* the bound analyte from the field-effect transducer, collapsing ΔV_th toward zero.

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — Sensor Modalities

| Property | Chemiresistive NW | NW-FET Biosensor | Plasmonic NW | Resonant NW |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Analyte type** | Gases, VOCs | Biomolecules (DNA, proteins) | Refractive-index changes, biomolecules | Mass loading |
| **Signal** | ΔR / ΔG | ΔI_D, ΔV_th | Δλ_LSPR | Δf |
| **Typical LoD** | ppb – ppm | fM – pM | nM – pM | fg |
| **Selectivity** | Functionalisation / doping | Bioreceptor (Ab, aptamer) | Surface chemistry | Receptor + frequency tracking |
| **Reusability** | High (thermal reset) | Limited (probe regeneration) | Moderate | High (Q-cleaning) |
| **Power needs** | Low (μW) | Low (μW) | Optical source required | Piezo drive required |
| **KTU weightage** | ★★★★★ | ★★★★★ | ★★★ | ★★ |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Generic Nanowire Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Analyte Influx] --> B[Receptor Functionalisation Layer]
    B --> C[Nanowire Channel]
    C --> D[Transduction Mechanism]
    D --> E[Signal Amplifier]
    E --> F[ADC and Processor]
    F --> G[Display / Wireless Tx]
    C --> H[Source and Drain Electrodes]
    H --> I[Bias Voltage Source]
    D --> J[Reference Baseline Library]
    J --> F
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Layer **A→B** captures the analyte and presents it to the molecular recognition layer.
- Layer **B→C** is the heart of the sensor: bioreceptor-analyte binding perturbs the NW's electronic structure.
- Layer **C→D** converts this perturbation into an electrical / optical / mechanical signal.
- Layers **E, F, G** constitute the read-out electronics chain.
- Block **J** stores a baseline response to enable differential (Δ) measurements — crucial for drift compensation.

## 4.2 Functional Signal-Transduction Flow in a NW-FET Biosensor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[Step 1: Receptor Immobilisation] --> S2[Step 2: Buffer Equilibration and Baseline I_D0]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Analyte Injection]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Binding Event on NW Surface]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Induced Surface Charge N_s]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Threshold Voltage Shift Delta V_th]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Drain Current Modulation Delta I_D]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Real Time Output Concentration]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Calibration Curve and LoD Extraction]
```

## 4.3 Cross-Sectional View of a Nanowire Sensor — Layer Stack (Mermaid Block View)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    L1[Topmost Layer: Target Analyte in Solution] --> L2[Receptor Probe Layer: Antibodies Aptamers DNA]
    L2 --> L3[Functional Surface: silane or thiol SAM]
    L3 --> L4[Native or Native Oxide Shell]
    L4 --> L5[Semiconducting or Metallic Nanowire Core]
    L5 --> L6[Back Gate Oxide Layer]
    L6 --> L7[Silicon or ITO Substrate and Gate]
    L8[Source Contact Left] --- L5
    L9[Drain Contact Right] --- L5
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Reading Tip:** In a board exam, drawing the **layer stack** (5–6 labelled horizontal bands) and the **source–drain–gate triangle** fetches full marks, even if the binding chemistry is described only qualitatively.

## 4.4 Decision Flowchart: Selecting a Nanowire Sensor Modality

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Q1{Analyte phase} -->|Gas or VOC| Q2{Operating temperature}
    Q1 -->|Biomolecule in liquid| Q3{Charge based or Mass based}
    Q2 -->|Room temp| R1[Use Conducting Polymer or Metal NW]
    Q2 -->|100 to 400 degree C| R2[Use Metal Oxide NW ZnO SnO2]
    Q3 -->|Charge based| R3[Use Semiconducting NW FET]
    Q3 -->|Mass based| R4[Use Resonant NW Cantilever]
    Q3 -->|Refractive index change| R5[Use Plasmonic Au or Ag NW]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Define a nanowire sensor. List any two key advantages of using nanowires over planar thin-film sensors."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1.5 marks):** A nanowire sensor is a one-dimensional nanoscale transducer (diameter 1–200 nm, length > 1 µm) whose electrical/optical properties change measurably upon binding or adsorption of target analytes on its surface.
- **Advantage 1 (0.75 mark):** Extremely high surface-to-volume ratio (SVR = 4/d), making the response to surface binding events far larger than in planar films.
- **Advantage 2 (0.75 mark):** When the wire diameter approaches twice the Debye length, the entire conducting channel is electrostatically gated by the surface charge, yielding ultra-high sensitivity (fM–aM LoD).

---

### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"Explain the role of Debye length in nanowire FET-based biosensors. Why are low-ionic-strength buffers preferred in such measurements?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Role of Debye length (1.5 marks):** The Debye length *λ_D* is the distance over which surface charge is screened by electrolyte ions. Only charges within *λ_D* of the NW surface can effectively gate the channel and produce a measurable ΔV_th. The governing expression is $\lambda_D = \sqrt{\varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0 k_B T / (2N_A e^2 I)}$.
- **Low-ionic-strength preference (1.5 marks):** In 1× PBS, *λ_D* ≈ 0.78 nm, much smaller than typical receptor biomolecules (5–10 nm). Lowering *I* to 10 mM increases *λ_D* to ~3 nm, allowing the bound analyte's field to penetrate to the NW and produce a useful signal. Hence dilute buffers (10–100 mM) are preferred for NW-FET biosensing.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (Module Internal Choice, 14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Forgetting to state units** of sensitivity, λ_D, ΔV_th — costs 0.5–1 mark per occurrence.
> 2. **Sign errors** in ΔV_th = −qN_s/C_ox — read the carrier type carefully (electron vs. hole).
> 3. **Skipping the derivation steps** in Part B derivations — KTU examiners allocate 1 mark per correct logical step. Always show the algebraic transition, even if "obvious".
> 4. **Confusing chemiresistive and FET mechanisms** — they share the word "nanowire" but answer scripts must keep them physically separate.
> 5. **Drawing the layer stack but forgetting the source/drain contacts** — full credit requires a complete cross-section.

### ✦ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *Chemiresistive & FET Pathways*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat cross-sectional diagram, describe the **layer-stack architecture** of a silicon nanowire FET biosensor for DNA detection. Explain each layer's function. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **threshold-voltage shift** in a NW-FET biosensor when negatively charged DNA molecules hybridise on the gate oxide. Show the **drain-current modulation** in the linear regime and comment on the role of **Debye length** in limiting sensitivity. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✦ Model Solution for Question A

**(a) Layer-Stack Architecture (7 marks)**

| Layer | Material / Thickness | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **(i) Source / Drain contacts** | Ti/Au or Al, ~100 nm | Inject/extract carriers; define channel length L |
| **(ii) Nanowire channel** | p- or n-Si, d = 20–50 nm, L = 1–5 µm | Active transduction element |
| **(iii) Gate dielectric** | SiO₂ (d_ox ≈ 10 nm) or HfO₂ | Passivates surface, supports biofunctionalisation |
| **(iv) Surface functionalisation** | APTES silane / thiol SAM | Covalent anchoring of receptors |
| **(v) Bioreceptor layer** | ss-DNA probe (~6 nm long) | Selective molecular recognition |
| **(vi) Target analyte (in buffer)** | Complementary ss-DNA | Hybridises, deposits negative charge |
| **(vii) Back gate / Si substrate** | Heavily doped Si, Al back contact | Sets baseline V_GS |

A clean, labelled 6-layer horizontal cross-section with the source on the left, drain on the right, and the bioreceptor-analyte hybrid drawn on top is mandatory. **Marks: 1 (diagram) + 6 × 0.5 (layer function descriptions) = 4 marks; remaining 3 marks for explanatory clarity and the use of proper terminology.** *(Awarded as: 1 cross-section diagram = 2 marks; 5 layer descriptions = 5 marks; total 7.)*

**(b) Derivation of Threshold-Voltage Shift (7 marks)**

Starting from charge neutrality at the gate oxide, the surface-bound charge sheet of density *N_s* (C/m²) induces a threshold shift:

$$
\Delta V_{\text{th}} = -\frac{q N_s}{C_{\text{ox}}} = -\frac{q N_s \, d_{\text{ox}}}{\varepsilon_{\text{ox}}}
$$

For a typical DNA hybrid carrying Z ≈ 100 negative charges per duplex at surface density N_m = 10¹² m⁻², with d_ox = 10 nm and ε_ox = 3.9 ε_0:

$$
N_s = Z e N_m = 100 \times 1.6\times10^{-19} \times 10^{12} = 1.6 \times 10^{-5}\ \text{C/m}^{2}
$$

$$
C_{\text{ox}} = \frac{\varepsilon_{\text{ox}}}{d_{\text{ox}}} = \frac{3.9 \times 8.85\times10^{-12}}{10\times10^{-9}} = 3.45\times10^{-3}\ \text{F/m}^{2}
$$

$$
\Delta V_{\text{th}} = -\frac{1.6\times10^{-5}}{3.45\times10^{-3}} \approx -4.6\ \text{mV}
$$

In the linear regime, drain current:

$$
I_D = \mu_n C_{\text{ox}} \frac{W}{L}\left[(V_{GS} - V_{\text{th}}) V_{DS} - \frac{V_{DS}^2}{2}\right]
$$

Differentiating with respect to *V_th* and normalising:

$$
\frac{\Delta I_D}{I_{D0}} = \frac{\Delta V_{\text{th}}}{V_{GS} - V_{\text{th},0}}
$$

For *V_GS* − *V_th,0* = 0.5 V, the relative current change is:

$$
\frac{\Delta I_D}{I_{D0}} = \frac{4.6\times10^{-3}}{0.5} \approx 0.92\%
$$

A ~1 % current shift is comfortably resolvable with a bench-top source-measure unit. The **Debye length** limits sensitivity by screening the negative charge of hybridised DNA over *λ_D* ≈ 0.78 nm in 1× PBS, reducing the effective *N_s* seen by the channel. Lowering ionic strength or shortening receptor length restores sensitivity — the canonical design rule: **d_NW ≲ 2λ_D and probe length ≤ λ_D**.

**Mark allocation:** 2 (ΔV_th derivation) + 2 (numerical evaluation) + 2 (drain current expression + ratio) + 1 (Debye discussion).

---

### ✦ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice (Material-Focus)*

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, 2024 Scheme, Module 4]**

**(a)** With the help of a labelled **block diagram**, describe the construction and working of a **metal-oxide nanowire (e.g., ZnO) chemiresistive gas sensor**. Explain the receptor-function, surface reaction, and signal-transduction stages. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ZnO nanowire of diameter **d = 50 nm** and length **L = 5 µm** is exposed to NO₂ gas. The electron density in the wire is **n = 10¹⁸ cm⁻³** and mobility **µ = 50 cm²/V·s**. Determine (i) the bulk conductance, (ii) the percentage change in conductance if the surface adsorbs a charge density **N_s = 5 × 10⁻⁶ C/m²** of acceptor-type species. Take q = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C. **(7 marks)**

---

#### ✦ Model Solution for Question B

**(a) Block Diagram & Working (7 marks)**

A **ZnO NW chemiresistive gas sensor** consists of:

1. **Substrate & heater** (alumina, Pt heater on back) — maintains operating temperature 200–400 °C.
2. **Interdigitated electrodes (IDE)** — Au or Pt, define the source and drain.
3. **ZnO nanowire bridging the IDE gap** — grown by VLS, hydrothermal, or electrospinning routes.
4. **Surface chemisorbed oxygen layer** (O⁻, O²⁻) — captures conduction electrons, creating a depletion shell.

**Working principle:**
- In air: oxygen adsorbs on ZnO surface → forms O⁻ + e⁻ from the conduction band → depletion width increases → resistance is high.
- On exposure to NO₂ (oxidising gas): NO₂(g) + e⁻ → NO₂⁻(ads) — additional electrons withdrawn → depletion widens further → resistance rises sharply (n-type ZnO).
- On exposure to a reducing gas (H₂, CO, ethanol): surface oxygen ions react, releasing trapped electrons back → depletion narrows → resistance falls.

The response is quantified as:

$$
S = \frac{\vert R_{\text{gas}} - R_{\text{air}} \vert}{R_{\text{air}}} \times 100\%
$$

The block diagram must show: **Gas inlet → NW surface → resistance-change transducer → amplifier → display**, with operating-temperature control loop. (Award 2 marks for diagram, 5 marks for explaining the four working stages.)

**(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks)**

**Given data:**

- d = 50 nm = 50 × 10⁻⁹ m → A = πd²/4 = π(50 × 10⁻⁹)²/4 = 1.963 × 10⁻¹⁵ m²
- L = 5 µm = 5 × 10⁻⁶ m
- n = 10¹⁸ cm⁻³ = 10²⁴ m⁻³
- µ = 50 cm²/V·s = 50 × 10⁻⁴ m²/V·s = 5 × 10⁻³ m²/V·s
- q = 1.6 × 10⁻¹⁹ C
- N_s = 5 × 10⁻⁶ C/m²

**(i) Bulk Conductance (3 marks):**

$$
\sigma = n q \mu = 10^{24} \times 1.6\times10^{-19} \times 5\times10^{-3}
$$

$$
\sigma = 0.8\ \text{S/m}
$$

$$
G_{\text{bulk}} = \frac{\sigma A}{L} = \frac{0.8 \times 1.963\times10^{-15}}{5\times10^{-6}}
$$

$$
G_{\text{bulk}} = 3.14 \times 10^{-10}\ \text{S} = 0.314\ \text{nS}
$$

**(ii) Percentage Change in Conductance (4 marks):**

Using the expression derived in Section 3.1:

$$
\frac{\Delta G}{G_{0}} = -\frac{4 N_s}{n d^{2}} = -\frac{4 \times 5\times10^{-6}}{10^{24} \times (50\times10^{-9})^{2}}
$$

$$
\frac{\Delta G}{G_{0}} = -\frac{2\times10^{-5}}{10^{24} \times 2.5\times10^{-15}} = -\frac{2\times10^{-5}}{2.5\times10^{9}} = -8 \times 10^{-15}
$$

This is a vanishingly small fractional change. **Why?** For a metallic-like ZnO (high n = 10¹⁸ cm⁻³), the *intrinsic* carrier density is so large that the adsorbed surface charge cannot appreciably deplete the wire. **Engineering implication:** The sensor must be operated in a regime where *n* is small (intrinsic or low-doped ZnO) or the diameter must be reduced (< 20 nm) to amplify ΔG/G. This is the classic *diametre-scaling* advantage of nanowires.

For a hypothetical 10-nm-diameter NW with the same n, the fractional change becomes:

$$
\frac{\Delta G}{G_{0}}\bigg\rvert_{d=10\,\text{nm}} = -\frac{4 \times 5\times10^{-6}}{10^{24} \times (10\times10^{-9})^{2}} = -2 \times 10^{-13}
$$

Still small in absolute terms, but a **25× improvement** over the 50-nm wire (1/d² scaling). The clean room route (VLS-grown, 10–20 nm Si or Ge NW) is therefore standard in commercial NW-FET biosensor chips.

**Mark allocation:** 3 (part i) + 3 (part ii derivation) + 1 (numerical value + engineering comment).

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module-4 Rapid-Revision Bullet Box — Nanowire-Based Sensors**

- **Definition:** 1-D nanostructures (d = 1–200 nm, aspect ratio > 100:1) that transduce surface adsorption into electrical/optical/mechanical signals.
- **Three pillars of NW sensing:** (1) Extreme SVR = 4/d, (2) Diameter comparable to 2λ_D, (3) Quasi-ballistic transport → low noise.
- **Transduction modes:** Chemiresistive (ΔR), FET (ΔV_th / ΔI_D), Plasmonic (Δλ_LSPR), Mass-loading (Δf_resonant).
- **Conductance formula (cylindrical):** $G = nq\mu\pi d^2 / (4L)$; **relative change:** $\Delta G/G_0 = -4N_s/(n d^2)$.
- **Threshold-voltage shift:** $\Delta V_{\text{th}} = -qN_s/C_{\text{ox}} = -qN_s d_{\text{ox}}/\varepsilon_{\text{ox}}$.
- **Debye length (water, 25 °C):** $\lambda_D = 0.304/\sqrt{I}$ nm. Design rule: $d \lesssim 2\lambda_D$ and probe length $\le \lambda_D$.
- **Types of NWs:** Si, Ge (FET); ZnO, SnO₂, TiO₂, WO₃ (chemiresistive); Au, Ag (plasmonic); Polypyrrole, PEDOT:PSS (polymer).
- **Functionalisation chemistries:** APTES/glutaraldehyde (Si), thiol SAM (Au), EDC-NHS (carboxyl–amine coupling for proteins).
- **LoD benchmarks:** Chemiresistive → ppb; NW-FET biosensor → fM–aM; Plasmonic → nM; Resonant → fg.
- **Two figures every KTU answer must contain:** (1) NW sensor cross-section / block diagram; (2) Calibration curve (concentration on log scale vs. response).
- **Always state:** *operating temperature for metal-oxide sensors (200–400 °C)*, *ionic strength of buffer for biosensors (< 100 mM preferred)*, *units of sensitivity (%, ppm, nm/RIU, Hz/ng)*.
- **Common pitfalls to avoid in the exam:**
  1. Mixing up ΔV_th sign for n-type vs. p-type channels.
  2. Forgetting to convert cm⁻³ → m⁻³ in numerical problems.
  3. Stating "Debye length" without the formula.
  4. Drawing a NW without source/drain contacts.
  5. Claiming the LoD is "very low" — always quote a number (e.g., 10 fM).

> **End of Module 4 Notes — Nanowire-Based Sensors** ✦

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT611 - BIOSENSORS & TRANSDUCERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Quantum Dots-Based Sensors (Basics of Sensing Only). Application of Nanomaterial Based Biosensors: Metal Nanoparticle-Based Biosensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Quantum Dots (QDs) – Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Quantum Dots (QDs)** are zero-dimensional (0-D) semiconductor nanocrystals, typically with physical dimensions in the range of **2 nm to 10 nm** (comparable to or smaller than the exciton Bohr radius of the bulk material), in which charge carriers (electrons and holes) are spatially confined in all three spatial dimensions. This confinement gives rise to **quantum size effects**, producing discrete, size-tunable electronic and optical properties.

Common QD materials include **CdSe**, **CdTe**, **ZnS**, **PbS**, **InP**, and graphene QDs.

**Key Optical Properties of QDs**
- **Size-tunable photoluminescence (PL)** emission: smaller dots emit blue; larger dots emit red.
- **Broad absorption spectrum** combined with a **narrow, symmetric emission** peak.
- **High quantum yield (QY)** and **photostability** versus organic fluorophores.
- **Large molar extinction coefficients** (~10⁵–10⁶ M⁻¹cm⁻¹).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Musical Instrument" View

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: QDs as Tiny Drums of Light**
> Imagine a tightly stretched drum. The smaller the drum, the higher the pitch it produces. Similarly, a smaller quantum dot confines the electron "wave" to a tinier space, raising the energy of emitted photons — so the dot glows **blue**. A larger dot gives the electron more room to "vibrate", producing lower-energy, **red** light. The bandgap is therefore **tunable purely by particle size**, which is the cornerstone of QD-based sensing.

### 1.3 Metal Nanoparticle-Based Biosensors – Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Metal Nanoparticle (MNP)-Based Biosensors** are optical/electrochemical biosensors that exploit the unique localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) properties of noble metal nanoparticles — primarily **Gold (AuNPs)** and **Silver (AgNPs)** — for the sensitive detection of biomolecular analytes such as DNA, proteins, small molecules, and viruses.

The sensing event is typically transduced as a **visible color change** (colorimetric) or a shift in the **plasmon absorption peak**.

### 1.4 Conceptual Analogy – The "Tunable Antenna" View

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy: AuNPs as Sub-Microscopic Antennas**
> Think of free electrons on a gold nanoparticle as a sea of oscillating charges. When light of just the right frequency strikes, the electron sea resonates collectively — much like a tuned radio antenna picking up a station. This collective oscillation is the **surface plasmon**. If biomolecules bind to the nanoparticle surface, the antenna's "tuning" changes, and the colour of the solution shifts from wine-red to purple (for AuNPs). This visible color shift is the **sensing signal**.

### 1.5 Why Nanomaterials? The "Surface-to-Volume" Revolution

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** When particle size shrinks from bulk to nano, two effects dominate:
> 1. **Surface Area Explosion:** A 10 nm sphere has ~10⁶× the surface-to-volume ratio of a 1 mm sphere — vastly more binding sites for bioreceptors.
> 2. **Quantum/Plasmonic Emergence:** New physical phenomena (quantum confinement, LSPR) appear that are absent in bulk.
> 
> **Physical constants of importance:**
> - **Boltzmann constant (k_B) = 1.38 × 10⁻²³ J/K**
> - **Reduced Planck's constant (ℏ) = 1.054 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s**
> - **Electron charge (e) = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ C**
> - **Free-electron mass (m₀) = 9.11 × 10⁻³¹ kg**

## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Quantum Confinement in Quantum Dots

When the particle radius **r** is comparable to or smaller than the **exciton Bohr radius (a_B)**, the electron and hole wavefunctions are squeezed into a box-like potential. The continuous energy bands of bulk semiconductors split into **discrete molecular-like energy levels**.

**Three Regimes of Confinement:**

| Regime | Condition | Behavior |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Weak Confinement | r > 2 a_B | Bulk-like behavior with minor perturbation |
| Strong Confinement | r < 2 a_B (typical QDs) | Quantized energy levels, size-tunable bandgap |
| Very Strong Confinement | r << a_B | Molecular-like discrete states |

### 2.2 The Brus Equation – Size-Dependent Bandgap

The effective bandgap of a nanocrystal is the bulk bandgap plus a quantum confinement correction minus a Coulomb term:

$$E_{gap}(r) = E_{gap}^{bulk} + \frac{h^2}{8 r^2}\left(\frac{1}{m_e^*} + \frac{1}{m_h^*}\right) - \frac{1.786 e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r r}$$

Where:
- $E_{gap}^{bulk}$ = bulk semiconductor bandgap (eV)
- $h$ = Planck's constant (6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s)
- $r$ = nanocrystal radius (m)
- $m_e^*, m_h^*$ = effective masses of electron and hole (kg)
- $\varepsilon_0$ = vacuum permittivity (8.854 × 10⁻¹² F/m)
- $\varepsilon_r$ = relative permittivity of the semiconductor
- $e$ = electron charge

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Interpretation:** The $1/r^2$ term dominates for small r, so as the dot shrinks, the bandgap **widens**, shifting emission toward **higher energy (blue)**. This is the cornerstone of QD optical tuning.

### 2.3 Particle-in-a-Sphere Confinement Energy

For a single charge carrier in a spherical potential well of radius r, the lowest confinement energy is:

$$E_n = \frac{h^2 n^2}{8 m^* r^2}, \quad n = 1, 2, 3, \ldots$$

The first-excited state energy is **4× the ground state**, giving the ladder of discrete levels.

### 2.4 Localized Surface Plasmon Resonance (LSPR) in MNPs

In a metal nanoparticle smaller than the wavelength of light, the conduction electrons are driven collectively by the incident electric field. The resonance condition is met when the real part of the metal's dielectric function satisfies:

$$\varepsilon_1(\omega) = -2 \varepsilon_m$$

Where $\varepsilon_m$ is the dielectric constant of the surrounding medium and $\varepsilon_1(\omega)$ is the real part of the metal's complex dielectric function $\varepsilon(\omega) = \varepsilon_1(\omega) + i\varepsilon_2(\omega)$.

### 2.5 Mie Theory – Extinction Cross-Section (Quasi-Static Limit)

For a small spherical nanoparticle (r << λ), the extinction cross-section simplifies to:

$$\sigma_{ext} = \frac{24 \pi^2 r^3 \varepsilon_m^{3/2}}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{\varepsilon_2(\omega)}{\left[\varepsilon_1(\omega) + 2\varepsilon_m\right]^2 + \varepsilon_2(\omega)^2}$$

The resonance peak occurs at the **minimum of the denominator**, i.e., when $\varepsilon_1(\omega) \approx -2\varepsilon_m$.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Typical Unit |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | $E_{gap}(r) = E_{gap}^{bulk} + \dfrac{h^2}{8 r^2}\left(\dfrac{1}{m_e^*} + \dfrac{1}{m_h^*}\right) - \dfrac{1.786 e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r r}$ | Size-dependent bandgap (Brus eq.) | eV |
| 2 | $E_n = \dfrac{h^2 n^2}{8 m^* r^2}$ | Confinement energy, particle-in-sphere | J |
| 3 | $a_B = \dfrac{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r \hbar^2}{m^* e^2}$ | Exciton Bohr radius | m |
| 4 | $\varepsilon_1(\omega) = -2 \varepsilon_m$ | LSPR resonance condition (Mie, quasi-static) | dimensionless |
| 5 | $\sigma_{ext} = \dfrac{24 \pi^2 r^3 \varepsilon_m^{3/2}}{\lambda} \cdot \dfrac{\varepsilon_2}{\left[\varepsilon_1 + 2\varepsilon_m\right]^2 + \varepsilon_2^2}$ | Extinction cross-section of MNP | m² |
| 6 | $\lambda_{max} = \lambda_0 + m \cdot \Delta n$ | Refractive-index sensitivity of LSPR peak | nm |
| 7 | $\Delta\lambda_{LSPR} = S \cdot \Delta n$ | Spectral shift vs. refractive index change | nm/RIU |
| 8 | $E = \dfrac{1240}{\lambda (nm)}$ eV | Photon energy from wavelength | eV |

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Medical diagnostics:** QD-labelled lateral flow assays (e.g., COVID-19 antigen tests).
- **DNA detection:** AuNP-based colorimetric biosensors exploiting salt-induced aggregation.
- **Live-cell imaging:** QDs as fluorescent tags with **narrow emission** for multiplexing.
- **Environmental monitoring:** Heavy-metal (Hg²⁺, Pb²⁺) detection via QD fluorescence quenching.
- **Point-of-care devices:** Both QD- and AuNP-based sensors allow **naked-eye** readouts without sophisticated instrumentation.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation I – Brus Equation from Particle-in-a-Sphere Model

**Goal:** Show that the bandgap of a quantum dot scales approximately as $1/r^2$.

**Step 1 — Start with the bulk exciton energy.**
In bulk, the bandgap is $E_{gap}^{bulk}$ and the electron–hole pair (exciton) is bound by Coulomb energy $E_{Coulomb} = \frac{1.786 e^2}{4\pi\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r a_B}$.

**Step 2 — Apply strong confinement assumption.**
When $r \ll a_B$, the exciton binding energy becomes negligible compared with the kinetic (confinement) energy of the two carriers treated as **independent particles** in a 3-D spherical well of radius r.

**Step 3 — Kinetic energy of a particle in a 3-D box.**
For a particle of mass $m^*$ confined in a 1-D box of length L = 2r, the lowest quantized energy is $E_1 = \frac{h^2}{8 m^* L^2} = \frac{h^2}{32 m^* r^2}$.

In 3-D, the total confinement energy of an electron plus a hole is the **sum** of their individual confinement energies:

$$E_{conf} = \frac{h^2}{8 (2r)^2}\left(\frac{1}{m_e^*} + \frac{1}{m_h^*}\right) = \frac{h^2}{32 r^2}\left(\frac{1}{m_e^*} + \frac{1}{m_h^*}\right)$$

Wait — re-checking carefully. The standard result uses a sphere of radius r; the lowest 1s-orbital in a sphere of radius r gives:

$$E_{1s} = \frac{\hbar^2 \pi^2}{2 m^* r^2} = \frac{h^2}{8 m^* r^2}$$

(for the simplest 1-D infinite-well analog, L = 2r gives the same factor.) Summing electron and hole:

$$E_{conf} = \frac{h^2}{8 r^2}\left(\frac{1}{m_e^*} + \frac{1}{m_h^*}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Include the Coulomb term.**
Even under strong confinement, a residual Coulomb interaction remains, treated as a perturbation:

$$E_{Coulomb} \approx \frac{1.786\, e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r r}$$

**Step 5 — Assemble the total bandgap.**

$$\boxed{E_{gap}(r) = E_{gap}^{bulk} + \frac{h^2}{8 r^2}\left(\frac{1}{m_e^*} + \frac{1}{m_h^*}\right) - \frac{1.786\, e^2}{4 \pi \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r r}}$$

**Step 6 — Verify the limiting case.**
As $r \to \infty$: $E_{conf} \to 0$, $E_{Coulomb} \to 0$, so $E_{gap}(r) \to E_{gap}^{bulk}$. ✔ (Consistent with bulk limit.)

As $r$ decreases: the $1/r^2$ confinement term dominates over the $1/r$ Coulomb term, so the bandgap **increases**. ✔ (Consistent with blue-shift in small QDs.)

### 3.2 Derivation II – LSPR Resonance from Quasi-Static Polarizability

**Goal:** Derive the LSPR condition $\varepsilon_1(\omega) = -2\varepsilon_m$.

**Step 1 — Place a small metal sphere of radius r in a static electric field $E_0$.**
The polarizability $\alpha$ of a sphere of dielectric function $\varepsilon(\omega)$ in a medium of dielectric constant $\varepsilon_m$ is:

$$\alpha = 4 \pi \varepsilon_0 r^3 \cdot \frac{\varepsilon(\omega) - \varepsilon_m}{\varepsilon(\omega) + 2 \varepsilon_m}$$

**Step 2 — Resonant enhancement condition.**
The polarizability becomes **maximal** when the denominator $\varepsilon(\omega) + 2\varepsilon_m$ approaches zero. For a complex $\varepsilon(\omega) = \varepsilon_1 + i\varepsilon_2$, this means:

$$\varepsilon_1(\omega) = -2\varepsilon_m \quad \text{and} \quad \varepsilon_2(\omega) \text{ is small}$$

**Step 3 — Conclude the resonance condition.**

$$\boxed{\varepsilon_1(\omega_{LSPR}) = -2 \varepsilon_m}$$

**Step 4 — Engineering implication.**
Since $\varepsilon_m$ appears in the condition, any change in the **refractive index** of the surrounding medium (e.g., due to biomolecule binding) **shifts the LSPR peak**. This is the **transduction principle** of LSPR biosensors.

### 3.3 Derivation III – Refractive-Index Sensitivity of LSPR

**Goal:** Quantify the peak-shift per refractive-index unit (nm/RIU).

For a Drude metal where $\varepsilon_1(\omega) = 1 - \frac{\omega_p^2}{\omega^2}$ with $\omega_p$ = bulk plasma frequency, the LSPR condition gives:

$$\omega_{LSPR} = \frac{\omega_p}{\sqrt{1 + 2\varepsilon_m}}$$

Differentiating with respect to $\varepsilon_m$:

$$\frac{d\omega_{LSPR}}{d\varepsilon_m} = -\frac{\omega_p}{2 (1+2\varepsilon_m)^{3/2}}$$

Converting to wavelength sensitivity:

$$\boxed{S_{\lambda} = \frac{d\lambda_{LSPR}}{d n_m} = \frac{\lambda_{LSPR}^2}{\omega_p \sqrt{1+2 n_m^2}} \cdot \text{(geometric factor)}}$$

In practice, **S ≈ 40–500 nm/RIU** is typical for Au/Ag nanospheres, while nanostars/ nanorods achieve **>700 nm/RIU** due to tip-enhanced fields.

### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix

| Property | Quantum Dots | Metal Nanoparticles |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Material class | Semiconductor (CdSe, ZnS, …) | Noble metal (Au, Ag, Pt) |
| Origin of property | Quantum confinement | Localized surface plasmon |
| Typical size | 2–10 nm | 5–100 nm |
| Optical signal | **Photoluminescence** (emission) | **Absorption / scattering** (LSPR) |
| Tunable parameter | **Size** → emission color | **Size, shape, composition** → peak λ |
| Detection mode | Fluorescence, FRET, PET | Colorimetric, LSPR shift, SERS |
| Strength | High QY, multiplexing, photostability | Naked-eye readout, simple assay |
| Limitation | Toxicity (Cd), blinking | Salt/aggregation sensitivity |
| Refractive-index sensitivity (S) | Low (typically indirect) | 40–700 nm/RIU |
| Typical figure-of-merit (FOM) | QY up to 80–90 % | FOM ≈ 1–10 for AuNPs |
